Tomato.overview: Tomato, commonly known as “Takkali” in Sri Lanka, is a widely cultivated vegetable crop belonging to the Solanaceae family and scientifically known as Solanum lycopersicum. It is grown across Sri Lanka in both low-country and up-country regions, with higher productivity in intermediate and cooler areas where temperatures are more suitable for fruit setting. Tomato grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 27°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and good sunlight exposure for healthy plant growth and proper flowering and fruit development. Tomatoes are widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine for curries, sambols, sauces, and salads and are an important source of vitamins A and C, antioxidants, and minerals. The crop is usually ready for harvest within 60 to 80 days after transplanting depending on the variety and growing conditions, with multiple harvests over a fruiting period. Important factors affecting tomato cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include whiteflies, aphids, fruit borers, thrips, bacterial wilt, early blight, and tomato leaf curl virus, which can significantly reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Deep plough to 30 cm
activities.description: Break clods, remove weeds and debris. Allow to weather for 5–7 days.
activities.why: Good tillage improves aeration and root penetration.

activities.title: Form raised beds
activities.description: Make 1 m wide beds, 20–25 cm high with 30 cm drainage channels.
activities.why: Raised beds prevent waterlogging — the main cause of wilt diseases.

activities.title: Apply basal compost
activities.description: Incorporate 10–15 t/ha well-decomposed compost into the bed.
activities.why: Organic matter improves soil structure and nutrient availability.

activities.title: Transplant 21-day seedlings
activities.description: Plant in holes 20 cm deep in late afternoon or cloudy day. Water immediately.
activities.why: Evening planting reduces heat stress on young seedlings.

activities.title: Water daily for first week
activities.description: Water gently at the base every morning for 7 days.
activities.why: Consistent moisture in first week is critical for root establishment.

activities.title: Install stakes
activities.description: Drive 1.5 m stakes 20 cm into soil, 10 cm from each plant.
activities.why: Staking prevents lodging and improves air circulation.

stages.name: Vegetative Growth
stages.description: Rapid leaf and stem growth phase.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Remove all weeds by hand or shallow hoe. Avoid root disturbance.
activities.why: Weeds reduce nutrients by 30–40% at this stage.

activities.title: Top dressing 1 — Urea
activities.description: Apply Urea 75 kg/ha in a ring 10 cm from base, cover with soil, water.
activities.why: Nitrogen drives rapid vegetative growth.

activities.title: Scout for pests and diseases
activities.description: Check leaf undersides for whitefly and aphids. Cut a stem base and place in water to test for bacterial wilt ooze.
activities.why: Early detection saves the crop.

stages.name: Flowering & Fruit Set
stages.description: Most critical stage — water and feeding directly affect yield.

activities.title: Top dressing 2 — Urea + MOP
activities.description: Apply Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha in ring around plant. Water immediately.
activities.why: Potassium improves fruit quality and prevents cracking.

activities.title: Maintain consistent moisture
activities.description: Water every 2 days. Never let soil dry completely.
activities.why: Irregular watering causes blossom drop and blossom-end rot.

activities.title: Check for fruit borer
activities.description: Look for entry holes and frass on fruits. Remove and destroy affected fruits.
activities.why: One larva can damage 5–10 fruits if unchecked.

activities.title: Begin first harvest
activities.description: Pick at 50–75% colour change (green to orange/red). Leave short stalk.
activities.why: Correct harvest stage gives best shelf life.

activities.title: Harvest every 2–3 days
activities.description: Pick all mature fruits. Leaving overripe fruits reduces further yield.
activities.why: Regular picking stimulates new fruit development.

fertilization.why: Phosphorus for roots; potassium for early plant strength.
Potassium at flowering improves fruit size and taste.

irrigation.notes: Reduce 10–14 days before final harvest to improve shelf life.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden whole-plant wilt; milky bacterial ooze from cut stem in water.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High soil moisture, 25–35°C, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Raised beds. Crop rotation (no Solanaceae for 3 years). Certified seedlings.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove and destroy plant. Lime affected soil.

diseases.name: Early Blight
diseases.symptoms: Dark brown concentric ring spots on lower leaves; leaves yellow and drop.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humidity >80%, 24–29°C, wet leaf surfaces.
diseases.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation. Good plant spacing.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) every 7–10 days.

diseases.name: Tomato Leaf Curl Virus
diseases.symptoms: Leaf curling upward, yellowing, stunted growth, poor fruit set.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High whitefly populations, hot dry weather.
diseases.prevention: Control whitefly with yellow sticky traps. Remove infected plants immediately.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Control whitefly vector with Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L).

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap; transmits Leaf Curl Virus causing major yield loss.
pests.identification: Tiny white insects on leaf undersides; leaves yellow and curl.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Marigold intercrop. Reflective mulch.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) on leaf undersides. Spray in evening.

pests.name: Fruit Borer
pests.damage: Larvae bore into fruits; one larva can damage 5–10 fruits.
pests.identification: Entry hole with frass (excrement) on fruit surface.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Remove and destroy infected fruits immediately.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L). No spray within 7 days of harvest.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks plant sap causing leaf curling, yellowing, and stunted growth.
pests.identification: Small soft-bodied insects clustered on new shoots and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray. Encourage natural predators.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) or Imidacloprid. Avoid over-application.

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Damages leaves and flowers; reduces fruit formation and surface quality.
pests.identification: Tiny yellowish insects; silvery streaks on leaves and flower petals.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Neem extract spray at early stages.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Fipronil. Spray early morning.

risks.name: Drought at Flowering
risks.description: Water deficit causes flower drop and 30–50% yield loss.
risks.signs: Morning wilting, flower drop, leaf edge browning.
risks.mitigation: Irrigate every 2 days. Mulch to retain moisture.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Standing water kills roots in 24–48 hours. Promotes wilt and rot.
risks.signs: Wilting despite wet soil, yellowing lower leaves.
risks.mitigation: Plant on raised beds. Clear drainage channels.

harvest.method: Twist-and-pull with short stalk. Use clean plastic crates.
harvest.post_harvest: Grade by size. Store cool and shaded. Market within 5–7 days.

Tomato.growth_stages_text: Tomato cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, nursery development, vegetative growth, flowering, fruit set, and harvesting. Seeds usually germinate within 6–10 days, after which seedlings are raised in a nursery for about 3–4 weeks before transplanting. Vegetative growth continues for 25–40 days after transplanting, followed by flowering around 40–55 days. Fruit set occurs shortly after flowering, and harvesting typically begins within 60–80 days after transplanting, continuing over several weeks as fruits mature at different stages. Farmers commonly use transplanting methods and maintain spacing of approximately 45–60 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure proper airflow, sunlight penetration, and reduced disease pressure.
Tomato.management_text: Successful tomato cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including nursery preparation, land preparation, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, staking, pruning, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is essential during vegetative growth, flowering, and fruiting stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to support plant vigor and improve fruit size, color, and yield. Irrigation frequency is highest during flowering and fruiting stages due to high water demand, while moderate irrigation is maintained during vegetative growth to avoid excessive foliage development. Staking and pruning are commonly practiced to improve air circulation, reduce disease incidence, and support fruit-bearing branches. Harvesting indicators include fully colored fruits (green, red, or yellow depending on variety), firm texture, and proper size before overripening.
Tomato.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are critical in tomato cultivation. Whiteflies and aphids suck plant sap, causing curling, yellowing, and weakening of plants, while thrips damage leaves and flowers, reducing fruit formation. Fruit borers damage developing fruits internally, leading to significant yield loss. Bacterial wilt causes sudden wilting and plant death due to vascular blockage, while early blight appears as dark concentric spots on leaves, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor. Tomato leaf curl virus causes severe leaf curling, stunted growth, and major yield reduction. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, field sanitation, removal of infected plants, use of resistant varieties, insect traps, biological control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, staking, balanced fertilizer use, and integrated pest management strategies help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Chilli.overview: Chilli, commonly known as “Miris” in Sri Lanka, is an important spice crop belonging to the Solanaceae family and scientifically known as Capsicum annuum (hot pepper varieties). It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones such as Anuradhapura, Polonnaruwa, and Hambantota where warm temperatures and low rainfall conditions are suitable for fruit development. Chilli grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and requires well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy plant growth and high fruit yield. Chilli is a key ingredient in Sri Lankan cuisine and is widely used as a spice in fresh, dried, and powdered forms, valued for its pungency and vitamin content. The crop generally takes about 70 to 100 days after transplanting to start harvesting depending on the variety and cultivation practices. Important factors affecting chilli cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include thrips, aphids, whiteflies, fruit borers, bacterial wilt, anthracnose, and viral leaf curl diseases, which can significantly reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough and level
activities.description: Deep plough to 30 cm. Remove weeds. Form raised beds.
activities.why: Good drainage prevents Phytophthora collar rot, common in chilli.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate compost 10 t/ha.
activities.why: Organic matter improves moisture retention in dry-zone soils.

activities.title: Transplant in evening
activities.description: Use healthy 25–30 day seedlings. Plant at 45 × 60 cm spacing. Water immediately.
activities.why: Evening planting reduces transplant shock.

activities.title: Daily watering for 10 days
activities.description: Water gently at base. Avoid wetting foliage.
activities.why: Consistent moisture prevents early wilting.

activities.title: Weed and hill up
activities.description: Remove weeds and heap soil around base of plant.
activities.why: Hilling strengthens stem base and prevents root exposure.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Apply Urea 75 kg/ha as ring around plants. Cover and water.
activities.why: Nitrogen supports branching for higher fruit-bearing capacity.

activities.title: Scout for thrips
activities.description: Check young leaves for silvering and distortion — signs of thrips. Check for viral symptoms (mosaic, crinkle).
activities.why: Thrips are the main vector of chilli viral diseases.

stages.name: Flowering & Fruiting
stages.description: Flowers and fruits develop. Maintain nutrition.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Apply Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Potassium improves fruit pungency and weight.

activities.title: Check for anthracnose
activities.description: Look for sunken dark lesions on fruits. Remove and destroy.
activities.why: Anthracnose spreads rapidly in humid conditions.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest at correct stage for intended use.

activities.title: Begin harvest (green or red)
activities.description: Pick green chillies for fresh market. Allow to turn red for dried/powder market.
activities.why: Red chillies have higher capsaicin and dry weight.

activities.title: Harvest every 5–7 days
activities.description: Pick mature fruits regularly to encourage continuous production.
activities.why: Regular harvesting extends productive period by 4–6 weeks.

irrigation.notes: Chilli tolerates short dry spells better than waterlogging. Reduce watering before red harvest.

diseases.name: Anthracnose
diseases.symptoms: Dark sunken circular lesions on green and red fruits; fruits rot and shrivel.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, 25–30°C, wounded fruits, high rainfall.
diseases.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation. Good spacing. Remove infected fruits immediately.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L) every 7 days.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden complete wilting; vascular browning visible in cut stem; milky ooze in water.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Wet soil, poor drainage, 25–35°C.
diseases.prevention: Raised beds. Crop rotation. Certified disease-free seedlings.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants immediately. Lime affected soil area.

diseases.name: Leaf Curl Virus
diseases.symptoms: Severe leaf curling, yellowing, and distortion; stunted growth; drastically reduced fruit yield.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High thrips and whitefly populations; hot dry weather.
diseases.prevention: Control vector insects with sticky traps. Remove infected plants immediately.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Manage thrips: Spinosad (0.3 ml/L). Manage whitefly: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L).

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Sucks sap from young leaves and flowers; causes silvering, curling, and distortion; primary vector of leaf curl virus.
pests.identification: Tiny yellowish insects in flower buds and leaf folds; silver streaks on leaves.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Reflective mulch. Avoid over-fertilisation with nitrogen.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Fipronil. Apply every 7 days during high infestation.

pests.name: Fruit Borer
pests.damage: Larvae bore into fruits causing internal damage and market rejection.
pests.identification: Entry holes with frass on fruit surface; larvae visible inside when cut.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Remove and destroy infested fruits immediately.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) at fruiting stage.

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap; transmits leaf curl virus causing major yield loss.
pests.identification: Clouds of tiny white insects on leaf undersides when disturbed.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) on leaf undersides.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes leaf curling and stunted shoot growth; transmits viruses.
pests.identification: Dense colonies on new shoot tips and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) if infestation exceeds threshold.

risks.name: Drought
risks.description: Dry spells during flowering cause flower and fruit drop.
risks.mitigation: Drip or furrow irrigation every 3–4 days. Mulch.

risks.name: Excessive Rain
risks.description: Promotes anthracnose and Phytophthora root rot.
risks.mitigation: Raised beds. Copper spray before rain period.

indicators: Red stage: fully red, slightly firm
harvest.method: Hand pick with short stalk. Avoid bruising.
harvest.post_harvest: Dry in sun for red/dried market. Grade by size and colour. Store in dry ventilated area.

Chilli.growth_stages_text: Chilli cultivation follows several important growth stages including seed germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, flowering, fruit setting, fruit development, ripening, and harvesting. Seeds are usually sown in nursery beds and germinate within 7–14 days under favorable conditions. Seedlings are maintained in the nursery for approximately 30–45 days before transplanting into the main field. After transplanting, plants undergo rapid vegetative growth with the development of branches and leaves. Flowering generally begins around 35–50 days after transplanting, followed by fruit setting and fruit development. Fruits gradually enlarge and change color from green to red or other mature colors depending on the variety. Harvesting can begin approximately 70–100 days after transplanting and may continue for several months through multiple pickings.
Chilli.management_text: Successful chilli cultivation requires a well-organized crop management schedule including land preparation, nursery establishment, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting. Farmers generally transplant seedlings at a spacing of about 45–60 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to provide adequate sunlight and airflow. Fertilizer management is essential for achieving high yields. Nitrogen promotes vegetative growth, phosphorus supports root development and flowering, while potassium improves fruit size, color, and quality. Organic manure or compost is commonly incorporated during land preparation to improve soil fertility and moisture retention.
Chilli.disease_management_text: Irrigation management plays a critical role in chilli production. Frequent light irrigation is required immediately after transplanting to ensure successful establishment. During vegetative growth and flowering, soil moisture should be maintained consistently to support healthy plant development. Water stress during flowering and fruit setting can result in flower drop and reduced fruit production. However, excessive irrigation should be avoided because waterlogged conditions encourage root diseases and reduce plant vigor. Drip irrigation systems are often recommended because they improve water-use efficiency and reduce disease incidence.
Chilli.risk_mitigation_text: Disease and pest management are among the most important aspects of chilli cultivation. Thrips attack young leaves and flowers, causing curling, silvering, and distorted growth. Aphids and whiteflies feed on plant sap and act as vectors for viral diseases, particularly leaf curl virus. Fruit borers damage developing fruits by boring holes and feeding internally, leading to direct yield losses. Bacterial wilt causes sudden wilting and plant death, especially under warm and moist conditions. Anthracnose disease produces dark, sunken lesions on fruits, making them unsuitable for market. Viral leaf curl disease causes severe leaf curling, stunted growth, and reduced fruit production. Regular monitoring, field sanitation, removal of infected plants, use of resistant varieties, balanced fertilization, and integrated pest management practices are effective strategies for controlling these problems. Risk mitigation strategies are important for maintaining stable chilli production and minimizing losses. During periods of heavy rainfall, proper drainage systems should be maintained to prevent waterlogging and disease outbreaks. During drought conditions, mulching and efficient irrigation practices help conserve soil moisture and reduce plant stress. Farmers should regularly inspect fields for early signs of pest and disease infestations to enable timely intervention. Crop rotation, use of certified healthy seedlings, proper plant spacing, and balanced nutrient management improve crop health and reduce vulnerability to environmental stresses. These practices help ensure consistent yields, high-quality fruits, and greater resilience to changing climatic conditions.

Brinjal (Eggplant).overview: Brinjal, commonly known as eggplant or “Wambatu” in Sri Lanka, is a widely cultivated vegetable crop belonging to the Solanaceae family and scientifically known as Solanum melongena. It is grown throughout Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones as well as home gardens, due to its adaptability to warm tropical climates and high market demand. Brinjal grows best in temperatures between 22°C and 30°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy or sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy plant growth and continuous fruit production. Brinjal is an important vegetable in Sri Lankan cuisine and is widely used in curries and fried dishes, valued for its nutritional content including fiber, vitamins, and antioxidants. The crop can be harvested within 60 to 90 days after transplanting depending on the variety and growing conditions, with repeated harvesting over a long period. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include fruit and shoot borers, aphids, whiteflies, bacterial wilt, and fungal leaf spot diseases, which can significantly reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly controlled.

activities.title: Plough deeply
activities.description: Plough to 35 cm. Brinjal has a deep root system.
activities.why: Deep tillage supports the large root system needed for a long-duration crop.

activities.title: Add compost and lime if needed
activities.description: Incorporate 15 t/ha compost. Apply lime to raise pH if soil is below 5.5.
activities.why: Brinjal is sensitive to acidic soils.

activities.title: Transplant 25-day seedlings
activities.description: Plant in late afternoon. Water immediately. Shade newly transplanted seedlings for 2–3 days if hot.
activities.why: Shading reduces stress; improves survival rate.

activities.title: Water daily for 2 weeks
activities.description: Water at base every morning.
activities.why: Critical for root establishment.

stages.name: Vegetative Growth
stages.description: Build strong frame for long productive life.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha as ring around plant.
activities.why: Nitrogen builds the vegetative frame that supports months of fruiting.

activities.title: Inspect for fruit and shoot borer
activities.description: Look for wilted/dead shoot tips and bored fruits — key symptom of brinjal FSB.
activities.why: FSB is the most destructive pest of brinjal in Sri Lanka.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha.
activities.why: Higher potassium supports continuous fruiting.

activities.title: Regular FSB scouting
activities.description: Check daily for wilted shoot tips. Cut and remove infested shoots.
activities.why: Destroying infested shoots kills larvae before they reach fruits.

activities.title: Begin first harvest
activities.description: Pick fruits when full sized, shiny, and firm. Do not allow to over-ripen.
activities.why: Over-ripe fruits reduce plant vigour and attract pests.

activities.title: Harvest every 3–4 days
activities.description: Regular picking extends crop life.
activities.why: Continuous harvesting keeps plant productive for 4–6 months.

irrigation.notes: Brinjal is moderately drought tolerant but yields drop significantly without regular irrigation.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden wilting and plant death; vascular browning; milky ooze from cut stem in water.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Wet soil, 25–35°C, poor drainage, previously infected soil.
diseases.prevention: Crop rotation. Raised beds. Certified transplants.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants. Lime affected area. Improve drainage.

diseases.name: Fungal Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Dark lesions with lighter centres on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and plant vigour.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, overcrowding, wet foliage.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L).

pests.name: Fruit and Shoot Borer
pests.damage: Major pest — bores into growing shoot tips causing 'dead hearts'; bores into fruits making them unmarketable.
pests.identification: Wilted/dead shoot tips; circular entry holes on fruits with frass.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Remove and destroy infested shoots and fruits daily. Use clean nursery seedlings.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Emamectin benzoate (0.4 g/L). Spray at 7–10 day intervals.

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes yellowing, leaf drop, and possible virus transmission.
pests.identification: White clouds when plants disturbed; sticky honeydew on leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) on leaf undersides.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes curling and stunted growth of young shoots.
pests.identification: Dense colonies on growing tips and undersides of leaves.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Encourage natural enemies.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L).

risks.name: Fruit & Shoot Borer Outbreak
risks.description: Main threat to brinjal. Can destroy entire crop if unchecked.
risks.signs: >25% shoot tips wilted, many fruits with holes.
risks.mitigation: Daily monitoring. Pheromone traps. Weekly Spinosad spray.

risks.name: Drought
risks.description: Reduces fruit size and increases bitterness.
risks.mitigation: Regular drip irrigation. Mulch.

indicators: Full sized fruit with glossy shiny skin
harvest.method: Cut fruit with 2 cm stalk using sharp knife or scissors.
harvest.post_harvest: Handle carefully to avoid spine injury. Store in cool shaded area. Market within 3–5 days.

Brinjal (Eggplant).growth_stages_text: Brinjal cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, nursery development, vegetative growth, flowering, fruit formation, and harvesting. Seeds usually germinate within 6–10 days, after which seedlings are raised in a nursery for about 3–4 weeks before transplanting. Vegetative growth continues for 25–40 days after transplanting, followed by flowering around 45–60 days. Fruit formation begins shortly after flowering, and harvesting can start within 60–90 days after transplanting, continuing for several weeks due to the plant’s ability to produce multiple harvests. Farmers commonly use transplanting methods and maintain spacing of approximately 60–75 cm between plants and 75–90 cm between rows to ensure proper airflow, sunlight exposure, and strong plant development.
Brinjal (Eggplant).management_text: Successful brinjal cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including nursery preparation, land preparation, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, pruning, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is crucial during vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to support strong plant development and improve fruit yield and quality. Irrigation frequency is highest during flowering and fruiting stages due to high water demand, while moderate irrigation is maintained during vegetative growth to avoid excessive leaf development. Harvesting indicators include glossy, firm fruits with uniform color and tender texture before seeds become hard and mature.
Brinjal (Eggplant).disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in brinjal cultivation. Fruit and shoot borers cause internal damage by boring into stems and fruits, leading to wilting and reduced yield. Aphids and whiteflies suck plant sap, resulting in curling, yellowing, and stunted growth. Bacterial wilt causes sudden plant collapse due to vascular infection, while fungal leaf spot diseases create dark lesions on leaves that reduce photosynthesis and plant vigor. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, field sanitation, removal of infected plant parts, use of resistant varieties, pheromone traps for borers, and biological or chemical control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, balanced fertilizer use, and integrated pest management strategies help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Capsicum.overview: Capsicum, commonly known as “Maalu Miris” or sweet pepper in Sri Lanka, is a high-value vegetable crop belonging to the Solanaceae family and scientifically known as Capsicum annuum. It is mainly cultivated in cooler and intermediate regions such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where temperatures are suitable for proper fruit setting and color development. Capsicum grows best in temperatures between 18°C and 28°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy soil rich in organic matter with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and good sunlight exposure for healthy plant growth and high-quality fruit production. Capsicum is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine for curries, salads, and stir-fry dishes and is valued for its rich vitamin C content and nutritional benefits. The crop generally takes about 70 to 90 days after transplanting to start harvesting, depending on the variety and growing conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, thrips, whiteflies, fruit borers, bacterial wilt, and viral infections, which can reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough and form raised beds
activities.description: Plough 30 cm deep, form 1 m beds.
activities.why: Good drainage critical to prevent Phytophthora.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 15 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Capsicum is a heavy feeder. Rich organic base is essential.

activities.title: Transplant 25–30 day seedlings
activities.description: Plant in late afternoon. Shade for 3 days.
activities.why: Capsicum seedlings are sensitive to transplant shock.

activities.title: Water daily first 2 weeks
activities.description: Water gently at base. Never on foliage.
activities.why: Prevents damping off of young seedlings.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha as ring.
activities.why: Nitrogen for branching structure.

activities.title: Check for thrips and viral symptoms
activities.description: Look for silvery streaks and leaf distortion.
activities.why: Thrips spread Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus — the most damaging disease of capsicum.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha + Calcium Nitrate 25 kg/ha.
activities.why: Calcium prevents blossom-end rot; potassium improves colour.

activities.title: Watch for anthracnose
activities.description: Inspect fruits for sunken lesions. Remove immediately.
activities.why: Anthracnose can destroy the entire fruit crop quickly in humid weather.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest at green or fully coloured stage.

activities.title: Begin green harvest
activities.description: Harvest when full sized and firm (green stage) for early market.
activities.why: Green capsicum commands earlier market prices.

activities.title: Red/Yellow harvest
activities.description: Allow to fully colour for premium market. Pick before softening.
activities.why: Coloured capsicum fetches 2–3× higher price than green.

irrigation.notes: Very sensitive to water stress. Consistent moisture is essential for uniform fruit sizing.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden wilting and plant death; vascular browning visible in stem cross-section.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Wet, warm soil conditions, poor drainage, previously infected plots.
diseases.prevention: Crop rotation. Raised beds. Avoid Solanaceae family for 3+ years.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove and destroy infected plants. Lime affected soil.

diseases.name: Viral Mosaic
diseases.symptoms: Mosaic patterns, leaf distortion, stunted growth, poor fruit set.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High aphid/thrips populations; hot dry weather.
diseases.prevention: Control vectors. Use virus-free transplants. Remove infected plants.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Manage vectors: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) for aphids; Spinosad for thrips.

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Damages flowers and young fruits; causes silvering, distortion, and reduced fruit set.
pests.identification: Tiny yellowish insects in flowers and leaf folds; silvery scarring.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Reflective mulch. Remove infested flowers.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Fipronil. Apply early morning.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; major vector of viral mosaic diseases.
pests.identification: Dense colonies on new shoots and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray. Reflective mulch.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L).

pests.name: Fruit Borer
pests.damage: Larvae bore into fruits reducing market quality.
pests.identification: Entry holes with frass on fruit surface.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Remove infested fruits immediately.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) at first fruit set.

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes yellowing and may transmit viruses.
pests.identification: White clouds when plants disturbed; sticky honeydew on leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L).

risks.name: Low Temperature (<15°C)
risks.description: Poor fruit set and colour development.
risks.mitigation: Use polytunnel or frost cover in up-country during cold nights.

risks.name: Drought
risks.description: Blossom drop and fruit cracking.
risks.mitigation: Drip irrigation. Mulch heavily.

indicators: Green → yellow/red colour change for coloured stage
harvest.method: Cut with sharp knife leaving 2 cm stalk.
harvest.post_harvest: Grade by size and colour. Pack carefully in ventilated boxes. Refrigerate at 8–12°C if possible.

Capsicum.growth_stages_text: Capsicum cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, nursery development, vegetative growth, flowering, fruit setting, and harvesting. Seeds usually germinate within 7–14 days, after which seedlings are raised in a nursery for about 3–5 weeks before transplanting. Vegetative growth continues for 25–40 days after transplanting, followed by flowering around 45–60 days. Fruit set occurs shortly after flowering, and harvesting typically begins within 70–90 days after transplanting when fruits reach proper size and color development. Farmers commonly use transplanting methods and maintain spacing of approximately 45–60 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure adequate sunlight penetration, airflow, and healthy fruit development.
Capsicum.management_text: Successful capsicum cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including nursery preparation, land preparation, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, staking, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to support strong plant growth and improve fruit size, color, and quality. Irrigation frequency is highest during flowering and fruit setting stages due to high water demand, while moderate irrigation is maintained during vegetative growth to prevent excessive leaf growth. Harvesting indicators include firm fruits with bright color (green, red, or yellow depending on variety) and proper size before over-ripening occurs.
Capsicum.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in capsicum cultivation. Aphids, thrips, and whiteflies suck plant sap, causing leaf curling, yellowing, and reduced plant vigor. Fruit borers damage developing fruits internally, leading to reduced market value. Bacterial wilt causes sudden wilting and plant death due to vascular infection, while viral diseases may cause mosaic patterns, leaf distortion, and stunted growth. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, field sanitation, removal of infected plants, use of resistant varieties, insect traps, biological control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, balanced fertilizer use, staking support systems, and integrated pest management techniques help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Potato.overview: Potato, commonly known as “Ala” in Sri Lanka, is a cool-climate tuber crop belonging to the Solanaceae family and scientifically known as Solanum tuberosum. It is mainly cultivated in the up-country regions of Sri Lanka such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where lower temperatures are favorable for tuber formation and growth. Potato grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 20°C and requires deep, fertile, well-drained sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.0 to 6.5. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy plant growth and high tuber yield. Potatoes are one of the most important food crops in Sri Lanka and are widely used in curries, fried dishes, and various food preparations due to their high carbohydrate content and nutritional value. The crop is generally harvested within 90 to 120 days after planting depending on the variety and climatic conditions. Important factors affecting potato cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include potato tuber moth, aphids, late blight, early blight, bacterial wilt, and fungal tuber rot, which can significantly reduce yield and tuber quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Deep plough to 40 cm
activities.description: Break all clods. Potato tubers need loose soil to develop freely.
activities.why: Compacted soil causes deformed small tubers.

activities.title: Apply compost and lime
activities.description: Compost 15 t/ha + agricultural lime to bring pH to 5.5–6.0.
activities.why: Potato scab is worse in alkaline soils above pH 6.5.

stages.name: Planting & Emergence
stages.description: Plant seed tubers and encourage emergence.

activities.title: Plant seed tubers
activities.description: Place 60–80 g seed tubers in furrows 10 cm deep. Cover with soil. Spray with Mancozeb before planting to prevent seed tuber rot.
activities.why: Healthy seed tubers are the foundation of a good crop.

activities.title: Irrigate if no rain
activities.description: Maintain soil moisture but avoid waterlogging.
activities.why: Consistent moisture ensures uniform sprouting.

activities.title: Check emergence
activities.description: 80% emergence should occur by day 14–18.
activities.why: Poor emergence may indicate seed tuber rot — check a few plants.

stages.name: Vegetative Growth
stages.description: Build foliage for photosynthesis and tuber fill.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 100 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: High nitrogen demand during rapid foliage growth.

activities.title: Earthing up (hilling)
activities.description: Heap soil 15–20 cm high around plant base.
activities.why: Earthing up prevents green tubers (toxic solanine) and supports plant.

activities.title: Begin late blight monitoring
activities.description: Check for water-soaked lesions on leaves — especially after rain. Spray preventively.
activities.why: Late blight can destroy a crop within 5–7 days in cool wet weather.

stages.name: Tuber Development
stages.description: Critical phase — nutrition and disease control determine yield.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: MOP 100 kg/ha + Urea 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Potassium is critical for tuber starch content and yield.

activities.title: Blight spray schedule
activities.description: Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) every 7 days. Do not miss a spray in wet weather.
activities.why: Late blight spreads exponentially. One missed spray can cost the crop.

activities.title: Remove haulm (tops)
activities.description: Cut or pull off foliage 2 weeks before harvest.
activities.why: Haulm removal hardens tuber skin and reduces tuber disease at harvest.

activities.title: Dig and harvest
activities.description: Use fork or digger. Avoid tuber damage. Sort and grade immediately.
activities.why: Wounded tubers rot quickly in storage.

fertilization.why: High P for tuber initiation; K for yield.

irrigation.notes: Reduce watering 2 weeks before harvest to harden tuber skin.

diseases.name: Late Blight
diseases.symptoms: Dark water-soaked lesions on leaves spreading rapidly; white fluffy growth on leaf underside in humid conditions; plants collapse.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Cool temperatures (10–20°C), high humidity (>90%), wet weather, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation. Use blight-resistant varieties. Destroy infected debris.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) preventively every 7 days during high-risk periods. Chlorothalonil (2 g/L) as alternative.

diseases.name: Early Blight
diseases.symptoms: Brown concentric ring spots on lower leaves; premature defoliation; reduced tuber size.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Moderate temperatures (24–29°C), humidity fluctuations, nutrient stress.
diseases.prevention: Balanced fertilisation. Good spacing. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Iprodione (1.5 ml/L) every 10 days.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden wilting; vascular browning; tubers show vascular ring discolouration.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Warm moist soil, infected seed tubers, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed tubers. Crop rotation. Raised ridges.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants. Do not plant in affected areas for 3+ years.

diseases.name: Fungal Tuber Rot
diseases.symptoms: Internal decay and discolouration of tubers; soft rot starting from surface wounds.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged soil, wounds during earthing-up or harvest, high moisture.
diseases.prevention: Careful handling. Avoid waterlogging. Well-drained ridges.
diseases.treatment: Thiram or Copper fungicide drench at early detection.

pests.name: Potato Tuber Moth
pests.damage: Larvae mine leaves and bore into tubers; stored tubers heavily damaged.
pests.identification: Serpentine mines in leaves; larvae and tunnels visible inside cut tubers.
pests.prevention: Hill up soil over tubers. Harvest on time. Store in dark cool conditions.
pests.treatment: Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) spray (2 g/L) in field. Pheromone traps for monitoring.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; major vector of potato virus Y (PVY) and potato leaf roll virus.
pests.identification: Colonies on young shoots; virus symptoms appear after aphid feeding.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective silver mulch. Remove virus-infected plants.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam. Spray fortnightly.

risks.name: Late Blight Epidemic
risks.description: Can destroy 100% of crop in 1–2 weeks during cool wet weather.
risks.signs: Rapid brown leaf collapse, dark water-soaked lesions.
risks.mitigation: Never miss fungicide schedule. Remove first infected plants to slow spread.

risks.name: Frost (below 2°C)
risks.description: Kills foliage rapidly. Common in Nuwara Eliya November–January.
risks.signs: Black wilted foliage overnight. Ice crystals visible on leaves.
risks.mitigation: Cover with gunny sacks or polytunnel at night during risk period.

indicators: Foliage turns yellow and dies back naturally
Tuber skin does not peel when rubbed
harvest.method: Dig with fork carefully. Avoid cutting tubers. Grade into A, B, C sizes.
harvest.post_harvest: Cure in shade for 7–10 days. Store in cool dark ventilated store. Do not expose to sunlight — causes greening and toxicity.

Potato.growth_stages_text: Potato cultivation follows several important growth stages including sprouting, vegetative growth, tuber initiation, tuber bulking, and harvesting. The crop is usually propagated using seed tubers rather than true botanical seeds, and sprouting occurs within 2–3 weeks after planting. Vegetative growth continues for about 30–50 days, during which strong foliage develops to support photosynthesis. Tuber initiation begins around 35–50 days after planting, followed by tuber bulking, where underground tubers expand rapidly and accumulate starch. Harvesting is typically carried out between 90–120 days when plants start yellowing and drying, indicating that tubers have reached maturity. Farmers commonly plant seed tubers at a depth of about 5–10 cm and maintain spacing of approximately 20–30 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure proper tuber development, soil aeration, and ease of harvesting.
Potato.management_text: Successful potato cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed tuber planting, fertilizer application, irrigation, earthing-up, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is critical for high yield, with balanced use of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium to support both foliage growth and tuber formation. Potassium is particularly important for improving tuber size, quality, and storage life. Irrigation is most important during sprouting, flowering, and tuber bulking stages, while excessive water should be avoided to prevent tuber rot. Earthing-up is an essential practice where soil is mounded around the plant base to protect developing tubers from sunlight exposure and greening.
Potato.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are vital in potato cultivation. Potato tuber moth damages both foliage and stored tubers, while aphids weaken plants by feeding on sap and also transmit viral diseases. Late blight is one of the most destructive diseases, causing dark lesions on leaves and rapid plant collapse under cool, wet conditions. Early blight creates concentric ring-like spots on leaves, reducing photosynthesis and yield. Bacterial wilt causes sudden wilting and plant death, while fungal tuber rot leads to decay during growth or storage. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, use of certified disease-free seed tubers, proper field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, timely irrigation management, and integrated pest management practices. Regular monitoring and early intervention are essential to minimize losses and ensure high-quality potato production.

Elabatu (Thai Eggplant).overview: Thai Eggplant, commonly known as “Elabatu” in Sri Lanka, is a tropical vegetable crop belonging to the Solanaceae family and scientifically identified as Solanum melongena. It is widely cultivated in many parts of Sri Lanka due to its adaptability to warm climatic conditions and its importance in local cuisine. Elabatu grows best in temperatures between 22°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained fertile loamy or sandy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop requires moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and sufficient sunlight for healthy growth and fruit development. Thai eggplant plants produce small round green or light-colored fruits that are commonly used in curries and traditional dishes. The crop can be cultivated throughout the year under suitable environmental conditions and generally starts harvesting within 70 to 90 days after planting. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, humidity, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Farmers must also manage common pests and diseases such as aphids, whiteflies, fruit and shoot borers, bacterial wilt, and fungal leaf spot diseases, which can significantly reduce crop yield and quality if not properly controlled.

activities.title: Plough and prepare beds
activities.description: Plough 30 cm deep. Form raised beds.
activities.why: Good drainage essential for this long-duration crop.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 10–15 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Organic base supports long cropping duration.

activities.title: Transplant in evening
activities.description: Plant 25-day-old seedlings. Water immediately.
activities.why: Reduces transplant shock.

activities.title: Water daily first 2 weeks
activities.description: Gentle watering at base.
activities.why: Critical establishment period.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha ring application.
activities.why: Nitrogen for branching.

activities.title: Scout for Fruit & Shoot Borer
activities.description: Check for wilted shoot tips daily.
activities.why: FSB is the main pest of all eggplant types.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha.
activities.why: Sustained nutrition for continuous fruiting.

activities.title: Regular pest and disease check
activities.description: Inspect fruits and shoots.
activities.why: Continuous monitoring maintains crop health.

activities.title: Begin first harvest
activities.description: Pick small green fruits when full sized and firm.
activities.why: Harvesting at correct size gives best culinary quality.

activities.title: Harvest every 3–5 days
activities.description: Remove all mature fruits regularly.
activities.why: Regular picking maintains plant productivity.

irrigation.notes: Elabatu tolerates moderate drought but needs consistent moisture for continuous fruiting.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden wilting of entire plant; milky ooze from cut stem in water.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Wet soil, warm temperatures (25–35°C), poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Crop rotation. Raised beds. Use disease-free planting material.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants. Drench soil with lime.

diseases.name: Fungal Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Dark or yellowish spots on leaves reducing photosynthesis and plant vigour.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, overhead irrigation, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Avoid wetting foliage.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Chlorothalonil (2 g/L) spray.

pests.name: Fruit and Shoot Borer
pests.damage: Bores into shoots and fruits causing wilting of growing tips and internal fruit damage.
pests.identification: Withered shoot tips ('dead heart'); entry holes on fruits with frass.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Remove and destroy infested shoot tips and fruits.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L). Apply fortnightly.

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap causing leaf curling and yellowing; may transmit viral diseases.
pests.identification: Clouds of tiny white insects rising from leaves when disturbed.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective mulch to deter whiteflies.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) on leaf undersides.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks plant sap; causes curling, yellowing, and stunted shoot growth.
pests.identification: Dense clusters on new growth and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil (5 ml/L). Encourage beneficial insects (ladybirds).
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) if severe.

risks.name: Fruit & Shoot Borer
risks.description: Primary threat. Can cause >50% loss.
risks.signs: Multiple wilted tips, many bored fruits.
risks.mitigation: Daily scouting, pheromone traps, weekly spray.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Root rot and wilt promotion.
risks.mitigation: Raised beds, drainage channels.

harvest.method: Cut with short stalk. Handle carefully.
harvest.post_harvest: Store in cool shaded area. Market within 3–4 days.

Elabatu (Thai Eggplant).growth_stages_text: Thai eggplant cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, flowering, fruit development, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 7–14 days after sowing, while the vegetative growth stage continues for about 30–40 days depending on environmental conditions and soil fertility. Flowering begins approximately 45–60 days after planting, followed by fruit formation and harvesting. Harvesting generally starts within 70–90 days after planting when the fruits become fully developed, firm, and reach the desired market size. Farmers commonly use seed propagation methods and maintain plant spacing of approximately 60–75 cm between plants and rows to ensure proper sunlight exposure, healthy branching, and good air circulation.
Elabatu (Thai Eggplant).management_text: Successful Thai eggplant cultivation requires a week-by-week crop management schedule including nursery preparation, land preparation, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth, flowering, and fruiting stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to improve plant health and fruit production. Irrigation frequency varies according to crop growth stages, with frequent watering required during germination, flowering, and fruit development stages, while moderate irrigation is sufficient during vegetative growth. Farmers identify harvesting readiness when the fruits become shiny, firm, and reach suitable size and color for consumption and market use.
Elabatu (Thai Eggplant).disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in Thai eggplant cultivation. Aphids and whiteflies suck plant sap, causing curling, yellowing, and weakening of leaves, while fruit and shoot borers damage shoots and fruits by feeding internally, reducing fruit quality and yield. Bacterial wilt causes sudden wilting and collapse of plants due to bacterial infection in the root and stem system, and fungal leaf spot diseases appear as dark or yellowish spots on leaves that reduce photosynthesis and plant vigor. These problems can be controlled through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plant parts, biological pest control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, maintaining soil fertility, use of healthy seedlings, and timely pest and disease control measures  help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Tibbatu (Turkey Berry).overview: Tibbatu, scientifically known as Solanum torvum, is a small perennial vegetable and medicinal plant commonly grown in Sri Lanka. It belongs to the Solanaceae family and is widely cultivated in home gardens and rural agricultural areas throughout the country. Tibbatu grows well in tropical climatic conditions with temperatures ranging between 20°C and 30°C and can tolerate moderate drought conditions. The crop prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH between 5.5 and 7.0. It requires moderate rainfall, adequate sunlight, and regular watering for healthy flowering and fruit production. The plant produces small green berry-like fruits that are commonly used in Sri Lankan cuisine and traditional medicine due to their nutritional and medicinal value. Tibbatu can be cultivated throughout the year under suitable environmental conditions and usually begins fruiting within a few months after planting. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, sunlight, rainfall, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases affecting the crop include aphids, fruit borers, bacterial wilt, and fungal leaf diseases, which can reduce plant health and yield if not properly managed.

duration.note: days after planting to first harvest

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Minimal land prep needed — hardy plant.

activities.title: Clear land and dig planting holes
activities.description: Dig holes 30×30×30 cm. Fill with compost mix.
activities.why: Good start promotes fast establishment.

activities.title: Plant seedlings or cuttings
activities.description: Water immediately. Shade for first week if hot.
activities.why: Good establishment reduces care needed later.

activities.title: Water every 3 days for first month
activities.description: Maintain moisture at base.
activities.why: Young plants need consistent moisture.

activities.title: Apply compost mulch
activities.description: Apply 2 kg compost per plant as ring mulch.
activities.why: Slow-release nutrition for perennial plant.

activities.title: Check for aphids and leaf spot
activities.description: Inspect leaves for pest and disease signs.
activities.why: Early management prevents spread.

activities.title: Encourage branching
activities.description: Lightly prune main stem tip to encourage lateral branching.
activities.why: More branches = more fruiting points.

activities.title: Maintain moisture during fruiting
activities.description: Water every 5–7 days.
activities.why: Consistent moisture improves fruit size.

activities.title: Begin first harvest
activities.description: Pick berry clusters when fully green and firm.
activities.why: Over-ripe berries become soft and less flavourful.

activities.title: Harvest every 7–10 days
activities.description: Pick full clusters regularly.
activities.why: Regular picking stimulates new berry production.

irrigation.notes: Tibbatu is drought tolerant once established. Reduce watering in rainy season.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden wilting and collapse of plant; vascular browning in cut stem.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High soil moisture, warm temperatures, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Crop rotation. Raised beds. Avoid Solanaceae family in same plot.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants. Lime soil. Improve drainage.

diseases.name: Fungal Leaf Disease
diseases.symptoms: Yellow, brown, or black spots on leaves; reduces photosynthesis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, poor air circulation, wet foliage.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L).

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from young shoots causing leaf curling and weak growth.
pests.identification: Small soft-bodied insects on shoot tips and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Yellow sticky traps.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) if heavy infestation.

pests.name: Fruit Borer
pests.damage: Damages developing fruits by feeding inside; reduces marketable yield.
pests.identification: Entry holes on fruit surface with frass; internal damage visible when cut.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Remove and destroy infested fruits.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L). Spray when first entry holes noticed.

risks.name: Drought
risks.description: Tibbatu is naturally drought tolerant. Severe drought reduces fruit size.
risks.mitigation: Water during prolonged dry spells.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Root rot in poorly drained soils.
risks.mitigation: Plant on raised ground or ridges.

harvest.method: Cut entire berry clusters with scissors. Handle carefully.
harvest.post_harvest: Store in cool shaded area. Use fresh for cooking. Market within 2–3 days.

Tibbatu (Turkey Berry).growth_stages_text: Tibbatu cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, flowering, fruit development, and harvesting. Germination generally occurs within 7–14 days after sowing, while the vegetative growth stage continues for about 30–45 days depending on environmental conditions and soil fertility. Flowering begins around 45–60 days after planting, followed by fruit formation and harvesting. Harvesting can usually begin within 70–90 days after planting when the fruits become fully developed, green, and firm. Farmers mainly use seed propagation methods and maintain proper spacing of approximately 60–90 cm between plants to ensure healthy plant growth, proper sunlight exposure, and air circulation.
Tibbatu (Turkey Berry).management_text: Successful cultivation requires a week-by-week management schedule including nursery preparation, land preparation, planting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed management, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using organic compost together with nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium fertilizers to improve plant growth and fruit production. Irrigation frequency varies according to the crop stage, with regular watering required during germination, flowering, and fruiting stages, while moderate watering is sufficient during mature vegetative growth. Harvesting indicators include firm green fruits, healthy fruit size, and mature clusters suitable for consumption and market use.
Tibbatu (Turkey Berry).disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in tibbatu cultivation. Aphids attack young leaves and shoots by sucking plant sap, causing leaf curling and weak plant growth. Fruit borers damage developing fruits by feeding inside them, reducing fruit quality and market value. Bacterial wilt causes sudden wilting and collapse of plants due to bacterial infection in the stem and root system, while fungal leaf diseases appear as yellow, brown, or black spots on leaves that reduce photosynthesis and overall plant health. These problems can be controlled through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plant parts, proper drainage, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, maintaining healthy soil moisture levels, use of healthy seedlings, timely disease identification, and proper irrigation management help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Bitter Gourd.overview: Bitter gourd, commonly known as “Karawila” in Sri Lanka, is a tropical vegetable crop belonging to the Cucurbitaceae family and scientifically known as Momordica charantia. It is widely cultivated throughout Sri Lanka due to its adaptability to warm climatic conditions and its nutritional and medicinal value. Bitter gourd grows best in temperatures between 24°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.7. The crop requires moderate to high rainfall, regular irrigation, and adequate sunlight for healthy vine growth and fruit development. Bitter gourd is commonly used in Sri Lankan cuisine and traditional medicine because of its health benefits, including its ability to help regulate blood sugar levels. The crop generally begins harvesting within 55 to 70 days after planting and can continue producing fruits for several weeks under proper management. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, humidity, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases affecting bitter gourd include fruit flies, aphids, red pumpkin beetles, powdery mildew, downy mildew, and viral diseases, which can significantly reduce crop quality and yield if not properly controlled.

activities.title: Dig planting pits
activities.description: Dig 45×45×45 cm pits. Fill with compost + topsoil mix (1:1).
activities.why: Deep pits with rich organic matter support vigorous vine growth.

activities.title: Install trellis
activities.description: Erect bamboo or wooden poles 2 m high with wire or string netting.
activities.why: Bitter gourd is a climbing vine and needs firm support from the start.

activities.title: Sow seeds in pit
activities.description: Sow 2–3 seeds per pit at 2 cm depth. Water gently.
activities.why: Multiple seeds ensure at least one germinates.

activities.title: Thin to one plant
activities.description: Remove weaker seedlings, keeping the strongest.
activities.why: Competition between seedlings reduces overall vigour.

activities.title: Water every 2 days
activities.description: Keep soil moist. Avoid waterlogging.
activities.why: Consistent moisture needed for germination.

stages.name: Vine Growth
stages.description: Train vines and build vegetative frame.

activities.title: Train vines onto trellis
activities.description: Guide young tendrils onto the trellis as they emerge.
activities.why: Proper training maximises light interception and air flow.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha in ring around plant.
activities.why: Nitrogen drives rapid vine extension.

activities.title: Weed around base
activities.description: Remove weeds within 30 cm of plant base.
activities.why: Weeds reduce nutrients and harbour pests.

activities.title: Check male/female flower ratio
activities.description: First flowers are male. Female flowers (with small fruit at base) appear later.
activities.why: Understanding flower types helps identify poor pollination issues.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Potassium during fruiting improves fruit weight and bitterness.

activities.title: Scout for fruit fly
activities.description: Check fruits for puncture marks and ooze. Set protein bait traps.
activities.why: Fruit fly is the most damaging pest of bitter gourd.

activities.title: First harvest
activities.description: Harvest fruits when full sized but still firm and green (before turning yellow/orange).
activities.why: Over-ripe fruits lose market value and reduce further yield.

activities.title: Harvest every 2–3 days
activities.description: Pick all mature fruits. Never leave yellowing fruits.
activities.why: Leaving mature fruits stops new fruit development.

fertilization.why: Rich pit preparation feeds vine throughout season.

irrigation.notes: Do not let soil dry out during flowering — causes flower and fruit drop.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powder-like fungal coating on upper leaf surfaces and stems.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High temperatures (25–30°C), low humidity, dry conditions.
diseases.prevention: Good air circulation. Avoid overcrowding. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Tebuconazole (1 ml/L) every 10 days.

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.symptoms: Yellow angular patches on upper leaf surface; greyish-purple downy growth below.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, cool nights, heavy dew, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Widen plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation. Trellis to improve airflow.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) every 7 days.

diseases.name: Viral Diseases (Mosaic)
diseases.symptoms: Mosaic yellow-green patterns on leaves; leaf distortion; stunted growth; reduced yield.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High aphid/whitefly populations; hot dry weather.
diseases.prevention: Control vectors with sticky traps. Use virus-free seeds. Remove infected plants immediately.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Focus on vector control: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam.

pests.name: Fruit Fly
pests.damage: Lays eggs inside developing fruits causing internal rotting and market rejection.
pests.identification: Soft watery spots on fruits; maggots inside when cut open.
pests.prevention: Protein bait traps. Cover young fruits with bags. Collect and destroy fallen fruits.
pests.treatment: Malathion bait spray (Malathion + molasses + water). Fipronil bait stations.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; major vector of viral mosaic diseases.
pests.identification: Clusters on new shoots; sticky honeydew on leaves attracting ants.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective silver mulch. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) or Acetamiprid. Spray at first sighting.

pests.name: Red Pumpkin Beetle
pests.damage: Adults feed on leaves and cotyledons of young plants; severe damage in early stages.
pests.identification: Shiny red-orange beetles (5–8 mm) visible on leaves; large irregular leaf holes.
pests.prevention: Row covers at seedling stage. Hand-pick adults in morning.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos 20EC (2 ml/L) or Carbaryl (2 g/L). Apply in early morning.

risks.name: Fruit Fly
risks.description: Most serious threat. Can destroy 40–60% of harvest.
risks.signs: Punctured fruits with ooze, larvae in cut fruits.
risks.mitigation: Protein bait traps from day 40. Harvest frequently. Remove fallen fruits daily.

risks.name: Excessive Rain
risks.description: Promotes powdery and downy mildew. Poor pollination in heavy rain.
risks.signs: White powdery patches, angular yellow spots.
risks.mitigation: Preventive fungicide spray before rainy season.

indicators: Fruit full sized with prominent ridges/warts
Skin is dark green and firm
harvest.method: Cut with sharp knife or scissors. Leave 2 cm stalk.
harvest.post_harvest: Store in cool shaded area. Market within 3–4 days. Avoid bruising.

Bitter Gourd.growth_stages_text: Bitter gourd cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative vine growth, flowering, fruit development, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after sowing, while vegetative growth continues for about 20–30 days depending on environmental conditions and nutrient availability. Flowering generally begins around 30–40 days after planting, followed by fruit formation and harvesting. The crop usually starts producing harvestable fruits within 55–70 days after planting. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain proper spacing of approximately 1.5–2 meters between plants and rows to provide enough space for vine spreading, sunlight exposure, and air circulation. Trellis systems or vertical supports are also commonly used to improve fruit quality and reduce disease incidence.
Bitter Gourd.management_text: Successful bitter gourd cultivation requires a week-by-week management schedule including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed management, vine training, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth, flowering, and fruiting stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to improve vine growth and fruit production. Irrigation frequency varies according to crop growth stages, with frequent watering required during germination, flowering, and fruit development stages, while moderate irrigation is maintained during mature vegetative growth. Farmers identify harvesting readiness when fruits become fully elongated, green, tender, and reach suitable size before over-ripening occurs.
Bitter Gourd.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in successful bitter gourd cultivation. Fruit flies lay eggs inside developing fruits, causing rotting and internal damage that reduce market quality. Aphids suck plant sap, resulting in curling, yellowing, and weakening of leaves, while red pumpkin beetles feed on leaves and young seedlings causing severe plant damage. Powdery mildew appears as white powder-like growth on leaves and stems, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor, while downy mildew causes yellow patches and fungal growth under humid conditions. Viral diseases may cause leaf curling, mosaic patterns, and stunted growth, significantly affecting yield. These problems can be controlled through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected fruits and leaves, use of trellis systems, biological pest control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper drainage management, timely irrigation, use of healthy seeds, and climate-aware cultivation methods help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Luffa.overview: Luffa, commonly known as “Niyan Wetakolu” or sponge gourd in Sri Lanka, is a tropical vegetable crop belonging to the Cucurbitaceae family and scientifically known as Luffa aegyptiaca (or Luffa cylindrica). It is widely cultivated in many parts of Sri Lanka, especially in home gardens and dry to intermediate zone agricultural areas, due to its adaptability to warm climatic conditions. Luffa grows best in temperatures between 24°C and 32°C and requires well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for vigorous vine growth and proper fruit development. Young luffa fruits are used as a vegetable in Sri Lankan cuisine, while mature fruits are used as natural sponges after drying. The crop can be harvested for vegetables within 50 to 70 days after planting, while mature sponge production takes longer depending on growing conditions. Important factors affecting luffa cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, sunlight, rainfall, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include fruit flies, aphids, red pumpkin beetles, powdery mildew, and downy mildew, which can reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly managed.

duration.note: days after planting for vegetable harvest

activities.description: 45×45×45 cm pits with compost mix. Install 2 m trellis.

activities.title: Sow 2 seeds per pit at 2 cm depth
activities.description: Water gently. Keep moist.
activities.why: Ensures at least one strong plant per pit.

activities.title: Thin to one plant per pit
activities.description: Remove weaker seedling.
activities.why: Avoids competition between plants.

stages.name: Vine Growth
stages.description: Rapid vine extension and leaf development.

activities.title: Guide vines onto trellis
activities.description: Direct main stem upward.
activities.why: Early training prevents tangled vines.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha ring around base.
activities.why: Supports rapid vine extension.

activities.title: Check for fruit fly
activities.description: Set protein bait traps. Check fruits for puncture marks.
activities.why: Fruit fly is main pest — act early.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Supports fruit development.

activities.title: First vegetable harvest
activities.description: Pick young tender fruits (20–30 cm, still soft). Cut with knife.
activities.why: Young fruits have best eating quality.

activities.title: Harvest every 2–3 days
activities.description: Do not allow fruits to mature if selling as vegetable.
activities.why: Over-mature fruits become fibrous and unsaleable.

irrigation.notes: Luffa is moderately drought tolerant but regular watering improves yield.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew

diseases.favorable_conditions: Dry weather, high temperatures, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Trellis to improve airflow. Remove infected leaves.
