Luffa.growth_stages_text: Luffa cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative vine growth, flowering, fruit development, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after sowing, while vigorous vine growth continues for about 20–30 days depending on environmental conditions and nutrient availability. Flowering generally begins around 35–45 days after planting, followed by fruit formation and harvesting. Vegetable harvesting usually starts within 50–70 days after planting when fruits are young, tender, and green, while sponge harvesting is done later when fruits become mature, dry, and fibrous. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 1.5–2 meters between plants and rows to support healthy vine spreading, sunlight exposure, and good air circulation. Trellis systems or vertical supports are widely used to improve fruit quality, reduce disease incidence, and make harvesting easier.
Luffa.management_text: Successful luffa cultivation requires a week-by-week crop management schedule including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed management, vine training, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth, flowering, and fruiting stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to improve vine growth and fruit production. Irrigation frequency changes according to crop growth stages, with frequent watering required during germination, flowering, and fruit development stages, while moderate irrigation is maintained during mature vegetative growth. Harvesting indicators for vegetable use include tender green fruits with soft skin and suitable length, while mature sponge production requires fruits to become brown, dry, lightweight, and fibrous before collection.
Luffa.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in successful luffa cultivation. Fruit flies lay eggs inside developing fruits, causing internal rotting and fruit damage that reduce market quality. Aphids suck plant sap and cause leaf curling, yellowing, and weakening of plants, while red pumpkin beetles feed on leaves and seedlings, resulting in severe plant damage during early growth stages. Powdery mildew appears as white powder-like fungal growth on leaves and stems, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor, while downy mildew causes yellow patches and fungal growth under humid conditions. These problems can be controlled through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected fruits and leaves, use of trellis systems, biological pest control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, maintaining proper drainage, timely irrigation management, use of healthy seeds, and climate-aware cultivation methods help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Pathola (Snake Gourd).overview: Pathola, commonly known in Sri Lanka as ridge gourd or “Pathola”, is a tropical vegetable crop belonging to the Cucurbitaceae family and scientifically known as Luffa acutangula. It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, especially in home gardens and warm lowland and intermediate zones, due to its high adaptability to tropical conditions. Pathola grows best in temperatures between 24°C and 32°C and requires well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy vine growth and proper fruit formation. The young fruits are commonly used as a vegetable in Sri Lankan cooking and are valued for its soft texture and nutritional benefits. Pathola can be harvested within 50 to 65 days after planting, depending on growing conditions and management practices. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include fruit flies, aphids, red pumpkin beetles, powdery mildew, and viral infections, which can reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly controlled.

Pumpkin.overview: Pumpkin, commonly known as “Wattakka” in Sri Lanka, is a widely cultivated tropical vegetable crop belonging to the Cucurbitaceae family and scientifically known as Cucurbita moschata (and sometimes Cucurbita maxima depending on the variety). It is grown throughout Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones as well as home gardens, due to its adaptability to warm climatic conditions and relatively low maintenance requirements. Pumpkin grows best in temperatures between 24°C and 32°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy or sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for vigorous vine growth and proper fruit development. Pumpkin is a highly nutritious vegetable rich in vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants, and it is widely used in Sri Lankan curries and traditional dishes. The crop generally takes about 90 to 120 days after planting to reach harvest depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting pumpkin cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include fruit flies, aphids, pumpkin beetles, powdery mildew, downy mildew, and viral infections, which can reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Prepare large pits for long-duration vines.

activities.title: Dig large pits
activities.description: 60×60×60 cm pits. Fill with rich compost + topsoil mix.
activities.why: Large pits support the extensive root system of pumpkin.

activities.title: Mark rows 3 m apart
activities.description: Allow 2×3 m spacing for vine spreading.
activities.why: Pumpkin vines can spread 3–4 m — adequate space prevents crowding.

activities.title: Sow 3 seeds per pit
activities.description: Sow at 3 cm depth. Water and cover.
activities.why: Pumpkin germinates reliably in warm soil.

activities.title: Thin to 2 plants per pit
activities.description: Remove weakest seedling once established.
activities.why: Two plants per pit improve pollination.

activities.title: Water every 3 days
activities.description: Keep pit moist. Do not waterlog.
activities.why: Even germination needs consistent moisture.

stages.name: Vine Extension
stages.description: Long vegetative phase — vigorous vine growth.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha ring around plant.
activities.why: Supports vigorous vine growth phase.

activities.title: Weed and mulch
activities.description: Remove weeds. Lay straw mulch around base.
activities.why: Mulch suppresses weeds and retains moisture during dry spells.

activities.title: Check for male/female flowers
activities.description: Male flowers appear first. Female flowers have small fruit base.
activities.why: Hand-pollinate early morning if natural pollination is poor.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: MOP 100 kg/ha between vines.
activities.why: High potassium for fruit development and storage quality.

activities.title: Place fruits on dry surface
activities.description: Slide a tile or wood piece under developing fruits.
activities.why: Prevents soil contact which causes rotting of the fruit base.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest mature fruits at correct stage.

activities.title: Check maturity signs
activities.description: Tap fruit — hollow sound indicates maturity. Skin should resist thumbnail pressure.
activities.why: Harvesting too early reduces storage life significantly.

activities.title: Harvest mature fruits
activities.description: Cut stalk leaving 5–10 cm attached to fruit.
activities.why: Long stalk prevents entry of pathogens into the fruit.

fertilization.why: Concentrated nutrition at root zone for long-duration crop.
Potassium for fruit fill and storage quality.

irrigation.frequency: Every 4–5 days during establishment; every 7 days when established
irrigation.notes: Pumpkin is drought tolerant once established. Reduce watering 2 weeks before harvest to improve storage life.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery growth on leaves and stems; reduces photosynthesis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Warm dry weather, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good vine management. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Tebuconazole (1 ml/L).

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.symptoms: Yellow patches on upper leaf surface; grey downy growth beneath.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, cool nights, wet conditions.
diseases.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation. Improve air circulation.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) every 7–10 days.

pests.name: Fruit Fly
pests.damage: Causes internal fruit rotting; major post-set damage.
pests.identification: Puncture marks on fruit surface; maggots inside.
pests.prevention: Protein bait traps. Collect and destroy fallen fruits daily.
pests.treatment: Malathion bait spray. Fipronil bait.

pests.name: Pumpkin Beetle
pests.damage: Defoliates young seedlings; adults eat leaves and stems.
pests.identification: Yellow-orange beetles on plants in early morning.
pests.prevention: Row covers at germination. Hand-pick adults early morning.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) or Carbaryl dust.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; weakens vines; transmits viruses.
pests.identification: Clusters on growing tips and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) at threshold.

risks.name: Drought During Fruit Set
risks.description: Fruit abortion and reduced fruit size.
risks.signs: Wilting vines, small or no fruits setting.
risks.mitigation: Maintain irrigation every 4–5 days during fruit set. Mulch heavily.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Root rot and vine collapse.
risks.signs: Yellowing wilting vines in wet conditions.
risks.mitigation: Ensure proper drainage from pit. Raise planting area if needed.

indicators: Skin hardens and resists thumbnail scratch
harvest.method: Cut with sharp knife, leave 5–10 cm stalk. Handle carefully to avoid bruising.
harvest.frequency: Single or multiple harvests over 2–3 weeks
harvest.post_harvest: Cure in shade for 7 days to harden skin. Store in cool dry ventilated area up to 3 months. Do not stack more than 2 layers.

Pumpkin.growth_stages_text: Pumpkin cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative vine growth, flowering, fruit development, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after sowing, while vigorous vine growth continues for about 30–45 days depending on environmental conditions and nutrient availability. Flowering begins around 45–60 days after planting, followed by fruit development and maturation. Harvesting generally takes place between 90–120 days after planting when fruits reach full size, develop a hard outer skin, and the stem near the fruit begins to dry. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 2–3 meters between plants and rows to allow extensive vine spreading, sunlight penetration, and proper air circulation. In many farming systems, vines are allowed to trail on the ground, though in smaller plots limited trellising may be used.
Pumpkin.management_text: Successful pumpkin cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, vine training, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during early vegetative growth, flowering, and fruit development stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to support strong vine growth and improve fruit size and quality. Irrigation frequency is higher during germination and flowering stages due to high water demand, while moderate irrigation is maintained during fruit maturation to prevent fruit cracking and disease development. Harvesting indicators include fully grown fruits with hard rinds, dull skin color, and drying stems indicating maturity.
Pumpkin.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in successful pumpkin cultivation. Fruit flies damage developing fruits by laying eggs inside, causing internal decay and reducing market value. Aphids feed on plant sap, resulting in curling, yellowing, and weakened vines, while pumpkin beetles attack leaves and seedlings causing severe damage during early growth stages. Powdery mildew appears as white fungal growth on leaves, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor, while downy mildew causes yellow patches and leaf collapse under humid conditions. Viral infections may cause mosaic patterns, stunted growth, and reduced yield. These problems can be controlled through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plant parts, use of resistant varieties, and biological or chemical pest control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, maintaining soil fertility, use of healthy seeds, and climate-aware cultivation techniques help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Cabbage.overview: Cabbage, commonly known as “Gowa Kol” in Sri Lanka, is a cool-climate leafy vegetable crop belonging to the Brassicaceae family and scientifically known as Brassica oleracea var. capitata. It is mainly cultivated in the up-country regions of Sri Lanka such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where low temperatures support proper head formation and compact growth. Cabbage grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 22°C and requires fertile, well-drained loamy soil rich in organic matter with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy vegetative growth and tight head development. Cabbage is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine in curries, stir-fries, and salads and is valued for its nutritional content including vitamins C and K, fiber, and antioxidants. The crop is generally ready for harvest within 70 to 90 days after transplanting depending on the variety and growing conditions. Important factors affecting cabbage cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, cabbage worms, diamondback moth, cutworms, club root, and fungal leaf spot diseases, which can reduce yield and head quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough and lime soil if needed
activities.description: Deep plough 30 cm. Apply lime if pH below 6.0. Form raised beds.
activities.why: Club root disease is promoted by acidic soils. Liming prevents it.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 15 t/ha well-decomposed compost.
activities.why: High organic matter supports uniform head development.

activities.title: Transplant 25-day seedlings
activities.description: Plant in late afternoon. Water immediately. Space 45×60 cm.
activities.why: Evening planting reduces heat stress on young seedlings.

activities.title: Water daily for 2 weeks
activities.description: Water at base morning and evening.
activities.why: Consistent moisture in first 2 weeks determines establishment success.

activities.title: First weeding and fertilize
activities.description: Weed thoroughly, then apply top dressing.
activities.why: Clean weeds improve air circulation — reduces disease risk.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 100 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha ring around plant.
activities.why: Nitrogen drives leaf development; potassium improves head density.

activities.title: Daily DBM scouting
activities.description: Inspect undersides of leaves for DBM eggs and larvae (tiny green caterpillars).
activities.why: Diamondback Moth is the #1 threat to cabbage. Early detection is critical.

stages.name: Head Development
stages.description: Critical stage — head fills and hardens.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Final nitrogen push for head fill.

activities.title: Check for head rot
activities.description: Look for slimy soft areas at head base. Improve drainage if seen.
activities.why: Black rot and head rot can destroy heads quickly in wet conditions.

activities.title: Maintain consistent moisture
activities.description: Irregular watering at this stage causes head splitting.
activities.why: Head splitting reduces market value to zero.

activities.title: Test for maturity
activities.description: Press head with palm — firm solid head indicates maturity.
activities.why: Soft heads are immature; over-mature heads split and rot.

activities.title: Cut heads
activities.description: Cut with sharp knife at base leaving 3–4 outer leaves for protection.
activities.why: Outer leaves protect the head during transport.

irrigation.notes: Maintain consistent moisture during head fill. Irregular watering = head splitting.

diseases.name: Club Root
diseases.symptoms: Swollen, distorted club-shaped roots; wilting and severely stunted growth.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Acidic soil (pH < 6.0), high soil moisture, warm temperatures.
diseases.prevention: Apply lime to raise soil pH above 6.5. Crop rotation for 4+ years. Good drainage.
diseases.treatment: No effective chemical. Lime soil. Remove and destroy infected plants.

diseases.name: Fungal Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Brown circular spots with concentric rings on leaves; reduces photosynthesis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, cool weather, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Remove infected leaves. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Iprodione (1.5 ml/L).

pests.name: Diamondback Moth
pests.damage: Larvae scrape and eat leaf tissue; creates 'window pane' pattern; develops resistance to insecticides quickly.
pests.identification: Small pale green caterpillars on leaf undersides; window-like transparent patches on leaves.
pests.prevention: Use Bt sprays. Rotate insecticide classes to prevent resistance. Remove heavily damaged leaves.
pests.treatment: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) 2 g/L or Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L). Never use same chemical repeatedly.

pests.name: Cabbage Worm
pests.damage: Large caterpillars eat entire leaves leaving only main veins; causes severe defoliation.
pests.identification: Green velvety caterpillars on plants; white butterfly visible in field.
pests.prevention: Hand-pick caterpillars and egg masses. Use fine netting.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) or Bt spray (2 g/L).

pests.name: Cutworm
pests.damage: Cuts seedling stems at soil level; young transplants collapse overnight.
pests.identification: Cut-off stems at ground level; fat grey-brown caterpillar found in soil near base.
pests.prevention: Soil cultivation before planting. Remove surface debris.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules in soil at planting or Carbofuran (2 g/m²).

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Dense colonies on inner leaves; causes distortion and reduced head quality.
pests.identification: Grey-green colonies inside developing heads.
pests.prevention: Inspect inner leaves regularly. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) before heads close.

risks.name: Diamondback Moth Resistance
risks.description: DBM has developed resistance to most insecticides. Poor control = total crop loss.
risks.signs: Spraying with no effect on caterpillar populations.
risks.mitigation: Use Bt and Spinosad. Rotate with different modes of action. Never spray synthetic pyrethroids only.

risks.name: Head Splitting
risks.description: Sudden rain after dry period causes rapid water uptake and splits heads.
risks.mitigation: Maintain consistent irrigation. Harvest promptly when mature.

indicators: Head is firm and compact when pressed
harvest.method: Cut at base with sharp knife. Keep 3–4 outer leaves.
harvest.post_harvest: Store in cool ventilated shade. Remove damaged outer leaves. Market within 7–10 days.

Cabbage.growth_stages_text: Cabbage cultivation follows several important growth stages including nursery development, transplanting, vegetative growth, head formation, and harvesting. Seeds are usually sown in nurseries and germinate within 5–8 days, with seedlings ready for transplanting after 3–4 weeks. After transplanting, vegetative growth continues for about 30–45 days, followed by head initiation and compact head formation. Harvesting is generally done within 70–90 days after transplanting when heads become firm, compact, and reach the desired size. Farmers commonly use transplanting methods and maintain spacing of approximately 45–60 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure adequate space for head development, air circulation, and disease prevention.
Cabbage.management_text: Successful cabbage cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including nursery preparation, land preparation, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is essential during vegetative growth and head formation stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to support strong leaf development and tight head formation. Adequate nitrogen is important early in growth, but balanced nutrition is required later to avoid loose or split heads. Irrigation is most critical during head formation, as water stress can reduce head size and quality, while excessive water may lead to root diseases.
Cabbage.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are crucial in cabbage cultivation. Aphids suck plant sap, causing curling and weakening of leaves, while cabbage worms and diamondback moth larvae feed on leaves, causing severe defoliation. Cutworms damage young seedlings by cutting stems at soil level. Club root is a serious soil-borne disease that causes swollen, distorted roots and poor plant growth, while fungal leaf spot diseases reduce photosynthesis and overall plant vigor. These issues can be managed through crop rotation, field sanitation, removal of infected plants, use of resistant varieties, biological control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, balanced fertilizer use, and integrated pest management techniques help improve yield and maintain high-quality cabbage heads.

Cauliflower (Gowa).overview: Cauliflower, commonly known as “Gowa” in Sri Lanka, is a cool-climate vegetable crop belonging to the Brassicaceae family and scientifically known as Brassica oleracea var. botrytis. It is mainly cultivated in cooler regions of Sri Lanka such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy because these areas provide favorable environmental conditions for growth. Cauliflower grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 25°C and requires well-drained loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 6.8. The crop requires moderate rainfall, adequate sunlight, and regular irrigation for proper curd development and healthy growth. It is usually harvested within 60 to 75 days after transplanting and can produce an average yield of 6–9 tons per hectare under suitable cultivation conditions. Common factors affecting cauliflower cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, humidity, and sunlight. Farmers must also manage diseases such as club root, damping off, and yellowing curds, which are commonly caused by acidic soil conditions, fungal infections, poor drainage, excessive sunlight, or high temperatures.

activities.title: Plough, lime, and raise beds
activities.description: pH should be 6.0–6.8. Lime if needed. Form raised beds.
activities.why: Acidic soils cause club root and poor curd development.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 15 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Cauliflower is a heavy feeder needing rich soil.

activities.title: Transplant 25-day seedlings
activities.description: Plant in late afternoon. Water immediately.
activities.why: Protects seedlings from heat stress.

activities.title: Water daily 2 weeks
activities.description: Water at base daily.
activities.why: Critical establishment period.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 100 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha ring.
activities.why: Nitrogen for leaf development; potassium for curd quality.

activities.title: Scout for DBM
activities.description: Check leaf undersides for larvae and diamond-shaped holes.
activities.why: DBM damages leaves that are needed for curd development.

activities.title: Tie outer leaves over curd
activities.description: When small curd appears (5–8 cm), tie outer leaves over it to blanch.
activities.why: Exposure to sunlight turns curd yellow/brown and reduces quality.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Final nitrogen for curd fill.

activities.title: Check for curd browning
activities.description: Inspect tied curd for browning, rot, or pest entry.
activities.why: Browning reduces market value. Act quickly if seen.

activities.title: Harvest when curd is compact
activities.description: Cut when curd is firm, white, 15–20 cm diameter. Do not delay.
activities.why: Over-mature curds become loose (ricey) and unsaleable.

activities.title: Continue daily checks
activities.description: Adjacent plants may mature within days of each other. Check daily.
activities.why: Missing harvest peak = total loss of that head.

irrigation.notes: Never let soil dry during curd development — causes hollow stems and poor curd quality.

diseases.name: Club Root
diseases.symptoms: Swollen, distorted, club-shaped roots; wilting and stunted growth above ground.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Acidic soil (pH < 6.0), high moisture, warm temperatures.
diseases.prevention: Maintain soil pH above 6.5 with lime. Crop rotation (no Brassicas for 4 years). Good drainage.
diseases.treatment: No effective chemical cure. Apply lime to raise pH. Remove infected plants.

diseases.name: Damping Off
diseases.symptoms: Seedling stem rots at soil surface; seedlings collapse and die.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Overwatered nursery, poor drainage, dense sowing.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained nursery soil. Avoid overwatering. Good air circulation.
diseases.treatment: Drench with Captan or Thiram (2 g/L) at first sign of infection.

diseases.name: Yellowing Curds
diseases.symptoms: Curd develops yellow or brown discolouration instead of white.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Direct sunlight exposure during curd development, high temperatures.
diseases.prevention: Tie outer leaves over developing curd ('blanching') to block sunlight.
diseases.treatment: Not a disease — preventable by leaf blanching. No chemical treatment needed.

pests.name: Cabbage Worm (Imported)
pests.damage: Feeds on leaves causing irregular holes; can destroy young plants completely.
pests.identification: Green caterpillars on leaf undersides; white butterfly seen in field.
pests.prevention: Inspect regularly. Remove egg masses from leaf undersides. Use netting.
pests.treatment: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) spray (2 g/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L).

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from young shoots; excretes honeydew causing sooty mould.
pests.identification: Dense clusters of small grey-green insects on shoot tips.
pests.prevention: Encourage predatory insects. Neem oil spray (5 ml/L).
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) if infestation severe.

risks.name: High Temperature (>25°C)
risks.description: Causes poor curd set, loose ricey curds, or no curd formation.
risks.signs: Premature bolting, small or loose curds.
risks.mitigation: Plant in cooler season. Use temperature-tolerant varieties.

risks.name: Frost
risks.description: Kills young transplants and damages curds.
risks.mitigation: Cover with sacks during cold nights in Nuwara Eliya.

indicators: Firm compact white curd 15–20 cm diameter
Curd surface is smooth and even
harvest.method: Cut at base with sharp knife leaving 3–4 leaves.
harvest.frequency: Single harvest; check daily near maturity
harvest.post_harvest: Keep cool and shaded. Avoid sunlight — yellows quickly. Market within 3–5 days.

Cauliflower (Gowa).growth_stages_text: Cauliflower cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, curd formation, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after sowing, while the vegetative stage continues for about 20–30 days. Curd development begins around 45–55 days after transplanting, and harvesting is generally carried out between 60–75 days when the curds become compact, white, and fully developed. Farmers commonly use transplanting methods rather than direct seeding, maintaining plant spacing of approximately 45–60 cm between plants and rows to ensure healthy growth and air circulation.
Cauliflower (Gowa).management_text: Proper crop management requires a week-by-week schedule for activities such as land preparation, nursery management, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, pest management, and harvesting. Fertilizer application is important during early vegetative growth and curd development stages, using nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium-based fertilizers in recommended quantities to improve yield and curd quality. Irrigation frequency varies according to the crop stage, with frequent watering required during germination and curd formation stages, while moderate irrigation is sufficient during vegetative growth. Farmers identify harvest readiness when the cauliflower curds become firm, compact, and reach marketable size before flowering starts.
Cauliflower (Gowa).disease_management_text: Disease management is essential in cauliflower cultivation. Club root disease causes swollen and distorted roots, leading to wilting and stunted growth, and can be controlled through proper soil pH management and crop rotation. Damping off disease appears as seedling stem rot near the soil surface and can be reduced through proper drainage and fungicide treatment. Yellowing curds occur due to excessive sunlight or high temperatures and can be minimized by covering the curds with leaves during development. Risk mitigation practices such as proper drainage systems, balanced fertilizer use, timely irrigation, disease monitoring, resistant varieties, and climate-aware cultivation methods help farmers reduce crop damage and improve productivity under changing environmental conditions.

Kohlrabi (Nokol).overview: Kohlrabi, commonly known as “Nokol” in Sri Lanka, is a cool-climate vegetable crop belonging to the Brassicaceae family and scientifically known as Brassica oleracea var. gongylodes. It is mainly cultivated in cooler regions of Sri Lanka such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where the environmental conditions are suitable for its growth. Kohlrabi grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 25°C and requires fertile, well-drained loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The crop requires moderate rainfall, good sunlight exposure, and regular watering for healthy bulb development. It is generally harvested within 55 to 70 days after planting and can produce good yields under proper cultivation practices. The edible swollen stem is rich in vitamins, minerals, and dietary fiber, making it a nutritious vegetable. Important factors affecting kohlrabi cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, soil fertility, rainfall, humidity, and sunlight. Farmers must also manage common diseases and pests such as leaf spot, damping off, aphids, and cabbage worms, which can reduce crop quality and yield if not properly controlled.

activities.title: Plough and lime if needed
activities.description: Prepare beds, pH 6.0–7.0.
activities.why: Club root prevention requires correct pH.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 10 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Good organic matter improves bulb development.

activities.title: Transplant or direct sow
activities.description: 25-day transplants or direct sow at 30×40 cm. Water immediately.
activities.why: Close spacing produces uniform sized bulbs.

activities.title: Water daily first week
activities.description: Maintain consistent moisture.
activities.why: Fast-growing crop needs consistent water from day 1.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha ring.
activities.why: Nitrogen drives rapid stem swelling.

activities.title: Weed thoroughly
activities.description: Remove all weeds. Shallow hoeing.
activities.why: Weeds compete heavily with this fast-growing crop.

stages.name: Bulb/Stem Swelling
stages.description: Swollen stem development — harvest window approaches.

activities.title: Monitor bulb size
activities.description: Check swollen stem weekly.
activities.why: Kohlrabi can over-mature and become woody quickly.

activities.title: Maintain consistent moisture
activities.description: Water every 3 days.
activities.why: Irregular watering causes cracking of the swollen stem.

activities.title: Begin harvest
activities.description: Harvest when swollen stem is 6–8 cm diameter. Do not wait longer.
activities.why: Over-mature kohlrabi becomes tough and fibrous, losing market value.

activities.title: Harvest remaining plants over 7 days
activities.description: Stagger harvest as plants mature.
activities.why: Field does not mature uniformly — check every 2–3 days.

irrigation.notes: Consistent moisture prevents cracking. Reduce before harvest.

diseases.name: Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Small dark or yellowish circular spots on leaves; spreads rapidly under humid conditions.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, poor air circulation, overhead irrigation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead watering. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L) every 7–10 days.

diseases.name: Damping Off
diseases.symptoms: Young seedlings rot at soil surface and collapse.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged nursery, poor drainage, dense sowing.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained nursery. Moderate watering. Thin seedlings.
diseases.treatment: Captan or Thiram drench (2 g/L) at seedling stage.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks plant sap; weakens growth and excretes honeydew attracting ants.
pests.identification: Clusters of small soft-bodied insects on new growth and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Remove heavily infested shoots.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L). Spray in cool morning hours.

pests.name: Cabbage Worm
pests.damage: Feeds on leaves causing large irregular holes; reduces marketability.
pests.identification: Green caterpillars visible on leaf surfaces; white butterfly in field.
pests.prevention: Hand-pick caterpillars and egg masses. Use row covers.
pests.treatment: Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) 2 g/L or Chlorpyrifos 2 ml/L if severe.

risks.name: High Temperature
risks.description: Causes bolting (flowering) instead of stem swelling.
risks.signs: Plants bolt to flower without forming bulb.
risks.mitigation: Plant in cooler season. Use heat-tolerant variety.

risks.name: Late Harvest
risks.description: Over-mature stems become woody and unmarketable.
risks.mitigation: Harvest promptly at 6–8 cm diameter.

indicators: Stem still feels soft when pressed
harvest.method: Pull or cut at base. Remove large outer leaves.
harvest.frequency: Harvest over 7–10 days as plants mature
harvest.post_harvest: Remove leaves. Store in cool area. Market within 5–7 days.

Kohlrabi (Nokol).growth_stages_text: Kohlrabi cultivation includes several important growth stages such as germination, vegetative growth, bulb enlargement, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after sowing, while the vegetative stage continues for about 20–30 days. Bulb enlargement begins around 35–45 days after planting, and harvesting is generally carried out between 55–70 days when the swollen stem becomes tender, round, and reaches marketable size. Farmers commonly use transplanting methods or direct seeding methods with proper spacing of approximately 30–45 cm between plants and rows to ensure healthy bulb development and sufficient air circulation.
Kohlrabi (Nokol).management_text: Effective crop management requires a week-by-week schedule covering nursery preparation, land preparation, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest management, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during the vegetative and bulb development stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium fertilizers in recommended quantities to improve plant growth and bulb quality. Irrigation frequency depends on the crop stage, with regular watering needed during germination and bulb formation stages, while moderate irrigation is sufficient during vegetative growth. Harvesting is done when the swollen stem becomes firm, tender, and reaches the desired size before it becomes fibrous.
Kohlrabi (Nokol).disease_management_text: Disease and pest management play a major role in successful kohlrabi cultivation. Leaf spot disease appears as small dark or yellowish spots on leaves and can spread rapidly under humid conditions, while damping off causes young seedlings to rot near the soil surface, resulting in seedling death. Aphids suck plant sap and weaken plant growth, while cabbage worms feed on leaves and reduce crop quality. These problems can be managed through proper field sanitation, balanced fertilizer application, crop rotation, biological control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as maintaining proper drainage, regular field monitoring, timely irrigation, use of healthy seedlings, and climate-aware cultivation practices help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Radish.overview: Radish, commonly known as “Raabu” or “Mooli” in Sri Lanka, is a fast-growing root vegetable crop belonging to the Brassicaceae family and scientifically known as Raphanus sativus. It is mainly cultivated in cooler and intermediate regions such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where climatic conditions are suitable for proper root enlargement and quality development. Radish grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 25°C and requires loose, well-drained sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and good sunlight exposure for healthy growth and crisp root formation. Radish is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine in curries, salads, and pickles and is valued for its nutritional benefits including vitamins, fiber, and minerals. The crop is very fast-growing and can be harvested within 30 to 50 days after sowing depending on the variety. Important factors affecting radish cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, root maggots, flea beetles, damping off, and fungal leaf diseases, which can reduce yield and root quality if not properly managed.
Radish.propagation: Direct sowing only (taproots do not tolerate transplanting)

activities.title: Deep plough and fine tilth
activities.description: Plough to 30 cm. Break all clods finely. Remove stones.
activities.why: Stones and clods cause forked or misshapen roots — no market value.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 10 t/ha compost. Do not apply fresh manure.
activities.why: Fresh manure causes forking and hairy roots.

activities.title: Sow seeds in shallow furrows
activities.description: Draw furrows 1 cm deep, 25 cm apart. Sow seeds 5 cm apart. Cover lightly.
activities.why: Shallow sowing and fine soil ensures uniform germination.

activities.title: Thin seedlings
activities.description: Thin to 10 cm apart once 5–8 cm tall.
activities.why: Crowded radish develops no roots — only leaves.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Single nitrogen application at mid-growth.

activities.title: Weed between rows
activities.description: Hand weed carefully to avoid root disturbance.
activities.why: Weed competition causes poor root development.

activities.title: Water every 3–4 days
activities.description: Maintain consistent moisture.
activities.why: Irregular moisture causes cracking and hollow centres.

activities.title: Check for harvest readiness
activities.description: Pull one plant to check root size (3–5 cm diameter).
activities.why: Radish matures faster than expected in warm weather.

activities.title: Harvest in one go
activities.description: Pull all plants when mature. Do not leave over-ripe plants in soil.
activities.why: Over-mature radish becomes pithy, hollow, and hot — unmarketable.

fertilization.why: All nutrition needed at sowing — too short a crop for multiple applications.

irrigation.notes: Consistent moisture prevents cracking and hollow centres. Reduce before harvest.

diseases.name: Damping Off
diseases.symptoms: Seedling stems rot at soil surface; seedlings collapse and die.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Overwatering, poor drainage, dense sowing.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained seedbed. Thin to proper spacing. Moderate watering.
diseases.treatment: Captan or Thiram drench (2 g/L) at seedling stage.

diseases.name: Fungal Leaf Disease
diseases.symptoms: Dark spots or patches on leaves; reduces growth and yield.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, dense canopy, wet foliage.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2 g/L) if symptoms develop.

pests.name: Root Maggot
pests.damage: Tunnels inside developing roots causing internal damage and market rejection.
pests.identification: Entry holes at root crown; maggots visible inside when cut.
pests.prevention: Crop rotation. Avoid planting in previously infested soil.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules at sowing. Soil drench if early damage noticed.

pests.name: Flea Beetle
pests.damage: Creates small circular holes in leaves; severe in seedling stage.
pests.identification: Tiny black jumping beetles on plants; pin-point holes in leaves.
pests.prevention: Row covers on seedlings. Keep field clean.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos 20EC (2 ml/L) or Carbaryl dust if damage is significant.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from young leaves; causes curling.
pests.identification: Clusters on young growth.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) only if infestation is heavy.

risks.name: High Temperature (>25°C)
risks.description: Radish bolts to flower without forming root.
risks.mitigation: Plant in cooler season (up-country Maha). Avoid low country.

risks.name: Late Harvest
risks.description: Over-mature radish becomes pithy, hollow, and spicy-hot — unmarketable.
risks.mitigation: Set harvest reminders at day 30 and 35. Pull test 3–4 plants.

indicators: Root feels solid and heavy when pulled
harvest.method: Pull plant by hand. Remove leaves, leaving 3 cm of stalk.
harvest.post_harvest: Trim leaves. Wash roots. Bunch and market within 2–3 days. Wilts quickly without refrigeration.

Radish.growth_stages_text: Radish cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, root enlargement, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 3–7 days after sowing, making radish one of the quickest germinating root crops. Early seedling growth is rapid, followed by vegetative growth for about 10–20 days depending on environmental conditions and soil fertility. Root enlargement begins around 15–25 days after sowing, and harvesting is typically carried out between 30–50 days when roots become tender, firm, and reach the desired size and shape. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 5–8 cm between plants and 20–30 cm between rows to ensure proper root expansion and uniform development.
Radish.management_text: Successful radish cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, thinning, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during early vegetative growth and root development stages, commonly using balanced nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers and organic compost to support healthy root formation. Excess nitrogen is avoided as it can cause excessive leaf growth and poor root quality. Irrigation frequency is highest during germination and early root development stages to maintain uniform soil moisture, while moderate irrigation is maintained during later stages to prevent root cracking and pithiness.
Radish.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in radish cultivation. Aphids suck plant sap and cause leaf curling and weakening of plants, while root maggots damage developing roots by tunneling inside, reducing market value. Flea beetles create small holes in leaves, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor. Damping off affects young seedlings, causing stem rot near the soil surface and plant death, while fungal leaf diseases appear as dark spots or patches on leaves that reduce growth and yield. These problems can be controlled through crop rotation, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, proper irrigation management, removal of infected plants, and biological or chemical control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, timely weeding, maintaining soil fertility, and integrated pest management techniques help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Big Onion.overview: Big onion, commonly known as “Bombay onion” in Sri Lanka, is an important bulb vegetable crop belonging to the Amaryllidaceae family and scientifically known as Allium cepa. It is widely cultivated in Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones such as Anuradhapura, Polonnaruwa, and Hambantota due to their warm climate and suitable soil conditions. Big onion grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and requires well-drained sandy loam or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, controlled irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for proper bulb formation and development. Big onion is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine as a key ingredient in curries, sambols, and various food preparations, and is also an important commercial crop for local markets and imports. The crop generally takes about 90 to 120 days after transplanting to reach maturity depending on the variety and growing conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include thrips, onion maggots, aphids, purple blotch, downy mildew, and bacterial soft rot, which can significantly reduce yield and bulb quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Fine seedbed for uniform bulb development.

activities.title: Plough and fine till
activities.description: Plough twice. Final fine tilth with cross harrowing. Level carefully.
activities.why: Uneven fields cause irregular irrigation and uneven bulb development.

activities.title: Apply basal fertiliser
activities.description: Incorporate compost 10 t/ha and basal NPK.
activities.why: Onion has a shallow root system — all nutrients must be near the surface.

activities.title: Transplant 35–40 day seedlings
activities.description: Plant seedlings (pencil thickness) at 10×20 cm. Water immediately.
activities.why: Correct seedling size is critical — too small or large = poor establishment.

activities.title: Water daily for 2 weeks
activities.description: Twice daily if hot and dry.
activities.why: High water demand at establishment in dry zone conditions.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Nitrogen for leaf development — leaves manufacture the food that fills the bulb.

activities.title: Daily thrips scouting
activities.description: Check inner young leaves for silver streaking — sign of thrips.
activities.why: Thrips cause purple blotch and direct leaf damage — most destructive onion pest.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha.
activities.why: Second feed before bulbing begins.

stages.name: Bulb Development
stages.description: Leaves fold over as bulb swells.

activities.title: Reduce irrigation frequency
activities.description: Water every 5–7 days. Reduce nitrogen.
activities.why: Excessive nitrogen and water at this stage causes soft bulbs with poor storage.

activities.title: Check for purple blotch
activities.description: Look for water-soaked lesions with purple centres on leaves.
activities.why: Purple blotch from thrips damage spreads rapidly during bulbing.

activities.title: Stop irrigation 2 weeks before harvest
activities.description: Allow leaves to yellow and fall over naturally.
activities.why: Curing in the soil hardens the bulb skin for storage.

activities.title: Harvest when 75% of tops fall
activities.description: Pull bulbs when majority of tops have fallen over and yellowed.
activities.why: Premature harvest gives soft bulbs with short shelf life.

activities.title: Field cure for 7–10 days
activities.description: Leave harvested bulbs on the ground in the sun for 7–10 days.
activities.why: Sun curing hardens outer skin and improves storage life dramatically.

fertilization.why: Shallow-rooted crop needs all basal nutrients near surface.

irrigation.frequency: Every 3–4 days (leaf stage); every 5–7 days (bulbing stage)
irrigation.notes: Stop irrigation completely 2 weeks before harvest. This is critical for bulb quality and storage.

diseases.name: Purple Blotch
diseases.symptoms: Purple or brown lesions on leaves that gradually spread; premature drying and yield reduction.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid weather, prolonged leaf wetness, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove infected leaves early.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Iprodione (1.5 ml/L) every 7–10 days.

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.symptoms: Pale green to yellow patches on leaves; grey-purple fungal growth under humid conditions.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Cool humid weather, heavy dew, poor air circulation, overhead irrigation.
diseases.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation. Crop rotation. Resistant varieties.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) spray preventively in wet conditions.

diseases.name: Bacterial Soft Rot
diseases.symptoms: Watery soft decay of bulbs with foul smell; bulbs become unusable.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High moisture, wounds on bulbs, warm conditions.
diseases.prevention: Reduce irrigation before harvest. Handle bulbs carefully. Proper storage.
diseases.treatment: No chemical cure. Improve drainage and harvest handling. Proper curing of bulbs.

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Most damaging onion pest; silvery streaks on leaves; severe leaf curling; reduced bulb size.
pests.identification: Tiny yellowish insects in leaf folds; silvery scarring on leaves.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Avoid dense planting. Proper field sanitation.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Fipronil. Apply in early morning. Rotate chemicals.

pests.name: Onion Maggot
pests.damage: Larvae damage roots and bulb base causing rotting and plant death.
pests.identification: Plants wilting; soft rotting at bulb base; white maggots in soil.
pests.prevention: Crop rotation. Avoid planting where maggots were present previously.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules at planting. Soil drench if attack is identified early.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes leaf curling and may transmit viruses.
pests.identification: Colonies on young leaves and inside developing bulb.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Yellow sticky traps.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L).

risks.name: Excessive Rain During Bulbing
risks.description: Soft bulbs, neck rot, very poor storage life.
risks.signs: Soft watery bulbs, plants not falling over.
risks.mitigation: Plant in dry season. Ensure drainage. Reduce irrigation.

risks.name: Drought During Leaf Development
risks.description: Reduced leaf area = small bulbs.
risks.mitigation: Maintain regular irrigation schedule. Mulch between rows.

harvest.method: Pull by hand or use fork. Do not throw — bulbs bruise easily.
harvest.post_harvest: Field cure 7–10 days in sun. Top and root trim. Grade by size. Store in well-ventilated slatted trays. Shelf life 3–6 months.

Big Onion.growth_stages_text: Big onion cultivation follows several important growth stages including seed germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, bulb initiation, bulb enlargement, maturation, and harvesting. Seeds usually germinate within 7–12 days after sowing in nursery beds. Seedlings are maintained in the nursery for approximately 35–45 days before transplanting into the main field. After transplanting, the crop enters a vegetative growth stage where leaves develop rapidly. Bulb initiation generally begins 45–60 days after transplanting and is followed by bulb enlargement, during which nutrients and carbohydrates accumulate within the bulb. Harvesting is carried out when approximately 50–70% of the tops fall over naturally and leaves begin to dry, indicating bulb maturity. The entire crop cycle generally lasts 90–120 days depending on variety and environmental conditions.
Big Onion.management_text: Successful big onion cultivation requires a well-planned crop management schedule involving land preparation, nursery establishment, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation management, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting. Farmers typically prepare raised beds and incorporate organic matter before planting. Seedlings are transplanted at a spacing of approximately 10–15 cm between plants and 20–30 cm between rows to ensure adequate bulb development. Fertilizer management is critical, with nitrogen supporting leaf growth during early stages and phosphorus and potassium promoting bulb formation and overall plant health. Organic compost and well-decomposed manure are often added during land preparation to improve soil fertility and structure.
Big Onion.disease_management_text: Irrigation management plays a major role in onion production. Frequent light watering is required during germination and immediately after transplanting to establish healthy seedlings. During vegetative growth and bulb development, irrigation should be provided regularly to maintain consistent soil moisture. However, excessive water can increase disease incidence and reduce bulb quality. Irrigation is usually reduced or stopped completely two to three weeks before harvest to promote bulb maturity, improve storage quality, and reduce the risk of bulb rotting.
Big Onion.risk_mitigation_text: Disease and pest management are essential for achieving high-quality onion yields. Thrips are among the most damaging pests, feeding on leaf tissues and causing silvery streaks, leaf curling, and reduced bulb size. Onion maggots attack roots and bulbs, resulting in plant wilting and bulb decay. Aphids weaken plants by sucking sap and can transmit viral diseases. Purple blotch disease appears as purple-brown lesions on leaves, eventually causing leaf drying and reduced bulb development. Downy mildew causes pale green to yellow patches on leaves accompanied by gray fungal growth under humid conditions. Bacterial soft rot affects harvested and stored bulbs, causing water-soaked tissues, foul odor, and bulb decomposition. These problems can be managed through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer application, adequate plant spacing, good drainage, timely irrigation management, resistant varieties, and integrated pest management practices. Risk mitigation strategies are important for maintaining stable production and reducing losses. During periods of excessive rainfall, farmers should improve drainage systems to prevent waterlogging and disease outbreaks. During drought conditions, mulching and efficient irrigation practices help conserve soil moisture. Regular field inspections allow early detection of pests and diseases before severe damage occurs. Using certified seeds, maintaining proper nutrient management, and practicing crop rotation can significantly improve crop health and productivity. These measures help ensure stable yields, high-quality bulbs, and greater resilience to changing environmental conditions and climate-related stresses.

Red Onion.overview: Red onion, commonly known as “Rathu Lunu” in Sri Lanka, is an important bulb vegetable crop belonging to the Amaryllidaceae family and scientifically known as Allium cepa var. aggregatum (shallot-type red onion commonly grown in Sri Lanka). It is widely cultivated in dry and intermediate zones of Sri Lanka such as Anuradhapura, Polonnaruwa, and Hambantota due to their hot climate and well-drained soil conditions. Red onion grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and requires well-drained sandy loam or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, controlled irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for proper bulb development and maturation. Red onion is a key ingredient in Sri Lankan cooking and is widely used in curries, sambols, and various traditional dishes, valued for its strong flavor and nutritional benefits. The crop generally takes about 80 to 110 days after planting to reach maturity depending on the variety and growing conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include thrips, onion maggots, aphids, purple blotch, downy mildew, and bacterial soft rot, which can significantly reduce yield and bulb quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough and fine till
activities.description: Fine tilth to 20 cm. Level well. Form raised beds if needed.
activities.why: Shallow root system requires fine, even seedbed.

activities.title: Apply basal fertiliser
activities.description: Compost 10 t/ha + TSP + MOP incorporated.
activities.why: Base nutrients at surface for shallow feeder.

activities.title: Plant sets at 15×20 cm spacing
activities.description: Push sets 2–3 cm into soil. Pointed tip up. Water immediately.
activities.why: Correct depth — if too shallow, sets dry out; too deep, slow emergence.

activities.title: Water every 2 days first 2 weeks
activities.description: Keep soil moist for sprouting.
activities.why: Sets need moisture to sprout and establish.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Nitrogen for leaf and shoot development.

activities.title: Scout for thrips daily
activities.description: Check young inner leaves for silver streaks.
activities.why: Thrips are the main pest. Control before populations explode.

stages.name: Bulbing
stages.description: Bulbs swell as leaves transfer nutrients.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: MOP 75 kg/ha. Reduce Urea.
activities.why: Higher potassium for bulb fill.

activities.title: Stop irrigation
activities.description: Cease all irrigation 2 weeks before harvest.
activities.why: Dry conditions cure the bulb skin properly.

activities.title: Harvest when tops fall
activities.description: Pull when 70–80% of leaves have fallen and yellowed.
activities.why: Premature harvest = poor storage life.

activities.title: Sun-cure 5–7 days
activities.description: Spread in sun for 5–7 days.
activities.why: Sun curing hardens skin and extends shelf life to 6+ months.

fertilization.why: Shallow feeder — all basal near surface.

irrigation.notes: Stop irrigation 2 weeks before harvest — critical for storage quality.

diseases.name: Purple Blotch
diseases.symptoms: Purple-coloured lesions on leaves; premature leaf drying; reduced bulb size.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, prolonged leaf wetness, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Iprodione (1.5 ml/L) every 7–10 days.

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.symptoms: Pale green to yellow patches on leaves; grey fungal growth on leaf surface in humid conditions.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Cool humid weather, heavy dew, overhead irrigation.
diseases.prevention: Crop rotation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Good air circulation.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) preventively in wet season.

diseases.name: Bacterial Soft Rot
diseases.symptoms: Watery soft decay of bulbs; foul smell; affects stored bulbs.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High moisture, wounds during harvest, warm conditions.
diseases.prevention: Reduce irrigation 2 weeks before harvest. Handle bulbs carefully. Proper curing.
diseases.treatment: No chemical cure. Improve drainage and post-harvest handling.

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Most damaging pest; causes silvery leaf streaks, curling, and reduced bulb size.
pests.identification: Tiny yellowish insects in leaf folds; silver scarring on leaves.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Avoid dense planting.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Fipronil. Rotate chemical classes to avoid resistance.

pests.name: Onion Maggot
pests.damage: Larvae attack roots and bulb base causing rotting and plant collapse.
pests.identification: Wilting plants; maggots in soil around root zone; soft decaying base.
pests.prevention: Crop rotation. Avoid planting in infested fields.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules at planting.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes leaf curling; may transmit viral diseases.
pests.identification: Colonies on young leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L).

risks.name: Rain at Harvest
risks.description: Wet conditions re-sprout harvested bulbs and promote neck rot.
risks.mitigation: Time harvest for dry weather. Cure immediately after pulling.

risks.name: Planting Diseased Sets
risks.description: Infected sets spread disease to entire crop.
risks.signs: Early plant death, ring rot in bulbs.
risks.mitigation: Use certified or own disease-free sets only. Dip in Mancozeb solution before planting.

harvest.method: Pull by hand. Do not bruise. Remove soil.
harvest.post_harvest: Sun cure 5–7 days. Grade. Store in slatted open trays in cool ventilated shade. Shelf life 6–8 months.

Red Onion.growth_stages_text: Red onion cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, bulb initiation, bulb enlargement, maturation, and harvesting. Seeds or bulbs usually sprout within 5–10 days under suitable environmental conditions. During the seedling stage, roots and leaves develop rapidly to establish healthy plants. Vegetative growth continues for approximately 30–45 days, during which plants produce leaves that support future bulb formation. Bulb initiation begins when plants receive favorable temperature and day-length conditions. This is followed by bulb enlargement, where nutrients and carbohydrates accumulate in the bulbs. As the crop matures, the leaves gradually turn yellow and begin to fall over, indicating readiness for harvest. Harvesting is usually carried out between 80–110 days after planting when bulbs have reached their full size and maturity.
Red Onion.management_text: A typical crop management schedule begins with land preparation and planting. Well-decomposed organic manure or compost is incorporated into the soil before planting to improve fertility and soil structure. Red onions are commonly planted using bulbs or seedlings at a spacing of approximately 10–15 cm between plants and 20–30 cm between rows. Proper spacing improves air circulation, reduces disease incidence, and allows bulbs to develop uniformly. Weed control is particularly important during the early growth stages because weeds compete with onion plants for nutrients, water, and sunlight.
Red Onion.disease_management_text: Fertilizer management is essential for achieving high yields and good bulb quality. Basal fertilizer applications containing phosphorus and potassium are usually applied during land preparation to promote root development and early growth. Nitrogen is applied in split doses during vegetative growth to encourage healthy leaf development, which directly influences bulb size. Potassium is particularly important during bulb formation and enlargement because it improves bulb quality, storage life, and resistance to diseases. Excessive nitrogen application during late growth stages should be avoided because it can delay bulb maturation and reduce storage quality.
Red Onion.risk_mitigation_text: Irrigation management plays a critical role throughout the crop cycle. Adequate moisture is required during germination and early growth to ensure uniform plant establishment. During bulb initiation and enlargement, regular irrigation supports proper bulb development and prevents yield reduction. However, excessive watering should be avoided because waterlogged conditions encourage fungal diseases and bulb rot. Irrigation is usually reduced or stopped one to two weeks before harvest to allow bulbs to mature properly and improve storage quality. Disease and pest management are among the most important aspects of red onion production. Thrips are one of the most damaging pests and feed on leaves, causing silvery streaks, curling, and reduced photosynthesis. Onion maggots attack roots and bulbs, causing rotting and plant death. Aphids feed on plant sap and can transmit viral diseases. Purple blotch disease appears as purple-colored lesions on leaves, reducing photosynthetic activity and yield. Downy mildew causes pale green or yellow patches on leaves followed by fungal growth under humid conditions. Bacterial soft rot affects bulbs during growth and storage, causing soft, watery decay with a foul odor. These problems can be minimized through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, use of disease-free planting material, balanced fertilization, good drainage, and integrated pest management practices. Risk mitigation strategies are important for maintaining stable red onion production and reducing losses from environmental stresses. During periods of heavy rainfall, proper drainage systems should be maintained to prevent waterlogging and disease outbreaks. During drought conditions, efficient irrigation methods and mulching can help conserve soil moisture and reduce plant stress. Farmers should regularly monitor fields for early signs of pest and disease infestations and take timely corrective actions. Crop rotation, use of certified planting materials, balanced nutrient management, and proper storage practices help improve crop health and productivity. These measures ensure consistent bulb yields, improved bulb quality, longer storage life, and greater resilience to changing climatic conditions and environmental challenges.

Leeks.overview: Leeks are a cool-climate vegetable crop belonging to the Amaryllidaceae family and scientifically known as Allium ampeloprasum var. porrum. In Sri Lanka, leeks are mainly cultivated in cooler up-country regions such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where temperatures are favorable for growth. Leeks grow best in temperatures between 13°C and 24°C and require fertile, well-drained loamy soil rich in organic matter with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The crop requires moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and good sunlight exposure for proper stem development and healthy growth. Leeks are valued for their long edible white stems and are widely used in soups, salads, and vegetable dishes due to their mild onion-like flavor and nutritional benefits. The crop is generally harvested within 90 to 120 days after planting depending on the variety and growing conditions. Important factors affecting leek cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, sunlight, rainfall, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases affecting leeks include leaf miners, thrips, onion maggots, purple blotch disease, and fungal infections, which can reduce crop quality and yield if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Prepare deep fertile soil for long white stem development.

activities.title: Deep plough to 35 cm
activities.description: Fine tilth. Level well. Form beds.
activities.why: Leeks need deep soil for the long white stem to develop below ground.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 15 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Leeks are heavy feeders. Rich organic base is essential.

activities.title: Transplant 40–50 day seedlings into furrows
activities.description: Plant in 15 cm deep furrows at 15×30 cm spacing. Drop plant in, do not backfill yet.
activities.why: Deep planting in furrow promotes white stem (blanching) development.

activities.title: Water daily for 2 weeks
activities.description: Gentle watering at base.
activities.why: Deep-planted seedlings need moisture to establish.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 100 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: High nitrogen for the multi-leaf development phase.

activities.title: First earthing up
activities.description: Mound soil 10 cm up around plant stems.
activities.why: Earthing up excludes light and blanches the stem white.

activities.title: Scout for thrips and leaf miner
activities.description: Check leaves for silvering (thrips) and mines (leaf miner).
activities.why: Both pests cause leaf damage that reduces photosynthesis.

stages.name: Stem Development
stages.description: Continue earthing up for long white stem.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha.
activities.why: Sustains leaf growth and stem thickening.

activities.title: Second earthing up
activities.description: Mound soil further up stems.
activities.why: Second earthing extends the white stem length — improves market grade.

activities.title: Maintain regular irrigation
activities.description: Water every 4 days.
activities.why: Consistent moisture prevents pithy stems.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest when stems reach desired thickness.

activities.title: Check stem diameter
activities.description: Harvest when white stem is 2–3 cm diameter.
activities.why: Leeks can remain in ground longer than most vegetables if needed.

activities.title: Dig and harvest
activities.description: Use fork to loosen soil. Pull carefully.
activities.why: Leek stems are fragile — avoid bending or breaking.

irrigation.notes: Consistent moisture throughout. Leeks tolerate cooler conditions but not drought.

diseases.name: Purple Blotch
diseases.symptoms: Purple or brown lesions on leaves; gradually spreads causing premature leaf death.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, wet weather, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Iprodione (1.5 ml/L) every 7–10 days.

diseases.name: Fungal Stem Rot
diseases.symptoms: Yellowing, rotting, and collapse of leaves; white fluffy fungal growth at stem base.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, waterlogged soil, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained raised beds. Crop rotation. Remove infected plants.
diseases.treatment: Copper fungicide drench or Thiram spray at base of plant.

pests.name: Leaf Miner
pests.damage: Creates pale winding tunnels inside leaves; reduces photosynthesis.
pests.identification: Visible pale serpentine trails in leaf tissue.
pests.prevention: Remove heavily mined leaves. Avoid excessive nitrogen.
pests.treatment: Abamectin 1.8EC (0.5 ml/L) or Cyromazine if infestation is high.

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Sucks sap causing silvery patches, leaf curling, and drying.
pests.identification: Tiny yellowish insects in leaf folds; silvery scarring on leaves.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Reflective mulch. Avoid over-fertilisation.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Fipronil spray. Apply early morning.

pests.name: Onion Maggot
pests.damage: Damages roots and lower stems causing weak growth and rotting.
pests.identification: Wilting plants; white maggots visible in soil around root zone.
pests.prevention: Crop rotation. Avoid planting in previously affected soil.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules at planting. Soil drench if attack is early.

risks.name: High Temperature (>24°C)
risks.description: Bolting and poor stem development.
risks.signs: Plants flower without thick stem formation.
risks.mitigation: Plant in cool season only. Nuwara Eliya and Badulla.

risks.name: Insufficient Earthing Up
risks.description: Short green stem — low market value.
risks.mitigation: Earth up twice: at 5 WAP and 10 WAP.

harvest.method: Loosen soil with fork, pull plant carefully. Trim roots and top leaves.
harvest.frequency: Harvest over 2–3 weeks as plants reach size
harvest.post_harvest: Trim roots and outer leaves. Bunch in groups of 10. Store cool. Market within 5–7 days.

Leeks.growth_stages_text: Leek cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, stem thickening, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 7–14 days after sowing, while vegetative growth continues for several weeks depending on environmental conditions and nutrient availability. Stem thickening and elongation begin around 45–60 days after transplanting, and harvesting is generally carried out between 90–120 days when the stems become thick, long, tender, and reach marketable size. Farmers commonly use nursery-raised seedlings for transplanting and maintain proper spacing of approximately 15–20 cm between plants and 30–45 cm between rows to ensure healthy stem development and sufficient air circulation.
Leeks.management_text: Successful leek cultivation requires a week-by-week crop management schedule including nursery preparation, land preparation, transplanting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed management, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth and stem development stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to improve plant vigor and stem quality. Irrigation frequency varies according to crop growth stages, with regular watering required during germination and stem development stages, while moderate irrigation is maintained during mature vegetative growth. Harvesting indicators include thick white stems, healthy green leaves, and proper stem length suitable for consumption and market use.
Leeks.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in successful leek cultivation. Leaf miners create tunnels inside leaves that reduce photosynthesis and plant health, while thrips suck plant sap causing silvery patches, curling, and drying of leaves. Onion maggots damage roots and lower stems, leading to weak growth and rotting symptoms. Purple blotch disease appears as purple or brown lesions on leaves, which gradually spread and reduce plant vigor, while fungal infections can cause leaf yellowing, rotting, and poor stem quality under humid conditions. These problems can be managed through proper field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, crop rotation, proper spacing, removal of infected plants, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper drainage management, timely irrigation, use of healthy seedlings, and climate-aware cultivation methods help reduce crop damage and improve overall productivity.

Cowpea.overview: Cowpea, commonly known as “Vigna” or “Wandakka” in Sri Lanka, is a warm-season legume crop belonging to the Fabaceae family and scientifically known as Vigna unguiculata. It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones, due to its ability to tolerate drought conditions and grow in relatively poor soils. Cowpea grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.5. The crop requires low to moderate rainfall, good sunlight exposure, and minimal irrigation once established, making it suitable for rainfed agriculture. Cowpea is an important source of plant protein and is commonly consumed in Sri Lankan curries and dry vegetable dishes. It also improves soil fertility through nitrogen fixation, making it beneficial in crop rotation systems. The crop generally matures within 60 to 90 days after planting depending on the variety. Important factors affecting cowpea cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, bean flies, pod borers, thrips, powdery mildew, and viral infections, which can reduce yield and seed quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Minimal tillage needed — cowpea tolerates poor soils.

activities.title: Plough lightly to 20 cm
activities.description: Remove weeds. Break large clods.
activities.why: Cowpea tolerates poor soils but weeds in early stages cause significant yield loss.

activities.title: Sow 2 seeds per hole at 3 cm depth
activities.description: Space 20×45 cm. Cover and firm soil.
activities.why: Correct depth ensures rapid uniform germination.

activities.title: Thin to 1 plant per hole
activities.description: Remove weaker seedling once established.
activities.why: Avoids overcrowding and competition.

activities.title: Apply top dressing
activities.description: Urea 25 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha. Keep nitrogen low — cowpea fixes its own.
activities.why: Low nitrogen — excess inhibits the nitrogen-fixing nodules.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Hand weed or hoe between rows carefully.
activities.why: Critical weed-free period for establishment.

activities.title: Check for bean fly
activities.description: Look for wilted seedling stems with larval tunnels.
activities.why: Bean fly is the most damaging early pest of cowpea.

activities.title: Scout for pod borers
activities.description: Check developing pods for entry holes and frass.
activities.why: Pod borers are the main yield-reducing pest at this stage.

activities.title: Irrigate if dry during pod fill
activities.description: Water once if no rain for 10+ days during pod fill.
activities.why: Water stress during pod fill causes seed shrinkage and yield loss.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest at correct stage for intended use.
