activities.title: Green pod harvest (vegetable use)
activities.description: Harvest young tender pods when full but seeds not yet showing through skin.
activities.why: Green pods have best eating quality at this stage.

activities.title: Dry grain harvest
activities.description: Allow pods to dry and turn brown on plant. Harvest before pods shatter.
activities.why: Delay causes pod shattering and grain loss.

fertilization.why: Phosphorus for root nodule development. No nitrogen — cowpea fixes its own.
Minimal nitrogen only to kick-start early growth before nodules form.

irrigation.notes: Cowpea is drought tolerant. Avoid overwatering. One irrigation at pod fill if no rain is usually sufficient.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and plant vigour.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Hot dry weather alternating with humidity.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid excessive nitrogen.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Triadimefon (1 g/L).

diseases.name: Viral Mosaic
diseases.symptoms: Yellow-green mosaic patterns; leaf puckering; stunted growth; reduced pod fill.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High aphid populations; hot dry weather.
diseases.prevention: Use virus-free seeds. Control aphid vectors. Remove infected plants.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Control aphids with Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L).

pests.name: Bean Fly
pests.damage: Larvae mine stems causing 'dead heart'; young plants die; yield dramatically reduced.
pests.identification: Wilting seedlings at 7–14 days; maggots in stem when cut open.
pests.prevention: Seed dressing with insecticide. Early sowing. Use of resistant varieties.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos soil drench (2 ml/L) or Carbosulfan seed dressing.

pests.name: Pod Borer
pests.damage: Larvae feed inside developing pods reducing seed quality and yield.
pests.identification: Entry holes in pods; frass visible; damaged seeds inside.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Monitor pod formation stage closely.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) at flowering.

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Damages flowers and young pods; causes flower drop and reduced fruiting.
pests.identification: Tiny insects inside flowers; silvery petal scarring.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Spray at early flower bud stage.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Fipronil.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; major vector of mosaic viruses; causes curling and stunted growth.
pests.identification: Colonies on new growth and undersides of leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective mulch.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) or Acetamiprid.

risks.name: Drought at Pod Fill
risks.description: Shrivelled seeds, low grain weight, poor marketability.
risks.signs: Pods feel light, seeds not filling properly.
risks.mitigation: One irrigation during pod fill if dry for 10+ days.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Root rot kills plants rapidly. Cowpea has zero waterlogging tolerance.
risks.mitigation: Never plant in poorly drained areas. Raised beds in wetter zones.

indicators.green: Full pod, seeds not showing through skin, pod snaps cleanly
indicators.dry: Pods turn cream/brown, rattle when shaken

harvest.method: Hand pick green pods. Pull entire plant for dry grain. Thresh by beating.
harvest.frequency: Green pods: every 3–4 days. Dry grain: single harvest.
harvest.post_harvest: Sun dry grain 3–5 days. Store in sealed bags with neem leaves to repel weevils.

Cowpea.growth_stages_text: Cowpea cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, flowering, pod formation, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 4–7 days after sowing, while vegetative growth continues for about 20–30 days depending on environmental conditions and soil fertility. Flowering begins around 30–40 days after planting, followed by pod development and seed filling. Harvesting generally takes place between 60–90 days after planting when pods become mature, dry, and seeds reach full size. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 30–45 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure proper airflow, sunlight penetration, and healthy pod development.
Cowpea.management_text: Successful cowpea cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is generally moderate, with emphasis on phosphorus and potassium, while nitrogen requirements are lower due to the crop’s natural nitrogen fixation ability. Organic compost is often used to improve soil fertility and support early growth. Irrigation is most important during germination, flowering, and pod development stages, while mature plants can tolerate dry conditions. Harvesting indicators include fully developed pods that turn brown or dry and seeds that become hard and well-formed.
Cowpea.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in cowpea cultivation. Aphids feed on plant sap, causing curling, yellowing, and stunted growth, while bean flies attack seedlings and damage stems, leading to plant death in early stages. Pod borers feed inside developing pods, reducing seed quality and yield, while thrips damage flowers and reduce pod formation. Powdery mildew appears as white fungal growth on leaves, affecting photosynthesis and plant vigor, and viral infections may cause mosaic patterns, leaf distortion, and reduced productivity. These problems can be controlled through crop rotation, field sanitation, timely planting, balanced fertilizer use, resistant varieties, and biological or chemical control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, maintaining soil fertility, and integrated pest management techniques help reduce losses and improve overall productivity.

Mung Bean.overview: Mungbean, commonly known as “Mung” or “Green gram” in Sri Lanka, is a short-duration leguminous crop belonging to the Fabaceae family and scientifically known as Vigna radiata. It is widely cultivated in the dry and intermediate zones of Sri Lanka due to its ability to grow under warm climatic conditions and low rainfall environments. Mungbean grows best in temperatures between 25°C and 35°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.5. The crop requires low to moderate rainfall, full sunlight exposure, and minimal irrigation once established, making it suitable for rainfed cultivation systems. Mungbean is an important source of plant protein and is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine for curries, sprouts, and traditional dishes. It is also valuable for improving soil fertility through nitrogen fixation and is commonly used in crop rotation practices. The crop generally matures within 60 to 90 days after planting depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, pod borers, whiteflies, powdery mildew, bacterial leaf spot, and root rot, which can reduce yield and seed quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Light tillage — mung bean is a low-input crop.

activities.title: Plough lightly and clear weeds
activities.description: Shallow plough 15–20 cm. Remove previous crop residue.
activities.why: Good seedbed ensures uniform emergence.

activities.title: Fine tilth for small seeds
activities.description: Rake to fine seedbed — mung bean seeds are small.
activities.why: Fine tilth needed for good seed-soil contact.

stages.name: Sowing
stages.description: Broadcast or row sow at correct seed rate.

activities.title: Sow at 25–30 kg/ha seed rate
activities.description: Row sow 3 cm deep, 30 cm between rows. Or broadcast and rake in.
activities.why: Higher seed rate gives better canopy for weed suppression.

activities.title: Pre-sowing irrigation if soil is dry
activities.description: Irrigate 2 days before sowing to bring soil to field capacity.
activities.why: Dry soil causes poor germination of small mung bean seeds.

activities.title: First and only weeding
activities.description: Hand weed or hoe once between rows at 2 WAP.
activities.why: Mung bean smothers weeds once canopy closes — one early weeding is sufficient.

activities.title: Apply top dressing
activities.description: MOP 25 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Minimal potassium only. Nitrogen not needed — mung bean fixes its own.

activities.title: Scout for pod borers
activities.description: Check pods for entry holes. Act immediately at first sign.
activities.why: Pod borer can destroy 30–40% of pods in a week.

activities.title: One irrigation if very dry
activities.description: Water once at 50% flowering if no rain for 2+ weeks.
activities.why: Water stress at flowering causes flower drop and yield loss.

activities.title: First pod pick
activities.description: Harvest mature pods (black/dark) as they ripen. 2–3 picks needed.
activities.why: Mung bean pods do not all mature at once — picking avoids shattering loss.

activities.title: Final harvest
activities.description: Pull entire plant when 80% of pods are mature.
activities.why: Late pods continue ripening after cutting.

fertilization.why: Phosphorus for root nodule formation. No nitrogen needed.

irrigation.frequency: Minimal — 1–2 irrigations total if rain is insufficient
irrigation.notes: Mung bean is highly drought tolerant. Excess irrigation promotes fungal diseases and lush growth with fewer pods.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery fungal coating on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and pod fill.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Hot dry conditions alternating with humidity.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Use resistant varieties.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Triadimefon (1 g/L).

diseases.name: Bacterial Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Water-soaked angular dark lesions on leaves; lesions dry and may coalesce.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Warm humid weather, rain splash, overhead irrigation.
diseases.prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds. Crop rotation. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Copper Oxychloride spray (3 g/L).

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Yellowing, wilting, and death of plants; brown root decay.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged soil, heavy rain, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained soil. Avoid overwatering. Crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: Captan drench (2 g/L) or Metalaxyl (1.5 g/L).

pests.name: Pod Borer
pests.damage: Larvae feed inside developing pods; reduces seed quality and yield.
pests.identification: Entry holes in pods; frass visible; larvae inside when pods split open.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Monitor during pod fill stage.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) at pod fill stage.

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap; transmits Yellow Mosaic Virus causing major yield loss.
pests.identification: White insects on undersides of leaves; yellowing and curling.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective silver mulch.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) fortnightly.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; may transmit viral diseases.
pests.identification: Colonies on young shoots and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L).

risks.name: Drought at Flowering
risks.description: Flower drop and poor pod set.
risks.mitigation: One irrigation at 50% flowering.

risks.name: Heavy Rain at Harvest
risks.description: Pods shatter, seeds sprout on plant.
risks.signs: Pods splitting, seeds germinating on plant.
risks.mitigation: Harvest as soon as 70–80% mature. Do not wait for full maturity in rainy season.

indicators: Pods turn dark brown to black
harvest.method: 2–3 pickings of mature pods OR cut entire plant and sun-dry, then thresh.
harvest.post_harvest: Sun dry 3–5 days to 12% moisture. Winnow. Store in sealed bags with neem leaves to prevent weevil attack.

Mung Bean.growth_stages_text: Mungbean cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, flowering, pod formation, and harvesting. Germination typically occurs within 3–6 days after sowing, followed by rapid early growth for about 15–30 days. Flowering begins around 30–40 days after planting, followed by pod formation and seed filling until maturity. Harvesting is generally carried out within 60–90 days when most pods turn yellowish-brown and seeds become dry and fully developed. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 30–45 cm between plants and 45–60 cm between rows to ensure proper plant density, airflow, and efficient pod development.
Mung Bean.management_text: Successful mungbean cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer requirements are relatively low due to nitrogen fixation, but phosphorus and potassium play an important role in root development, flowering, and pod filling. Organic manure is often used to improve soil structure and moisture retention. Irrigation is most critical during germination and flowering stages, while the crop can tolerate dry conditions during later growth stages.
Mung Bean.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in mungbean cultivation. Aphids and whiteflies suck plant sap and may transmit viral diseases, causing stunted growth and leaf curling. Pod borers directly damage pods and reduce seed quality and yield. Powdery mildew appears as a white fungal coating on leaves, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor. Bacterial leaf spot causes dark lesions on leaves, while root rot occurs in poorly drained soils leading to plant death and yield loss. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, resistant varieties, timely sowing, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, and integrated pest management practices. Regular monitoring and early intervention help ensure healthy crop development and stable production.

Soybean.overview: Soybean, commonly known as “Soya Bean” in Sri Lanka, is an important leguminous oilseed crop belonging to the Fabaceae family and scientifically known as Glycine max. It is cultivated in several parts of Sri Lanka, particularly in dry and intermediate zones, due to its high nutritional value and adaptability to warm climatic conditions. Soybean grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy or sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The crop requires moderate rainfall, adequate sunlight, and regular soil moisture during flowering and pod development for optimal yield. Soybean is highly valued for its high protein and oil content and is widely used in food products, animal feed, and oil production. The crop generally matures within 90 to 120 days after planting depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting soybean cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, pod borers, soybean rust, bacterial blight, root rot, and fungal leaf spot diseases, which can significantly reduce yield and seed quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough to 20 cm and fine till
activities.description: Break clods. Level field for uniform irrigation.
activities.why: Even seedbed ensures uniform plant stand.

activities.title: Inoculate seed if first planting
activities.description: Treat seed with Bradyrhizobium japonicum inoculant if first time in field.
activities.why: Inoculant establishes nitrogen-fixing nodules — critical if soybean not grown before.

stages.name: Sowing
stages.description: Sow at correct rate and depth.

activities.title: Sow 40–50 kg/ha at 3–4 cm depth
activities.description: Row spacing 45 cm, 3–5 seeds per 30 cm.
activities.why: Correct population density is key to yield.

activities.title: Pre-sowing irrigation if dry
activities.description: Bring soil to field capacity before sowing.
activities.why: Dry soil causes poor germination.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Hand weed or inter-row cultivation.
activities.why: Weed-free first 4 weeks is critical.

activities.title: Apply top dressing
activities.description: MOP 50 kg/ha + Urea 25 kg/ha.
activities.why: Low nitrogen only as starter. Potassium for pod strength.

activities.title: Second weeding
activities.description: Final weeding — canopy closes after this.
activities.why: Canopy will suppress weeds from this point.

activities.title: Ensure adequate moisture
activities.description: Irrigate every 7–10 days if no rain. Critical stage.
activities.why: Water stress at R3–R5 (pod fill) causes the most significant yield reduction.

activities.title: Scout for pod borer
activities.description: Check pods for entry holes.
activities.why: Pod borer is the main pest of soybean.

activities.title: Reduce irrigation
activities.description: Stop watering 2 weeks before harvest.
activities.why: Allows uniform pod maturation and reduces lodging.

activities.title: Harvest when 90% pods are brown
activities.description: Cut or pull plants. Sun-dry before threshing.
activities.why: Premature harvest reduces seed oil content.

fertilization.why: Phosphorus and potassium only — no nitrogen basal.

irrigation.notes: Most critical: maintain moisture from R3 (pod beginning) to R6 (seed fill). Missing irrigation here causes 30–50% yield loss.

diseases.name: Soybean Rust
diseases.symptoms: Yellow-orange pustules mainly on leaf undersides; premature defoliation; significant yield loss.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, warm temperatures (17–27°C), prolonged leaf wetness.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Resistant varieties. Crop rotation. Remove infected debris.
diseases.treatment: Tebuconazole (1 ml/L) or Trifloxystrobin + Tebuconazole every 14 days.

diseases.name: Bacterial Blight
diseases.symptoms: Angular water-soaked lesions on leaves; lesion centres dry and fall out; stem lesions.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Cool wet weather, overhead irrigation, rain splash.
diseases.prevention: Use disease-free certified seeds. Avoid overhead irrigation. Crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L) spray.

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Yellowing, wilting, and death of plants; brown decayed roots.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged soil, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained soil. Raised beds. Avoid waterlogging.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl drench (1.5 g/L) at first symptoms.

pests.name: Pod Borer
pests.damage: Larvae feed inside pods reducing seed quality and yield.
pests.identification: Entry holes in pods; frass visible; seeds damaged inside.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Regular scouting at flowering.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) at pod fill stage.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; may transmit soybean mosaic virus causing stunted growth.
pests.identification: Colonies on growing tips and undersides of leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) if population exceeds threshold.

risks.name: Drought at Pod Fill
risks.description: Most critical yield risk. 30–50% yield reduction.
risks.signs: Pods feel light and flat, seeds shrivelled.
risks.mitigation: Irrigate every 7–10 days during R3–R6. Do not miss.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Kills plants within 48 hours.
risks.mitigation: Raised beds in wetter areas. Clear drainage channels.

harvest.method: Cut plants at soil level. Bundle and sun-dry 3–5 days. Thresh by beating or machine.
harvest.post_harvest: Sun dry to 12% moisture. Winnow. Store in sealed bags in cool dry place.

Soybean.growth_stages_text: Soybean cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, flowering, pod formation, and seed filling. Germination typically occurs within 4–8 days after sowing, followed by rapid seedling establishment and vegetative growth for about 20–40 days. Flowering usually begins around 35–50 days after planting, followed by pod formation and seed development, which continues until maturity. Harvesting is generally carried out within 90–120 days when pods turn brown and seeds become hard and dry. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 30–45 cm between plants and 45–60 cm between rows to ensure proper plant development, air circulation, and efficient nutrient use.
Soybean.management_text: Successful soybean cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer management is crucial, especially phosphorus and potassium, to support root development, flowering, and pod filling, while nitrogen requirements are largely met through biological nitrogen fixation. Rhizobium inoculation is commonly used to enhance nitrogen fixation and improve yield. Irrigation is most important during flowering and pod filling stages, while excessive water should be avoided to prevent root diseases.
Soybean.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in soybean cultivation. Aphids feed on plant sap and may transmit viral diseases, reducing plant vigor. Pod borers directly damage developing pods and reduce seed quality. Soybean rust causes yellow-orange pustules on leaves and can lead to premature defoliation, significantly reducing yield. Bacterial blight causes dark lesions on leaves and stems, while root rot occurs in poorly drained soils and leads to plant death. Fungal leaf spot diseases also reduce photosynthetic efficiency. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, use of resistant varieties, proper field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, timely sowing, and integrated pest management practices. Regular field monitoring and early intervention are essential to minimize losses and ensure stable production.

Pigeon Pea.overview: Pigeon pea, commonly known as “Kandula” in Sri Lanka, is a drought-tolerant leguminous crop belonging to the Fabaceae family and scientifically known as Cajanus cajan. It is mainly cultivated in the dry and intermediate zones of Sri Lanka due to its ability to grow under low rainfall conditions and poor soil fertility. Pigeon pea grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 35°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.0 to 7.0. The crop requires moderate rainfall, full sunlight exposure, and minimal irrigation once established, making it suitable for rainfed farming systems. Pigeon pea is an important source of plant protein and is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine in curries and traditional dishes. In addition to its food value, the crop improves soil fertility through nitrogen fixation and is often used in mixed cropping systems. Depending on the variety, pigeon pea generally takes about 120 to 180 days to mature and produce harvestable pods. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include pod borers, aphids, wilt disease, leaf spot diseases, and fungal infections, which can reduce pod yield and seed quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Minimal preparation — deep taproots handle difficult soils.

activities.title: Plough once and clear weeds
activities.description: Single plough 20 cm. Remove previous crop debris.
activities.why: Pigeon pea is tolerant but good start reduces early competition.

activities.title: Apply minimal basal fertiliser
activities.description: TSP 75 kg/ha incorporated. No nitrogen.
activities.why: Phosphorus helps early root development; no nitrogen — pigeon pea fixes its own.

activities.title: Sow 3 seeds per hole at 4 cm depth
activities.description: Space 60×90 cm. Sow at start of rain season.
activities.why: Pigeon pea benefits from early rains for fast establishment.

activities.title: Thin to 2 plants per hole
activities.description: Remove weakest seedling.
activities.why: 2 plants per hole improves stability and yield.

stages.name: Vegetative Growth
stages.description: Tall bushy plant develops over several months.

activities.title: Weed twice in first 6 weeks
activities.description: Weed at 3 and 6 WAP. Pigeon pea smothers weeds after.
activities.why: Weed competition in first 6 weeks is the main yield limiter.

activities.title: One top dressing
activities.description: MOP 50 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Potassium for stem strength and later pod filling.

stages.name: Flowering & Pod Fill
stages.description: Long fruiting period. Different plants mature at different times.

activities.title: Check for pod borer
activities.description: Inspect pods for entry holes and frass from day 75.
activities.why: Pod borer is the primary pest of pigeon pea.

activities.title: Supplemental irrigation if very dry
activities.description: One or two irrigations during pod fill if rainfall fails.
activities.why: Even drought-tolerant pigeon pea needs some moisture at pod fill.

activities.title: Begin picking mature pods
activities.description: Pick dark-coloured mature pods. Green pods for vegetable use at 100–120 days.
activities.why: Staggered harvest maximises total yield.

activities.title: Continue picking
activities.description: 2–4 picks over 3–4 weeks.
activities.why: All pods do not mature simultaneously.

fertilization.why: Potassium for stem and pod development.

irrigation.frequency: Rainfed primarily. 1–2 supplemental irrigations if needed.
irrigation.notes: Pigeon pea is one of the most drought-tolerant crops. Deep taproot accesses subsoil moisture. Avoid waterlogging at all costs.

diseases.name: Fusarium Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Sudden wilting and plant death; vascular browning in stem; yellowing starts from lower leaves.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Warm soil, poor drainage, infected soil, susceptible variety.
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Crop rotation. Well-drained soil. Avoid monoculture.
diseases.treatment: No effective chemical cure. Soil drench with Carbendazim (1 g/L) at first symptoms.

diseases.name: Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Brown circular spots with grey centres on leaves; defoliation under severe attack.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, dense canopy, overhead irrigation.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) spray.

pests.name: Pod Borer
pests.damage: Most destructive pest; larvae feed inside pods destroying developing seeds.
pests.identification: Circular entry holes on pods; frass and damaged seeds inside.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps for monitoring. Regular field scouting at flowering.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Emamectin benzoate (0.4 g/L) at pod fill stage.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from young growth; may transmit viral diseases causing stunted growth.
pests.identification: Colonies on shoot tips and undersides of young leaves.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Natural enemies (ladybirds).
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) if population builds up.

risks.name: Extended Drought at Pod Fill
risks.description: Reduces seed fill. Pigeon pea usually survives but yield drops.
risks.mitigation: Supplemental irrigation 1–2 times at pod fill.

risks.name: Fusarium Wilt
risks.description: Can kill 30–50% of plants in infected fields.
risks.mitigation: 3-year rotation. Never replant in affected area.

indicators.green: Pods full and plump, light green — 100–120 days
indicators.dry: Pods turn brown and hard — 150–180 days

harvest.method: Hand pick mature pods. 3–4 pickings over 3–4 weeks.
harvest.post_harvest: Sun dry grain. Thresh and winnow. Store in sealed bags with neem leaves.

Pigeon Pea.growth_stages_text: Pigeon pea cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, flowering, pod formation, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after sowing, followed by slow but steady vegetative growth for about 30–60 days depending on soil fertility and environmental conditions. Flowering typically begins around 60–90 days after planting, followed by pod development and seed filling. Harvesting is usually carried out between 120–180 days after planting when pods turn brown and seeds become fully mature and hard. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 60–90 cm between plants and 90–120 cm between rows to ensure proper branching, sunlight penetration, and air circulation, which are important for reducing disease pressure and improving yield.
Pigeon Pea.management_text: Successful pigeon pea cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer management is relatively low due to the crop’s nitrogen-fixing ability, but phosphorus and potassium are important for root development, flowering, and pod formation. Organic manure is often used to improve soil structure and long-term fertility. Irrigation is most critical during germination and early vegetative stages, while mature plants are highly drought tolerant and require minimal watering.
Pigeon Pea.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are important in pigeon pea cultivation. Pod borers are one of the most damaging pests, feeding inside pods and directly reducing seed yield and quality. Aphids suck plant sap and may also transmit viral diseases, leading to stunted growth and reduced vigor. Wilt disease causes sudden plant collapse due to vascular blockage, while leaf spot diseases reduce photosynthesis and plant health. Fungal infections may also occur under humid conditions, affecting leaves, stems, and pods. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, use of resistant varieties, field sanitation, timely sowing, balanced fertilizer use, and integrated pest management practices. Regular monitoring and early intervention help minimize losses and ensure stable production.

Winged Bean (Dambala).overview: Winged bean, commonly known in Sri Lanka as “Dambala”, is a tropical leguminous vegetable crop belonging to the Fabaceae family and scientifically known as Psophocarpus tetragonolobus. It is widely grown in home gardens and small-scale farms across Sri Lanka, especially in wet and intermediate zones, due to its high nutritional value and adaptability to warm, humid conditions. Winged bean grows best in temperatures between 22°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained fertile loamy soils rich in organic matter with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0. The crop requires moderate rainfall, regular watering during dry periods, and full sunlight exposure for proper vine growth and pod development. A unique feature of winged bean is that almost all parts of the plant—tubers, leaves, flowers, and pods are edible and highly rich in protein, vitamins, and minerals, making it an important traditional food source. The crop generally starts producing edible pods within 90 to 120 days after planting depending on conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, bean flies, pod borers, leaf spot diseases, and fungal infections, which can reduce yield and plant health if not properly managed.

duration.note: days after planting to first pod harvest

activities.title: Dig pits and install trellis
activities.description: 45×45×45 cm pits with compost mix. Erect 2 m trellis — winged bean vines are heavy.
activities.why: Strong trellis is critical — weak support causes vine collapse.

activities.title: Apply compost to pits
activities.description: 3–5 kg compost per pit.
activities.why: Rich organic matter for the long-duration perennial vine.

activities.title: Sow 2 seeds per pit at 3 cm depth
activities.description: Water immediately. Keep moist.
activities.why: Winged bean seeds can be slow to germinate — 10–14 days is normal.

activities.title: Thin to 1 strong plant
activities.description: Remove weaker seedling.
activities.why: One vigorous plant per pit is more productive than two competing.

activities.title: Guide main stem onto trellis
activities.description: Tie loosely to trellis. Direct lateral shoots outward.
activities.why: Early training prevents tangled mass that reduces air circulation.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha ring + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Moderate nitrogen — winged bean fixes some of its own nitrogen.

activities.title: Weed around base
activities.description: Keep base weed-free. Mulch with dry grass.
activities.why: Mulch retains moisture and suppresses weeds for this long-duration crop.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha.
activities.why: Sustained nutrition for continuous pod production.

activities.title: Check for pod borers and bean fly
activities.description: Inspect pods and stems daily.
activities.why: Early management maintains continuous production.

activities.title: Regular irrigation
activities.description: Water every 3–4 days. Critical for pod development.
activities.why: Consistent moisture keeps plant producing pods continuously.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Regular pod harvest encourages continuous production.

activities.title: Begin regular pod harvest
activities.description: Pick young tender pods when 10–15 cm, still flat.
activities.why: Young pods are tender and have best eating quality.

activities.title: Harvest every 3–4 days
activities.description: Remove all mature pods. Leaving mature pods stops new pod formation.
activities.why: Regular picking extends productive season to 6–12 months.

fertilization.why: Rich pit preparation for long-duration crop.

irrigation.notes: Winged bean needs consistent moisture throughout. Never allow prolonged dry spells during flowering.

diseases.name: Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Brown or dark lesions on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and plant health.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, poor air circulation, overhead irrigation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Trellis. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L).

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.cause: Ralstonia solanacearum — soil-borne bacterial pathogen that survives in soil for many years.
diseases.symptoms: Sudden wilting of the whole plant even when soil moisture is adequate. Cutting the stem base and suspending in water shows bacterial ooze streaming out.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Poorly drained soils, fields previously used for solanaceous crops (tomato, brinjal, potato), warm and humid conditions.
diseases.prevention: Improve field drainage, rotate crops — avoid planting after tomato, brinjal, or potato for at least 2-3 seasons. Remove and destroy infected plants with the surrounding soil.
diseases.treatment: No reliable chemical cure. Remove infected plants immediately with surrounding soil and sanitize the area. Do not compost infected material.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.cause: Fungal infection favoured by humid conditions with poor airflow between plants.
diseases.symptoms: White powdery patches on leaves and young stems; affected leaves yellow and dry. Reduced flowering and pod set in severe cases.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, poor plant spacing, overcrowding, overhead irrigation in the evening.
diseases.prevention: Provide proper plant spacing for good airflow, prune dense growth, avoid overhead irrigation late in the day.
diseases.treatment: Remove and destroy infected leaves. Apply a sulfur-based or DOA-recommended fungicide according to label instructions at first sign of infection.

pests.name: Bean Fly
pests.damage: Larvae mine stems causing death of young plants.
pests.identification: Wilting seedlings with maggots visible in stem cross-section.
pests.prevention: Early planting. Seed dressing. Good field sanitation.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos soil drench (2 ml/L) at planting.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from new growth; reduces plant vigour.
pests.identification: Soft-bodied insects on shoot tips and young leaves.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Yellow sticky traps.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) if heavy infestation.

pests.name: Pod Borer
pests.damage: Internal damage to pods; reduces seed quality.
pests.identification: Entry holes on pods; frass visible.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Monitor at pod initiation.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) at first pod stage.

risks.name: Drought
risks.description: Winged bean needs more water than other legumes. Flower drop without moisture.
risks.mitigation: Regular drip or furrow irrigation. Heavy mulching.

risks.name: Weak Trellis
risks.description: Heavy mature vines collapse, breaking stems and losing entire crop.
risks.mitigation: Use hardwood or concrete poles. Inspect trellis before rainy season.

indicators: Pods 10–15 cm long with prominent wings
Pods still flat — seeds not bulging
harvest.method: Hand pick individual pods. Cut with scissors to avoid stem damage.
harvest.post_harvest: Store cool. Market within 2–3 days. Young leaves can also be picked for cooking.

Winged Bean (Dambala).growth_stages_text: Winged bean cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative vine growth, flowering, pod formation, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 6–10 days after sowing, while vegetative growth continues for about 30–50 days depending on soil fertility and environmental conditions. Flowering typically begins around 60–80 days after planting, followed by pod development and tuber formation in some varieties. Harvesting of young pods generally starts within 90–120 days when pods are tender, green, and not fibrous. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 1–1.5 meters between plants to support vigorous vine growth, sunlight exposure, and air circulation. Trellis systems or staking are often used to improve pod quality, reduce disease incidence, and make harvesting easier.
Winged Bean (Dambala).management_text: Successful winged bean cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, vine training, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using phosphorus-rich fertilizers along with nitrogen, potassium, and organic compost to enhance vine development and pod production. Irrigation is especially important during germination, flowering, and pod development stages, while mature plants can tolerate short dry periods once well established. Harvesting indicators include tender green pods, good pod length, and soft texture suitable for consumption.
Winged Bean (Dambala).disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in successful winged bean cultivation. Aphids suck plant sap, causing curling, yellowing, and reduced plant vigor, while bean flies damage seedlings and weaken early plant establishment. Pod borers feed inside developing pods, reducing seed and pod quality, and leaf spot diseases appear as brown or dark lesions that reduce photosynthesis and plant health. Fungal infections may also occur under high humidity conditions, affecting vines and leaves. These problems can be controlled through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plant parts, trellis support systems, biological pest control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, maintaining soil fertility, use of healthy seeds, and integrated pest management techniques help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Maize.overview: Maize, commonly known as “Corn” in Sri Lanka, is an important cereal crop belonging to the Poaceae family and scientifically known as Zea mays. It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones such as Anuradhapura, Polonnaruwa, and Monaragala due to favorable warm climates and irrigation support. Maize grows best in temperatures between 21°C and 32°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy or sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0. The crop needs moderate rainfall, good sunlight exposure, and proper soil moisture during flowering and grain filling stages for high yield. Maize is an important food and industrial crop in Sri Lanka, used for human consumption, animal feed, and food processing industries, and is valued for its high carbohydrate and energy content. The crop generally takes about 90 to 120 days to mature depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include fall armyworm, stem borers, aphids, leaf blight, rust, and bacterial wilt, which can significantly reduce yield and grain quality if not properly managed.
Maize.propagation: Direct sowing (2 seeds per hole, thin to 1)

activities.title: Deep plough to 25 cm
activities.description: Break clods, level field, form rows.
activities.why: Maize has a deep root system that benefits from deep tillage.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 5–8 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Organic matter improves moisture retention in dry-zone soils.

activities.title: Sow 2 seeds per hole at 3–4 cm
activities.description: Row spacing 75 cm, spacing 25 cm. Aim for 50,000–60,000 plants/ha.
activities.why: Correct plant population is critical — too sparse or dense both reduce yield.

activities.title: Thin to 1 plant per hole
activities.description: Remove weaker seedling at 7–10 days.
activities.why: One strong plant per position is more productive than two competing.

activities.title: Irrigate if no rain
activities.description: Water every 5–7 days in first 3 weeks if dry.
activities.why: Uniform germination needs consistent moisture.

stages.name: Vegetative Growth
stages.description: Rapid tall stem growth — build leaf area.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Hoe between rows. Critical weed-free period.
activities.why: Weeds at this stage compete heavily — one weedy month can reduce yield 40%.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 100 kg/ha between rows. Side dress and hill up.
activities.why: High nitrogen demand during vegetative phase.

activities.title: Begin FAW scouting
activities.description: Check whorls of young plants for frass and feeding damage.
activities.why: Fall Armyworm hides in the whorl — must check inside plant.

activities.title: Second weeding and hill up
activities.description: Hill up soil 15 cm around plant base.
activities.why: Earthing up prevents lodging (plants falling over) and supports root development.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Final nitrogen push before cob fill.

activities.title: Ensure adequate moisture
activities.description: Irrigate every 5–7 days. Critical at silking.
activities.why: Water stress at tasseling/silking causes poor pollination and blank cobs.

activities.title: Check for stem borer
activities.description: Look for dead hearts (central dead leaf) or entry holes in stems.
activities.why: Stem borer at this stage bores into developing cob stalk.

activities.title: Maintain moisture
activities.description: Irrigate every 7 days during grain fill.
activities.why: Water stress during grain fill causes kernel abortion and reduces yield.

activities.title: Bird scaring
activities.description: Post reflective tape or place scarecrows. Patrol morning and evening.
activities.why: Birds and squirrels cause 10–20% grain loss at this stage.

activities.title: Check for harvest readiness
activities.description: Husk a cob — kernels should be hard and dented at crown.
activities.why: Soft kernels = too early. Dented hard kernels = ready for dry grain harvest.

activities.title: Harvest and dry
activities.description: Pull cobs, remove husks, sun dry.
activities.why: Sun drying reduces moisture to safe storage levels.

irrigation.notes: Most critical: NEVER stress maize at tasseling/silking — this is the single most yield-determining water timing.

diseases.name: Leaf Blight
diseases.symptoms: Large tan-grey elongated lesions on leaves; severe defoliation reduces yield.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, moderate temperatures, dense canopy.
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Crop rotation. Good spacing.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Propiconazole (1 ml/L).

diseases.name: Rust
diseases.symptoms: Reddish-brown pustules on leaf surfaces; reduces photosynthesis and grain fill.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid cooler conditions, dense planting.
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Good spacing.
diseases.treatment: Tebuconazole (1 ml/L) or Mancozeb (2.5 g/L).

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt / Stewart's Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Pale green-yellow leaf streaks; wilting and death of young plants in severe cases.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High flea beetle populations; warm humid conditions.
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Control flea beetle at seedling stage.
diseases.treatment: Flea beetle control with Chlorpyrifos.

pests.name: Fall Armyworm
pests.damage: Most destructive maize pest in Sri Lanka; feeds on whorl leaves and ears; severe yield loss.
pests.identification: Ragged windowpane damage on whorl leaves; frass and bored entry on ears.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps for early detection. Regular field scouting. Act immediately.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Emamectin benzoate (0.4 g/L) applied into whorl urgently.

pests.name: Stem Borer
pests.damage: Bores into stems causing dead heart; disrupts nutrient transport to ear.
pests.identification: Entry holes in stems; frass; wilted central shoot in young plants.
pests.prevention: Field sanitation. Trichogramma release at egg stage.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) spray. Apply into whorl at 2nd-3rd instar.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Forms dense colonies on tassels and upper leaves; reduces pollination and grain fill.
pests.identification: Dense yellow-green colonies on upper plant; sticky honeydew.
pests.prevention: Encourage natural enemies. Avoid excess nitrogen.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) or Dimethoate (1.5 ml/L) at threshold.

risks.name: Fall Armyworm Outbreak
risks.description: Can destroy entire crop if not controlled within 72 hours of detection.
risks.signs: Frass in whorls, ragged leaves, rapid defoliation.
risks.mitigation: Scout daily from day 14. Apply insecticide into whorl IMMEDIATELY at first sign.

risks.name: Drought at Tasseling
risks.description: Poor pollination causes blank cobs with no grain.
risks.signs: Brown silks before pollen shed. Empty cob at harvest.
risks.mitigation: Irrigate every 5–7 days. Never miss during this 2-week window.

indicators.grain: Kernels dented and hard, black layer visible at base of kernel
indicators.fresh: Silks brown, kernels milky when punctured

harvest.method: Pull cob downward and twist off. Remove husk. Spread to sun-dry.
harvest.frequency: Single harvest for dry grain; multiple for fresh corn
harvest.post_harvest: Sun dry 5–7 days to 13% moisture. Shell. Store in sealed bags or metal drums. Use phosphine tablets if storing more than 3 months.

Maize.growth_stages_text: Maize cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, tasseling, silking, pollination, grain filling, physiological maturity, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 4–8 days after sowing under suitable soil moisture and temperature conditions. During the seedling stage, roots and leaves develop rapidly, establishing a strong foundation for future growth. The vegetative stage lasts approximately 30–50 days, during which plants increase in height and leaf area. Tasseling marks the emergence of the male flowers at the top of the plant, while silking occurs when the female flower structures emerge from the ears. Successful pollination between tassels and silks is essential for complete kernel formation. Grain filling follows pollination as starch accumulates within developing kernels. The crop reaches physiological maturity when kernels become hard and a black layer forms at the base of the grain. Harvesting is generally carried out within 90–120 days after planting when grain moisture has decreased to suitable levels.
Maize.management_text: Successful maize cultivation requires a structured crop management schedule including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation management, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 20–30 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure adequate sunlight exposure, nutrient availability, and air circulation. Fertilizer management is critical because maize is a heavy nutrient feeder. Nitrogen is required for vigorous vegetative growth and leaf development, phosphorus promotes strong root growth and early establishment, while potassium improves grain filling, plant strength, and resistance to environmental stress. Organic manure or compost is often incorporated during land preparation to improve soil fertility and water retention.
Maize.disease_management_text: Irrigation management plays a significant role in achieving high maize yields. Adequate moisture is essential during germination to ensure uniform plant establishment. During vegetative growth, regular irrigation supports healthy leaf and stem development. Water demand becomes highest during tasseling, silking, pollination, and grain filling stages. Moisture stress during these critical periods can lead to poor pollination, incomplete kernel formation, and substantial yield reduction. While maize requires consistent soil moisture, excessive irrigation and waterlogging should be avoided because they can damage roots and increase the risk of disease outbreaks.
Maize.risk_mitigation_text: Disease and pest management are essential components of maize production. Fall armyworm is one of the most destructive pests affecting maize in Sri Lanka, feeding on leaves, whorls, tassels, and ears, causing severe yield losses. Stem borers tunnel into stems and weaken plant structure, reducing nutrient transport. Aphids suck plant sap and may transmit viral diseases. Leaf blight appears as elongated lesions on leaves that reduce photosynthesis and overall plant vigor. Rust disease causes reddish-brown pustules on leaves, leading to reduced growth and grain production. Bacterial wilt results in yellowing, wilting, and eventual death of infected plants. These pests and diseases can be controlled through crop rotation, use of resistant varieties, field sanitation, timely planting, balanced fertilizer application, biological control measures, and integrated pest management practices. Risk mitigation strategies are important for maintaining stable maize production and minimizing losses caused by environmental stresses. During drought periods, mulching, conservation tillage, and efficient irrigation systems help conserve soil moisture and reduce water stress. During periods of heavy rainfall, proper drainage systems prevent waterlogging and root damage. Regular field scouting enables early detection of pests and diseases, allowing timely intervention before major damage occurs. The use of certified seeds, proper plant spacing, balanced nutrient management, and crop rotation improves crop health and resilience. These practices help ensure consistent grain yields, better grain quality, and greater adaptability to changing climatic conditions and environmental challenges.

Sweet Corn.overview: Sweet corn, commonly known as “Sweet corn” in Sri Lanka, is a high-value cereal vegetable crop belonging to the Poaceae family and scientifically known as Zea mays saccharata. It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones as well as some irrigated farming areas, due to its fast growth and strong market demand. Sweet corn grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy or sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy plant growth, proper cob formation, and good kernel development. Sweet corn is widely consumed in Sri Lanka as a boiled snack, roasted food, and ingredient in salads and mixed dishes, and it is valued for its sweetness, fiber, and nutritional content. The crop generally takes about 65 to 100 days to mature depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include fall armyworm, stem borers, aphids, cutworms, leaf blight, rust, and bacterial wilt, which can significantly reduce yield and cob quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough 25 cm deep and fine till
activities.description: Level well. Sweet corn requires uniform growth for simultaneous harvest.
activities.why: Uniform field preparation = uniform plant population = single-time harvest.

activities.title: Sow 2 seeds per hole, 25×60 cm
activities.description: Cover 3–4 cm deep. Water.
activities.why: Higher population than grain maize for sweet corn.

activities.title: Thin to 1 plant
activities.description: Remove weaker seedling.
activities.why: Single strong plant per position.

activities.title: Weed and hill up
activities.description: Hoe between rows and heap soil around base.
activities.why: Hilling prevents lodging.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 100 kg/ha side dress.
activities.why: High nitrogen for sweet corn quality.

activities.title: Scout for Fall Armyworm
activities.description: Check whorls for frass.
activities.why: FAW management same as maize — critical early.

activities.title: Irrigate every 4–5 days
activities.description: Critical moisture at silking.
activities.why: Silk drying out = poor pollination = missing kernels.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Final nitrogen for sweet kernel development.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest at milky stage — timing is critical.

activities.title: Check for milky stage
activities.description: Pierce a kernel with thumbnail — milky liquid = harvest now.
activities.why: Sweet corn quality window is only 3–5 days. Miss it = starchy cobs.

activities.title: Harvest entire field within 3–5 days
activities.description: Stagger planting by 2 weeks for continuous supply.
activities.why: Once at milky stage, all cobs mature quickly.

irrigation.notes: Sweet corn has LESS drought tolerance than grain maize. Never miss irrigation at silking.

diseases.name: Leaf Blight
diseases.symptoms: Elongated tan/brown lesions on leaves; reduces photosynthesis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, warm weather, dense planting.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Resistant varieties. Crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Propiconazole (1 ml/L) spray.

diseases.name: Rust
diseases.symptoms: Reddish-brown pustules on leaf surfaces; defoliation under severe attack.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid weather, cool nights, moderate temperatures.
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Good plant spacing.
diseases.treatment: Tebuconazole (1 ml/L) or Mancozeb (2.5 g/L).

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt / Stewart's Wilt
diseases.symptoms: Pale green to yellow streaks on leaves; plants wilt and die in severe cases.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High flea beetle population; warm wet conditions.
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Flea beetle control at seedling stage.
diseases.treatment: Control flea beetle vector with Chlorpyrifos. No direct chemical cure.

pests.name: Fall Armyworm
pests.damage: Most destructive pest of maize and sweet corn; feeds on whorl leaves and developing cobs causing severe yield loss.
pests.identification: Ragged windowpane damage on whorl leaves; frass and entry holes on cobs.
pests.prevention: Regular monitoring from seedling stage. Pheromone traps. Early intervention.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Emamectin benzoate (0.4 g/L) into whorl. Act within 1–2 days of detection.

pests.name: Stem Borer
pests.damage: Larvae tunnel into stems causing dead heart and weakening plants; reduces nutrient flow to cob.
pests.identification: Entry holes in stems with frass; wilted growing point in young plants.
pests.prevention: Field sanitation. Release of Trichogramma egg parasitoids.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) at early infestation. Apply into whorl.

pests.name: Cutworm
pests.damage: Cuts young seedling stems at soil level overnight.
pests.identification: Collapsed seedlings; fat caterpillar in soil near base.
pests.prevention: Deep ploughing before planting to expose larvae.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules in soil at planting.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from leaves and tassels; reduces pollination and grain fill.
pests.identification: Dense colonies on leaves and tassels.
pests.prevention: Natural enemies. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) if severe.

risks.name: Fall Armyworm
risks.description: Destroys crop rapidly if not controlled within 48–72 hours.
risks.mitigation: Scout daily. Act immediately.

risks.name: Missing Harvest Window
risks.description: Over-mature sweet corn becomes starchy and worthless.
risks.signs: Hard starchy kernels. No milky liquid.
risks.mitigation: Check daily from day 63. Harvest entire block within 3–5 days of milky stage.

indicators: Cob feels plump and firm when squeezed through husk
Milky liquid spurts from kernel when pressed with thumbnail
harvest.method: Snap cob downward and twist off. Keep husks on for freshness.
harvest.frequency: Harvest all cobs within 3–5 days of reaching milky stage
harvest.post_harvest: Keep in husk. Store cool. Market within 24–48 hours. Sugars convert to starch rapidly at ambient temperature.

Sweet Corn.growth_stages_text: Sweet corn cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, tasseling, silking, pollination, cob development, grain filling, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 4–7 days after sowing under favorable moisture and temperature conditions. During the seedling stage, plants establish their root systems and begin rapid leaf development. Vegetative growth continues for approximately 25–40 days, followed by tasseling, where the male flowers emerge at the top of the plant. Silking occurs shortly afterward when female flower structures emerge from developing cobs. Successful pollination between tassels and silks is essential for complete kernel formation. Following pollination, kernels develop and fill with sugars, giving sweet corn its characteristic sweetness. Harvesting is generally carried out 65–100 days after planting when cobs reach the milk stage and kernels are fully developed but still tender.
Sweet Corn.management_text: Successful sweet corn cultivation requires a well-planned crop management schedule including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation management, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods with spacing of approximately 20–30 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to allow adequate sunlight penetration and air circulation. Fertilizer management is important because sweet corn has relatively high nutrient requirements. Nitrogen promotes vigorous vegetative growth, phosphorus supports root development and early plant establishment, while potassium improves cob development, kernel quality, and plant resistance to environmental stress. Organic manure or compost is often incorporated during land preparation to improve soil fertility and water-holding capacity.
Sweet Corn.disease_management_text: Irrigation management is critical throughout the growing season. Adequate moisture is essential during germination to ensure uniform seed emergence. During vegetative growth, regular watering supports leaf and stem development. Water demand is highest during tasseling, silking, pollination, and cob development stages, where moisture stress can significantly reduce kernel formation and yield. Irrigation should be applied regularly while avoiding waterlogging, which can damage roots and encourage disease development. Mulching and efficient irrigation methods can help conserve soil moisture during dry periods.
Sweet Corn.risk_mitigation_text: Disease and pest management are essential for maintaining healthy crops and achieving high yields. Fall armyworm is one of the most destructive pests, feeding on leaves, whorls, and developing cobs, causing severe yield losses. Stem borers tunnel into stalks and weaken plants, while aphids suck plant sap and may transmit diseases. Cutworms attack young seedlings by cutting stems near the soil surface. Leaf blight appears as elongated brown lesions on leaves and can reduce photosynthesis. Rust disease causes reddish-brown pustules on leaves, weakening plant growth. Bacterial wilt causes yellowing, wilting, and eventual plant death. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, resistant varieties, balanced fertilizer application, proper field sanitation, biological control methods, and integrated pest management practices. Risk mitigation strategies play an important role in ensuring stable sweet corn production. During periods of drought, mulching and efficient irrigation systems help conserve soil moisture and reduce water stress. Proper field drainage is necessary during heavy rainfall to prevent root damage and disease outbreaks. Regular field scouting allows early detection of pest and disease problems so that corrective measures can be implemented promptly. The use of certified seeds, timely planting, proper plant spacing, crop rotation, and balanced nutrient management improves crop health and productivity. These practices help ensure consistent yields, high-quality cobs, and greater resilience to changing environmental and climatic conditions.

Sorghum.overview: Sorghum, commonly known in Sri Lanka as “Dura” or “Jowar”, is a drought-tolerant cereal crop belonging to the Poaceae family and scientifically known as Sorghum bicolor. It is mainly cultivated in the dry and intermediate zones of Sri Lanka due to its strong ability to withstand high temperatures and low rainfall conditions. Sorghum grows best in temperatures between 25°C and 35°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.5. The crop requires low rainfall, full sunlight exposure, and minimal irrigation, making it suitable for rainfed and marginal agricultural lands. Sorghum is an important cereal used for human consumption, animal feed, and traditional food preparations, and it is valued for its high energy content and nutritional benefits. The crop generally matures within 90 to 120 days after planting depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting sorghum cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include shoot fly, stem borer, aphids, grain mold, leaf blight, and rust diseases, which can reduce yield and grain quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Light preparation — sorghum tolerates difficult conditions.

activities.title: Plough once to 20 cm
activities.description: Remove weeds and level.
activities.why: Sorghum tolerates poor conditions but good start improves yield significantly.

activities.title: Sow 2–3 seeds per hole at 3–4 cm depth
activities.description: Spacing 20×60 cm.
activities.why: Sow at start of rain season for best establishment.

activities.title: Thin to 1–2 plants per hole
activities.description: Keep stronger seedlings.
activities.why: Reduces competition while maintaining good stand.

stages.name: Vegetative Growth
stages.description: Rapid growth with good nutrient uptake.

activities.title: Weed once at 2–3 WAP
activities.description: Critical weed-free period.
activities.why: Weed competition in first 4 weeks reduces yield by up to 50%.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Sorghum responds well to nitrogen even in poor soils.

activities.title: Scout for shoot fly and stem borer
activities.description: Check for dead hearts and stem entry holes.
activities.why: Stem borer at heading reduces grain fill significantly.

activities.title: Irrigate if possible at heading
activities.description: One irrigation at heading if no rain.
activities.why: Even drought-tolerant sorghum benefits from moisture at heading.

activities.title: Watch for grain mold
activities.description: Check heads for black/pink mold during wet weather.
activities.why: Grain mold is the biggest risk in humid harvesting conditions.

activities.title: Harvest when grain is hard
activities.description: Heads turn brown, grain hard and dry.
activities.why: Early harvest when grain is soft increases mold and storage loss.

activities.title: Thresh and dry
activities.description: Cut heads. Thresh by beating. Sun dry 3–5 days.
activities.why: Rapid drying prevents grain mold in storage.

fertilization.why: Base P and K at planting.
Nitrogen and potassium for growth and grain fill.

irrigation.frequency: Rainfed mainly; 1–2 supplemental irrigations if available
irrigation.notes: Sorghum has a unique ability to pause growth during drought and resume when rain returns. Best drought tolerance of all cereal crops.

diseases.name: Grain Mold
diseases.symptoms: Pink, grey, or black discolouration of grain at maturity; reduced grain quality and market value.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity during grain maturation, delayed harvest, wet conditions.
diseases.prevention: Early harvest when moisture suitable. Use resistant varieties. Crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: Thiram or Captan seed dressing. Manage harvest timing carefully.

diseases.name: Leaf Blight
diseases.symptoms: Large elongated tan lesions on leaves; reduces photosynthesis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, dense canopy, moderate temperatures.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Resistant varieties.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Propiconazole (1 ml/L) spray.

diseases.name: Rust
diseases.symptoms: Reddish-brown elongated pustules on both leaf surfaces; severe defoliation.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid weather, moderate temperatures (20–28°C).
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Good plant spacing.
diseases.treatment: Tebuconazole (1 ml/L) or Mancozeb (2.5 g/L).

pests.name: Shoot Fly
pests.damage: Attacks seedlings causing 'dead heart' symptom; reduces plant population.
pests.identification: Withered central shoot of young plant; maggot inside stem base.
pests.prevention: Early sowing. Resistant varieties. Field sanitation.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules at sowing or Carbosulfan seed dressing.

pests.name: Stem Borer
pests.damage: Tunnels into stalks; causes dead heart in vegetative stage and white ear at heading.
pests.identification: Entry holes in stems; frass visible; wilted growing point or empty panicle.
pests.prevention: Field sanitation. Remove and destroy infested plants. Egg parasitoid release.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) spray at infestation. Trichogramma release at egg stage.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Dense colonies on leaves and stem; causes yellowing and sooty mould.
pests.identification: Large dense colonies on upper stem and panicle; honey dew and sooty mould.
pests.prevention: Natural predators. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) or Dimethoate (1.5 ml/L) at threshold.

risks.name: Rain at Harvest (Grain Mold)
risks.description: Grain mold is the primary risk. Renders entire crop unsaleable.
risks.mitigation: Harvest immediately when ripe. Dry rapidly under shelter if rain threatens.

risks.name: Birds
risks.description: Birds consume ripening grain — can cause 30–40% loss in dry zone.
risks.signs: Grain missing from heads, bird flocks.
risks.mitigation: Post scarecrows. Patrol field morning and evening. Reflective tape on stakes.

indicators: Grain hard and cannot be dented with thumbnail
Head fully coloured (red-brown or white depending on variety)
harvest.method: Cut heads with sickle. Bundle. Sun dry 5–7 days on clean surface. Thresh by beating or roll on concrete.
harvest.post_harvest: Dry to 12% moisture. Winnow clean. Store in sealed metal or plastic containers. Sorghum is susceptible to weevils.

Sorghum.growth_stages_text: Sorghum cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, tillering, panicle initiation, flowering, grain filling, and harvesting. Germination typically occurs within 4–8 days after sowing, followed by seedling establishment and vigorous vegetative growth. Tillering occurs during the early growth period, allowing the plant to produce additional stems that contribute to grain production. Panicle initiation usually begins around 40–60 days after planting, followed by flowering and pollination. Grain filling takes place over the following weeks as nutrients accumulate within the developing grains. Harvesting is generally carried out within 90–120 days when the panicles become dry, grains harden, and moisture content decreases to a suitable level. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 15–25 cm between plants and 45–60 cm between rows to promote healthy growth and maximize grain yield.
Sorghum.management_text: Successful sorghum cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer requirements are moderate, with nitrogen supporting vegetative growth and phosphorus and potassium promoting root development, flowering, and grain formation. Organic manure is often incorporated during land preparation to improve soil fertility and water-holding capacity. Irrigation is most important during germination, flowering, and grain filling stages, although sorghum can tolerate drought better than most cereal crops due to its deep root system and efficient water-use characteristics.
Sorghum.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential for maintaining sorghum productivity. Shoot fly attacks young seedlings and causes dead-heart symptoms where the central shoot dries and dies, reducing plant population. Stem borers tunnel into stems, weakening plants and interfering with nutrient transport. Aphids suck plant sap, causing leaf curling and reduced growth. Grain mold develops under humid conditions during grain maturation and can significantly reduce grain quality and market value. Leaf blight causes elongated lesions on leaves that reduce photosynthetic activity, while rust diseases appear as reddish-brown pustules that weaken plants and lower yields. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, timely planting, field sanitation, resistant varieties, balanced fertilizer application, and integrated pest management practices. Risk mitigation strategies such as conserving soil moisture, maintaining proper plant spacing, monitoring pest populations regularly, and applying appropriate control measures when necessary help ensure stable production and improve resilience to environmental stresses.

Kurakkan.overview: Kurakkan, commonly known as finger millet in Sri Lanka, is a traditional cereal crop belonging to the Poaceae family and scientifically known as Eleusine coracana. It is widely cultivated in the dry and intermediate zones of Sri Lanka due to its high tolerance to drought and ability to grow in poor, marginal soils. Kurakkan grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.0 to 7.5. The crop requires low rainfall, full sunlight exposure, and minimal irrigation, making it highly suitable for rainfed agriculture. Kurakkan is an important staple grain in Sri Lankan traditional diets and is commonly used to make flour for porridge, roti, and other local food products, valued for its high fiber, calcium, and mineral content. The crop generally matures within 90 to 120 days after planting depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include shoot fly, stem borers, blast disease, leaf spots, and fungal infections, which can reduce grain yield and quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Minimal preparation — kurakkan grows in poor soils.

activities.title: Light plough and clear weeds
activities.description: Plough 15–20 cm. Remove weeds.
activities.why: Good weed control at start is the most important management step.

activities.title: Fine tilth for small seeds
activities.description: Rake to fine seedbed — kurakkan seeds are tiny.
activities.why: Very small seeds need good seed-soil contact for germination.

activities.title: Broadcast sow at 10–12 kg/ha or transplant
activities.description: Broadcast and rake in 1–2 cm, OR transplant 25-day nursery seedlings at 10×30 cm.
activities.why: Transplanting gives better establishment but more labour. Both methods work.

activities.title: One light watering if soil is dry
activities.description: Mist spray or gentle watering.
activities.why: Small seeds need surface moisture for germination.

activities.title: First weeding (critical)
activities.description: Hand weed or hoe carefully. Small plants are easily damaged.
activities.why: Weed competition at tillering stage reduces yield more than any other management factor.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Modest nutrition — kurakkan is adapted to low-fertility soils.

activities.title: Second weeding
activities.description: Final weeding before canopy closes.
activities.why: Two clean weedings are usually sufficient for the crop.

stages.name: Heading & Grain Fill
stages.description: Finger-like heads develop and fill with grain.

activities.title: Watch for blast disease
activities.description: Check leaves and heads for lesions — blast is the key disease of kurakkan.
activities.why: Blast can destroy 50–80% of grain in susceptible conditions.

activities.title: Bird protection
activities.description: Post scarecrows and patrol morning and evening.
activities.why: Birds are a serious problem at grain fill — 20–40% loss possible.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest heads when grain fully formed.

activities.title: Cut heads when grain hard
activities.description: Grain hard and brown. Cut heads with sickle.
activities.why: Harvesting heads only reduces labour compared to cutting whole plant.

activities.title: Sun dry and thresh
activities.description: Dry heads on clean mats 5–7 days. Thresh by beating or rolling.
activities.why: Proper drying prevents grain mold in storage.

irrigation.frequency: Rainfed primarily; 1–2 supplemental irrigations if available
water_stress_signs: Small heads with poor grain fill
irrigation.notes: Kurakkan is one of the most drought-tolerant crops in Sri Lanka. Grown successfully on rainfall of 600–1200 mm. Overwatering promotes blast disease.

diseases.name: Blast Disease
diseases.symptoms: Spindle-shaped lesions on leaves, necks, and ear heads; 'neck blast' causes ear head death and severe yield loss.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, warm temperatures (24–28°C), lush canopy, excess nitrogen.
diseases.prevention: Resistant varieties. Balanced fertilisation. Good plant spacing. Avoid excess nitrogen.
diseases.treatment: Tricyclazole (0.6 g/L) or Carbendazim (1 g/L) spray at boot stage and heading.

diseases.name: Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Brown lesions on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and grain quality.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, dense crop, cool wet conditions.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Resistant varieties. Remove infected material.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) preventive spray.

pests.name: Shoot Fly
pests.damage: Attacks young seedlings causing 'dead heart' — central shoot dies reducing plant stand.
pests.identification: Withered central shoot; maggot visible in stem base.
pests.prevention: Early sowing to avoid pest peak. Seed dressing.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules at sowing or Carbosulfan seed dressing.

pests.name: Stem Borer
pests.damage: Bores into stems; causes 'dead heart' in vegetative stage or 'white ear' at heading.
pests.identification: Entry holes in stems; frass near bore entry; wilted growing point.
pests.prevention: Field sanitation. Remove infested plants.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) spray at infestation.

risks.name: Blast (Neck Blast)
risks.description: White ears (empty heads) from neck blast can destroy 50–80% of grain.
risks.signs: White empty heads. Grey lesions on leaf necks.
risks.mitigation: Tricyclazole spray at booting and heading. Avoid dense planting.

risks.name: Birds
risks.description: Birds devastate ripening grain in dry zone.
risks.signs: Missing grain on heads, bird flocks in field.
risks.mitigation: Daily patrols. Scarecrows every 10 m.

indicators: Finger-like heads fully formed and grain doesn't dent when pressed
harvest.method: Cut heads with sickle. Spread on clean mat. Sun dry 5–7 days. Thresh by beating sacks against floor.
harvest.post_harvest: Dry to 12% moisture. Winnow. Store in sealed containers. Kurakkan stores very well — 1–2 years in cool dry conditions.

Kurakkan.growth_stages_text: Kurakkan cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, tillering, vegetative growth, flowering, grain filling, and harvesting.
