Germination usually occurs within 4–7 days after sowing under suitable soil moisture conditions. During the seedling and tillering stages, the crop develops multiple shoots that contribute to grain production. Vegetative growth continues for approximately 30–50 days, followed by flowering and pollination. Grain filling occurs after flowering as nutrients accumulate in the developing seed heads. Harvesting is generally carried out within 90–120 days after planting when the ear heads turn brown and the grains become hard and fully mature. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 10–15 cm between plants and 25–30 cm between rows to ensure proper growth and efficient use of sunlight and nutrients.
Kurakkan.management_text: Successful kurakkan cultivation requires a well-planned crop management schedule that includes land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation management, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting. The crop is often grown on upland fields where soil drainage is good. Organic manure or compost is commonly incorporated during land preparation to improve soil fertility and moisture retention. Fertilizer requirements are generally lower than many other cereal crops, but balanced application of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium can improve plant growth and grain yield. Weed control is particularly important during the first four to six weeks after planting, as young plants compete poorly with weeds.
Kurakkan.disease_management_text: Irrigation requirements for kurakkan are relatively low due to its drought tolerance. Adequate moisture is important during germination and early seedling establishment to ensure uniform crop growth. During vegetative growth, the crop can tolerate short dry periods, while moisture availability during flowering and grain filling stages helps improve grain development and yield. Excessive irrigation should be avoided because waterlogged conditions can negatively affect root growth and increase disease incidence. Mulching and soil conservation practices can help retain soil moisture during prolonged dry periods.
Kurakkan.risk_mitigation_text: Disease and pest management are essential for maintaining healthy crop growth and maximizing grain production. Shoot fly attacks young seedlings and causes dead-heart symptoms where the central shoot dries and dies. Stem borers tunnel into plant stems, weakening the plants and reducing nutrient transport. Blast disease is one of the most serious fungal diseases affecting finger millet and appears as spindle-shaped lesions on leaves, necks, and ear heads, often causing significant yield losses. Leaf spot diseases produce brown or dark lesions on leaves, reducing photosynthetic activity. Other fungal infections may occur under humid conditions and can affect overall crop health and grain quality. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, use of disease-resistant varieties, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer application, and integrated pest management practices. Risk mitigation strategies are important for ensuring stable production and reducing losses caused by environmental stresses. During drought conditions, moisture conservation techniques such as mulching and minimum tillage can help maintain soil moisture. Proper field drainage should be maintained during periods of excessive rainfall to prevent root damage and disease outbreaks. Regular field inspections allow farmers to detect pest and disease problems early and take corrective action before severe damage occurs. The use of certified seeds, timely planting, crop rotation, and proper nutrient management further improves crop health and productivity. These practices help ensure consistent grain yields, improved grain quality, and greater resilience to climate variability and challenging growing conditions.

Carrot.overview: Carrot, commonly known as “Gajar” in Sri Lanka, is a cool-climate root vegetable crop belonging to the Apiaceae family and scientifically known as Daucus carota. It is mainly cultivated in the up-country regions of Sri Lanka such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where low temperatures support proper root development and good color formation. Carrot grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 22°C and requires deep, loose, well-drained sandy loam soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy growth and uniform root formation. Carrots are highly nutritious and rich in beta-carotene, vitamins, and dietary fiber, making them an important vegetable in Sri Lankan diets, commonly used in curries, salads, and juices. The crop is generally harvested within 70 to 100 days after sowing depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting carrot cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include root knot nematodes, aphids, leaf blight, soft rot, and fungal infections, which can reduce yield and root quality if not properly managed.
Carrot.propagation: Direct sowing only (taproot cannot be transplanted)

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Deep fine tilth is essential for straight roots.

activities.title: Deep plough to 40 cm
activities.description: Remove all stones, clods, and hard pan. Carrot roots fork around obstacles.
activities.why: Any obstruction causes forked or misshapen roots — these are unsaleable.

activities.title: Apply compost, not fresh manure
activities.description: Incorporate well-composted material 10 t/ha. NEVER fresh manure.
activities.why: Fresh manure causes hairy, forked roots.

activities.title: Sow thinly in shallow furrows 1 cm deep
activities.description: Mix seeds with fine sand (1:10) for even distribution. Cover lightly.
activities.why: Carrot seeds are tiny — mixing with sand prevents clumping.

activities.title: Gentle watering after sowing
activities.description: Use fine rose or mist sprayer. Do not wash seeds.
activities.why: Heavy watering displaces the tiny seeds.

activities.title: Thin seedlings
activities.description: Thin to 5 cm spacing when 5–8 cm tall.
activities.why: Crowded carrots produce thin, poor-quality roots.

stages.name: Leaf & Root Growth
stages.description: Leaf canopy develops while root elongates.

activities.title: First weeding — very carefully
activities.description: Hand weed only. Hoe between rows with care.
activities.why: Carrot roots are shallow and easily damaged by hoeing.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Potassium is critical for root colour and sugar content.

activities.title: Maintain consistent moisture
activities.description: Water every 3–4 days. Even moisture prevents cracking.
activities.why: Alternating wet-dry cycles cause cracking and splitting of roots.

activities.title: Check root shoulder
activities.description: Scrape soil at base — root diameter 1–2 cm at shoulder indicates approaching maturity.
activities.why: Checking root size avoids both early harvest and over-mature woody roots.

activities.title: Reduce watering frequency
activities.description: Water every 5–6 days.
activities.why: Reducing water slightly concentrates sugars and improves flavour.

activities.title: Pull-test a few roots
activities.description: Pull test carrots — roots should pull cleanly with orange colour through to centre.
activities.why: Pale centre = immature. Woody = over-mature.

activities.title: Harvest in cool morning
activities.description: Pull or dig in early morning. Wash immediately.
activities.why: Cool morning harvest extends shelf life.

fertilization.why: All basal for direct-sown root crop.
Root development nutrition — high potassium for colour and sweetness.

irrigation.notes: Absolutely even moisture is the key management factor. Drought followed by irrigation causes cracking.

diseases.name: Leaf Blight
diseases.symptoms: Dark brown irregular spots on leaf margins and surface; leaves yellow and dry.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, wet conditions, dense canopy.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Remove infected foliage. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Iprodione (1.5 ml/L) every 10 days.

diseases.name: Soft Rot
diseases.symptoms: Soft watery decay of roots; foul smell; internal breakdown.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Wounds during harvest, high moisture, warm conditions.
diseases.prevention: Careful handling during harvest. Proper drainage. Avoid injury to roots.
diseases.treatment: No effective chemical. Improve harvest handling and storage conditions.

pests.name: Root Knot Nematode
pests.damage: Forms galls (knots) on roots causing forked, deformed, unmarketable roots.
pests.identification: Swollen knobby galls on root surface; stunted growth.
pests.prevention: Crop rotation (avoid carrots for 3 years). Deep ploughing. Marigold intercrop.
pests.treatment: Carbofuran granules (5 g/m²) at planting or Neem cake application.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from foliage; causes leaf curling and weakening.
pests.identification: Colonies on young leaves and shoot tips.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Yellow sticky traps.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) if infestation is high.

risks.name: Stones and Clods
risks.description: Forked and misshapen roots cannot be sold. 100% loss in affected areas.
risks.mitigation: Deep ploughing and stone removal before sowing. Non-negotiable.

risks.name: Frost (Nuwara Eliya)
risks.description: Kills young seedlings and damages roots.
risks.mitigation: Cover with frost cloth on cold nights. Harvest before frost season.

indicators: Root pulls out with firm texture — not soft or woody
harvest.method: Loosen soil with fork beside row. Pull plant by leaves. Trim tops leaving 2 cm.
harvest.post_harvest: Wash, trim tops to 2 cm, grade by size. Store in cool ventilated area. Shelf life 2–3 weeks.

Carrot.growth_stages_text: Carrot cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, root enlargement, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 7–14 days after sowing, followed by slow early seedling growth due to the development of the taproot system. Vegetative growth continues for about 25–40 days depending on environmental conditions, while root enlargement begins around 40–50 days after sowing and continues until harvest. Harvesting is typically carried out between 70–100 days when roots reach the desired size, color, and tenderness. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 5–10 cm between plants and 25–40 cm between rows to ensure proper root expansion, soil aeration, and uniform development.
Carrot.management_text: Successful carrot cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, thinning, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during early vegetative growth and root development stages, commonly using phosphorus and potassium-rich fertilizers along with organic compost to support strong root formation and improve color quality. Excess nitrogen is avoided as it may cause excessive leaf growth instead of root development. Irrigation frequency is highest during germination and root enlargement stages to maintain uniform soil moisture, while moderate irrigation is maintained later to prevent root cracking.
Carrot.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in carrot cultivation. Root knot nematodes damage roots by forming galls, reducing quality and yield. Aphids suck plant sap, causing curling and weakening of leaves. Leaf blight appears as dark spots on foliage, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor. Soft rot affects harvested or developing roots, leading to decay under high moisture conditions, while fungal infections may cause discoloration and reduced market value. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, deep plowing, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, use of resistant varieties, proper irrigation management, and recommended chemical or biological control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, maintaining proper soil structure, timely weeding, and integrated pest management techniques help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Beetroot.overview: Beetroot is a cool-climate root vegetable belonging to the Amaranthaceae family and scientifically known as Beta vulgaris. In Sri Lanka, beetroot is mainly cultivated in cooler regions such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy where environmental conditions are favorable for root development. Beetroot grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 25°C and requires well-drained fertile loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The crop requires moderate rainfall, adequate sunlight, and regular irrigation to maintain proper soil moisture for healthy root formation. Beetroot is valued for its edible swollen roots, which are rich in vitamins, minerals, antioxidants, and dietary fiber. The crop is generally harvested within 60 to 90 days after planting depending on the variety and cultivation conditions. Important factors affecting beetroot cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, sunlight, rainfall, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases affecting beetroot include leaf miners, aphids, root rot, damping off, and fungal leaf spot diseases, which can reduce crop yield and quality if not properly managed.
Beetroot.propagation: Direct sowing (each seed is actually a fruit cluster with 2–4 seeds)

activities.title: Deep plough to 30 cm
activities.description: Remove stones and clods. Fine tilth.
activities.why: Compaction causes deformed roots.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 10 t/ha compost. Check pH — lime if below 6.0.
activities.why: Acidic soil prevents nutrient uptake. Beetroot needs pH 6.0–7.0.

activities.title: Sow 3–4 cm deep, 10 cm apart in rows
activities.description: Each 'seed' contains 2–4 plants. Thin after emergence.
activities.why: Multiple seedlings per position are normal — thinning is essential.

activities.title: Thin to single plant
activities.description: Remove extras. Keep strongest.
activities.why: Crowded beetroot produces small stringy roots.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Hand weed carefully.
activities.why: Beet is a poor weed competitor in early stages.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha.
activities.why: Potassium for deep red colour and sugar content.

activities.title: Maintain even moisture
activities.description: Water every 3–4 days. Avoid wet-dry cycles.
activities.why: Inconsistent moisture causes internal white rings (zoning defect) and cracking.

activities.title: Check root shoulder
activities.description: Scrape soil to check — 4–6 cm diameter at shoulder.
activities.why: Checking avoids both immature and over-mature harvest.

activities.title: Reduce slightly
activities.description: Water every 5 days.
activities.why: Slight moisture reduction concentrates sugars and improves flavour.

activities.title: Harvest test
activities.description: Pull a root — should be 5–8 cm diameter, deep red, no softness.
activities.why: Over-mature beetroot develops a woody core.

activities.title: Harvest in cool morning
activities.description: Pull by hand or loosen with fork. Trim tops to 3 cm.
activities.why: Keep leaf stub to prevent bleeding during transport.

irrigation.notes: Even moisture is critical for quality. Drought followed by rain causes 'zoning' (white rings inside root) and cracking.

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Soft, decaying roots; black discolouration extending into flesh.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Excessive soil moisture, waterlogging, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained raised beds. Crop rotation. Avoid overwatering.
diseases.treatment: Improve drainage. Metalaxyl drench (1.5 g/L) if early detection.

diseases.name: Damping Off
diseases.symptoms: Young seedlings collapse at soil level; stem rot near soil surface.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged soil, dense sowing, cool wet conditions.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained seedbed. Thin to proper spacing. Avoid overwatering.
diseases.treatment: Captan or Thiram drench (2 g/L) at seedling stage.

diseases.name: Fungal Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Brown or dark circular spots with pale centres on leaves.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, overcrowding, wet foliage.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) every 10 days.

pests.name: Leaf Miner
pests.damage: Creates pale tunnels in leaves; heavy infestation reduces photosynthesis.
pests.identification: Visible pale winding trails inside leaf tissue.
pests.prevention: Remove affected leaves. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertiliser.
pests.treatment: Abamectin 1.8EC (0.5 ml/L) or Cyromazine when mines first appear.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from young leaves causing curling and yellowing.
pests.identification: Small green or black insects on new growth and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Encourage ladybird beetles.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) if heavy infestation.

risks.name: Drought
risks.description: Causes small, tough, cracked roots.
risks.mitigation: Consistent irrigation every 3–4 days.

risks.name: Boron Deficiency
risks.description: Internal black heart (dark tissue inside root). Common in leached soils.
risks.mitigation: Apply Borax 10–15 kg/ha at planting in boron-deficient soils.

harvest.method: Pull by hand or loosen with fork. Cut tops leaving 3 cm stub.
harvest.post_harvest: Keep cool. Do not remove leaf stub (prevents bleeding). Grade by size. Store 1–2 weeks in cool area.

Beetroot.growth_stages_text: Beetroot cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, root enlargement, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after sowing, while the vegetative growth stage continues for about 20–30 days depending on environmental conditions and nutrient availability. Root enlargement begins around 35–45 days after planting, and harvesting is generally carried out between 60–90 days when the roots become fully developed, firm, and reach marketable size. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods with proper spacing of approximately 10–15 cm between plants and 30–45 cm between rows to ensure healthy root growth and sufficient soil aeration.
Beetroot.management_text: Successful beetroot cultivation requires a week-by-week crop management schedule including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth and root development stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to improve plant growth and root quality. Irrigation frequency varies according to crop growth stages, with regular watering required during germination and root enlargement stages, while moderate irrigation is maintained during mature growth periods to avoid waterlogging. Harvesting indicators include swollen roots with deep red coloration, firm texture, and suitable root diameter for market and consumption purposes.
Beetroot.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in successful beetroot cultivation. Leaf miners create tunnels inside leaves that reduce photosynthesis and plant health, while aphids suck plant sap causing curling, yellowing, and weakening of leaves. Root rot commonly appears as soft, decaying roots caused by excessive soil moisture and fungal infections, while damping off affects young seedlings by causing stem rot near the soil surface. Fungal leaf spot diseases appear as brown or dark spots on leaves, reducing plant vigor and crop quality. These problems can be managed through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, proper drainage, removal of infected plants, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, timely irrigation management, maintaining healthy soil fertility, use of quality seeds, and climate-aware cultivation practices help reduce crop damage and improve overall productivity.

Cassava.overview: Cassava, commonly known as “Manioc” or “Cassava root” in Sri Lanka, is a tropical root crop belonging to the Euphorbiaceae family and scientifically known as Manihot esculenta. It is widely cultivated in Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones, due to its high tolerance to drought and ability to grow in low-fertility soils. Cassava grows best in temperatures between 25°C and 35°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0. The crop requires moderate rainfall and full sunlight exposure for healthy root development, although it can survive under relatively dry conditions once established. Cassava is an important carbohydrate-rich food crop and is commonly consumed boiled, fried, or processed into flour and other food products in Sri Lanka. The crop generally takes about 8 to 12 months to reach maturity depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cassava cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include cassava mosaic disease, mealybugs, mites, bacterial blight, and root rot, which can reduce yield and tuber quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Deep preparation for large tuber development.

activities.title: Deep plough to 40 cm
activities.description: Remove all stumps, roots, and large stones.
activities.why: Cassava develops large tubers up to 60 cm long — compaction causes misshapen tubers.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 5 t/ha compost. Do not over-fertilize.
activities.why: Excess nitrogen promotes leaf growth over tuber development.

stages.name: Planting & Establishment
stages.description: Plant stem cuttings at correct angle.

activities.title: Plant cuttings at 45° angle
activities.description: Plant 25–30 cm cuttings at 45° into soil with 3–4 nodes underground. Do not plant vertically.
activities.why: Angled planting improves rooting and makes tubers easier to harvest.

activities.title: Water once after planting if dry
activities.description: One irrigation to establish if soil is very dry.
activities.why: Cassava establishes well even in dry soil — do not overwater.

activities.title: Check shooting
activities.description: Buds should sprout within 10–14 days.
activities.why: Poor sprouting indicates old or diseased cuttings.

activities.title: Weed twice: at 1 and 2 months
activities.description: Critical weed-free first 2 months. After canopy closes, weeds are suppressed.
activities.why: Weed competition in first 2 months is the primary yield reducer.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 100 kg/ha.
activities.why: Modest nitrogen with high potassium for tuber starch content.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: MOP 100 kg/ha. No nitrogen — avoid excessive leaf growth.
activities.why: High potassium drives starch accumulation in tubers.

activities.title: Monitor for mosaic disease
activities.description: Check leaves for yellow-green mosaic pattern — sign of Cassava Mosaic.
activities.why: Mosaic can reduce yield by 50–80% in severe cases.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest when tubers reach desired size.

activities.title: Check maturity
activities.description: Dig up one plant and check tuber — no blue or dark discolouration of flesh.
activities.why: Blue/dark colour = prussic acid (HCN) — ensure cooking destroys it.

activities.title: Harvest and process quickly
activities.description: Cut stems, dig tubers, process within 24–48 hours.
activities.why: Cassava deteriorates rapidly after harvest — within 48 hours becomes unsaleable.

fertilization.why: No nitrogen basal — wait for establishment.
Potassium only for tuber starch fill.

irrigation.frequency: Rainfed mainly. Cassava tolerates 3–6 months dry season.
irrigation.notes: Cassava naturally drops leaves during drought and resumes growth when rain returns. This is a survival mechanism — not a sign the crop is dying.

diseases.name: Cassava Mosaic Disease
diseases.symptoms: Chlorotic (yellow-green) mosaic patterns on leaves; severe distortion; stunted growth; significantly reduced root yield.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High whitefly populations; planting infected cuttings.
diseases.prevention: Use certified disease-free stem cuttings. Control whitefly vector. Remove infected plants.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Manage whitefly: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L). Plant from healthy mother plants only.

diseases.name: Bacterial Blight
diseases.symptoms: Angular water-soaked spots on leaves; leaf blight; stem cankers; gummosis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, rain splash, infected planting material.
diseases.prevention: Use disease-free cuttings. Avoid overhead irrigation. Crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L) spray. Remove and destroy heavily infected material.

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Soft decaying roots; white fungal growth; reduced starch content.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged soil, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained ridges or mounds for planting. Avoid waterlogging.
diseases.treatment: Improve drainage. Metalaxyl drench if early signs detected.

pests.name: Mealybug
pests.damage: Sucks sap from growing tips; causes severe stunting and deformation of young leaves.
pests.identification: White waxy cottony masses on shoot tips and undersides of leaves.
pests.prevention: Use certified cutting material. Release parasitoid wasp (Anagyrus lopezi) for biocontrol.
pests.treatment: White oil spray (1%) or Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) on growing tips.

pests.name: Red Spider Mite
pests.damage: Sucks sap from leaf undersides; causes yellowing, leaf bronzing, and defoliation in dry conditions.
pests.identification: Fine webbing on leaf undersides; bronze or yellowish mottling on upper leaf surface.
pests.prevention: Irrigation reduces mite populations. Avoid excessive nitrogen.
pests.treatment: Abamectin 1.8EC (0.5 ml/L) or Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L).

risks.name: Cassava Mosaic Virus
risks.description: Can destroy 50–80% yield if uncontrolled whitefly spread infection.
risks.mitigation: Plant virus-free cuttings ONLY. Control whitefly from day 1.

risks.name: Post-harvest Deterioration
risks.description: Cassava tubers become inedible within 48–72 hours of harvest.
risks.signs: Blue/black vascular discolouration in tubers after cutting.
risks.mitigation: Harvest only as much as can be processed/sold within 48 hours.

indicators: Leaves begin to yellow and drop
harvest.method: Cut stems at 30 cm from ground. Use fork or dig by hand. Pull tubers out carefully.
harvest.post_harvest: Process within 24–48 hours. Boil, steam, dry, or grate for flour. Store processed cassava only — not fresh tubers.

Cassava.growth_stages_text: Cassava cultivation follows several important growth stages including stem establishment, vegetative growth, root development, and harvesting. The crop is usually propagated using stem cuttings rather than seeds, and these cuttings establish roots within 1–3 weeks after planting. Vegetative growth continues for about 2–4 months, during which the plant develops strong stems and foliage that support photosynthesis and root bulking. Root development begins after the vegetative phase and continues until maturity, with storage roots gradually enlarging and accumulating starch. Harvesting is typically carried out between 8–12 months after planting when leaves begin to yellow and roots reach optimal size and starch content. Farmers commonly use stem cuttings of 20–30 cm length and maintain spacing of approximately 1–1.5 meters between plants and rows to ensure proper root expansion and canopy development.
Cassava.management_text: Successful cassava cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, stem planting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer management is relatively low compared to other crops, but balanced use of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium helps improve root yield and plant vigor. Organic manure is often applied to enhance soil structure and long-term fertility. Irrigation is mainly important during the early establishment phase, while mature plants are highly drought-tolerant and require minimal watering.
Cassava.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are critical in cassava production. Cassava mosaic disease is one of the most serious viral diseases, causing leaf distortion, yellow mosaic patterns, and reduced root yield. Mealybugs and mites suck plant sap, weakening the plant and reducing productivity. Bacterial blight causes leaf spotting and premature defoliation, while root rot occurs in poorly drained soils leading to decay of storage roots. These problems can be managed through the use of disease-free planting materials, crop rotation, field sanitation, resistant varieties, proper spacing, and integrated pest management practices. Regular monitoring and timely removal of infected plants are essential to prevent large-scale yield losses and ensure stable production.

Innala.overview: Innala, commonly known in Sri Lanka as yam or lesser yam, is a traditional tuber crop belonging to the Dioscoreaceae family and scientifically known as Dioscorea species, particularly Dioscorea esculenta. It is widely cultivated in rural areas and home gardens across Sri Lanka due to its nutritional value and adaptability to tropical climatic conditions. Innala grows best in temperatures between 22°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soils rich in organic matter with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop requires moderate rainfall, adequate soil moisture, and good sunlight exposure for healthy vine growth and tuber development. Innala is an important carbohydrate-rich food source and is commonly consumed boiled, steamed, or prepared in traditional Sri Lankan dishes. The crop generally takes about 6 to 9 months to mature depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include yam beetles, nematodes, tuber rot, and fungal leaf diseases, which can reduce tuber quality and yield if not properly managed.
Innala.propagation: Small tubers or tuber pieces (100–200 g each)

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Prepare deep soil and install trellis.

activities.title: Deep plough to 40 cm
activities.description: Dig planting mounds or pits 45×45×45 cm.
activities.why: Yam tubers grow deeply — 30–60 cm. Compaction causes poor yields.

activities.title: Install trellis
activities.description: Erect 2.5–3 m trellis or use trees as support.
activities.why: Yam vines are heavy and climb very high — needs strong support.

activities.title: Plant tuber seed pieces
activities.description: Place 100–200 g tuber pieces 5–8 cm deep in prepared mounds. Sprouted end up.
activities.why: Innala is slow to establish — 3–4 weeks for first shoots is normal.

activities.title: Guide vines onto trellis
activities.description: Direct shoots upward onto trellis from first emergence.
activities.why: Early training prevents ground-level disease and maximises leaf area.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha ring + MOP 100 kg/ha.
activities.why: Moderate nitrogen; high potassium for tuber starch.

activities.title: Weed twice in first 2 months
activities.description: Hand weed around base. Do not disturb developing tubers.
activities.why: Weed control in first 2 months is critical.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: MOP 100 kg/ha. No extra nitrogen.
activities.why: Potassium for starch accumulation.

activities.title: Check for tuber rot
activities.description: Scrape soil to inspect — brown soft patches on tubers indicate rot.
activities.why: Tuber rot is the main disease risk for innala.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest when vine dies back naturally.

activities.title: Reduce irrigation
activities.description: Stop irrigation 4–6 weeks before harvest.
activities.why: Allows tuber skin to harden for storage.

activities.title: Harvest after vine yellows
activities.description: Dig carefully when vine yellows and dries back.
activities.why: Natural vine dieback is the signal for harvest readiness.

fertilization.why: Rich pit base for long-duration crop.

irrigation.notes: Innala prefers moist but well-drained conditions. Avoid waterlogging at all times.

diseases.name: Tuber Rot
diseases.symptoms: Soft decaying tubers; foul smell; brown discolouration of tuber flesh.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Poorly drained soil, waterlogging, mechanical wounds.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained raised beds or ridges. Handle tubers carefully. Good drainage.
diseases.treatment: Improve drainage. Copper fungicide drench. Remove and destroy rotten tubers.

diseases.name: Leaf Disease
diseases.symptoms: Brown spots or lesions on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and vigour.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, dense canopy, wet conditions.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Staking to improve airflow. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) spray as preventive measure.

pests.name: Yam Beetle
pests.damage: Adults and larvae damage underground tubers causing decay and yield loss.
pests.identification: Round beetle; feeding damage visible on tuber surface.
pests.prevention: Crop rotation. Deep ploughing. Harvest on time to avoid prolonged soil exposure.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules (5 g/m²) at planting.

pests.name: Root Knot Nematode
pests.damage: Forms galls on roots and tubers causing deformation and reduced yield.
pests.identification: Swollen galls on tuber surface; stunted plant growth.
pests.prevention: Crop rotation. Deep ploughing. Marigold intercrop.
pests.treatment: Carbofuran granules at planting. Neem cake soil application.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Causes tuber rot — the primary yield loss factor.
risks.mitigation: Raise mounds. Ensure drainage channels are always clear.

risks.name: Mechanical Damage at Harvest
risks.description: Damaged tubers rot within days and cannot be stored.
risks.mitigation: Dig carefully with hands near tubers. Use fork only in outer area.

indicators: Vine yellows and dries back naturally
Tubers feel firm and solid when dug
harvest.method: Dig carefully by hand around mound. Extract tubers without cutting. Clean soil gently.
harvest.post_harvest: Cure in shade 7–10 days. Store in cool dry ventilated area. Shelf life 2–3 months.

Innala.growth_stages_text: Innala cultivation follows several important growth stages including sprouting, vegetative vine growth, tuber initiation, tuber bulking, and harvesting. The crop is usually propagated using small tubers or tuber pieces, which sprout within 2–4 weeks after planting. Vegetative vine growth continues for about 2–3 months, during which strong climbing vines develop and support photosynthesis. Tuber initiation begins after the vegetative phase, followed by gradual tuber bulking underground. Depending on the variety, harvesting is carried out between 6–9 months when vines begin to dry and tubers reach full size and starch content. Farmers commonly plant tuber pieces at a depth of about 5–10 cm and maintain spacing of approximately 60–100 cm between plants to allow proper tuber expansion and vine spread.
Innala.management_text: Successful innala cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, planting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, vine support, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is generally moderate, with emphasis on organic manure and balanced nutrients to support tuber development and improve soil fertility. Potassium plays an important role in enhancing tuber size and quality, while excessive nitrogen is avoided as it promotes excessive vine growth. Irrigation is most important during sprouting and early vegetative growth, while mature plants are moderately drought-tolerant once tuber development is established.
Innala.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in innala production. Yam beetles damage underground tubers by feeding on them, reducing quality and market value. Nematodes attack roots and tubers, causing deformities and yield loss. Tuber rot occurs in poorly drained soils and leads to decay during growth or storage, while fungal leaf diseases reduce photosynthesis and plant vigor. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, proper field sanitation, use of healthy planting material, good drainage practices, balanced fertilizer application, and integrated pest management techniques. Regular field monitoring and timely intervention are important to minimize losses and ensure stable tuber production.

Kiri Ala.overview: “Kiri Ala” in Sri Lanka refers to sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas), a widely grown root crop belonging to the Convolvulaceae family. It is cultivated throughout Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones, as well as home gardens, due to its ability to tolerate drought and grow in relatively poor soils. Sweet potato grows best in warm tropical temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.5. The crop requires moderate rainfall and good sunlight exposure, but it is highly tolerant to dry conditions once established. “Kiri Ala” is an important staple food in Sri Lankan diets and is consumed in boiled, steamed, or curry preparations, valued for its high carbohydrate content, fiber, vitamins, and beta-carotene in orange-fleshed varieties. The crop is generally ready for harvest within 90 to 150 days depending on the variety and growing conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include sweet potato weevils, aphids, fungal leaf spots, and root rot, which can reduce yield and tuber quality if not properly managed.
Kiri Ala.propagation: Vine cuttings (30–40 cm from healthy vines)

activities.title: Form ridges 25–30 cm high
activities.description: Row spacing 90 cm. High ridges prevent waterlogging.
activities.why: Sweet potato tubers form in ridges — loose well-drained soil produces best yields.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 8 t/ha compost into ridges.
activities.why: Organic matter improves drainage and water retention.

activities.title: Plant cuttings on ridge
activities.description: Press cutting into ridge at 45° angle with 2–3 nodes underground. Water immediately.
activities.why: Nodal buds underground develop both roots and tubers.

activities.title: Water every 3 days for 2 weeks
activities.description: Maintain moisture for rooting.
activities.why: Consistent moisture in first 2 weeks is critical for vine establishment.

activities.title: Weed once at 3 WAP
activities.description: Weed thoroughly. After this, vines suppress weeds.
activities.why: One thorough weeding before vine spread is all that is usually needed.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 100 kg/ha along ridges.
activities.why: Low nitrogen prevents excessive vine growth at expense of tubers. High potassium for tuber fill.

activities.title: Turn vines back onto ridge
activities.description: Redirect straying vines back onto ridge every 2 weeks.
activities.why: Vines rooting into soil away from ridge reduce tuber yield on main plant.

activities.title: Check tuber size
activities.description: Scrape ridge to check — tubers should be developing well.
activities.why: Early check allows harvesting young small tubers as a premium product.

activities.title: Maintain moderate irrigation
activities.description: Water every 7–10 days. Reduce near harvest.
activities.why: Consistent moisture prevents cracked misshapen tubers.

stages.name: Harvest
stages.description: Harvest when tubers reach desired size.

activities.title: Early harvest option
activities.description: Harvest some plants at 90 days for young sweet potatoes (smaller, more tender).
activities.why: Early sweet potato commands better price.

activities.title: Main harvest
activities.description: Cut vines, dig ridges by hand or fork. Handle carefully.
activities.why: Full-sized tubers at 120–150 days.

fertilization.why: Base P. No K or N basal.
Low N, high K — the correct ratio for tuber crops.

irrigation.notes: Sweet potato tolerates moderate drought well. Excess nitrogen or water causes masses of vines with few tubers.

diseases.name: Sweet Potato Virus Disease
diseases.symptoms: Severe leaf distortion and stunting. Yellow streaks and mosaic on leaves. Tuber yield reduced 50–80%.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Infected planting material, whitefly and aphid vectors.
diseases.prevention: Use certified virus-free cuttings. Control whitefly.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Use clean planting material only.

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Soft rot of tubers in waterlogged conditions.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged ridges.
diseases.prevention: High ridges for good drainage.
diseases.treatment: No treatment. Drainage prevention.

pests.name: Sweet Potato Weevil
pests.damage: Adults and larvae destroy tubers — entry holes, frass, brown tunnels inside tubers. Primary pest.
pests.identification: Entry holes on tubers. Frass and tunnels when cut open.
pests.prevention: Healthy certified cuttings. High ridges prevent surface laying. Harvest promptly.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyriphos soil drench at planting. Harvest at correct time — delay dramatically increases damage.

pests.name: Aphids
pests.damage: Virus vectors. Sap sucking.
pests.identification: Clusters on new growth.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps.
pests.treatment: Neem oil 5 ml/L.

risks.name: Sweet Potato Weevil
risks.description: Primary risk — delayed harvest dramatically increases damage.
risks.mitigation: Harvest promptly at maturity. Never leave tubers in ground beyond maturity date.

risks.name: Excessive Nitrogen
risks.description: Causes excessive vine growth with few or small tubers.
risks.signs: Dense vine canopy, poor tuber yield.
risks.mitigation: Keep nitrogen low. Do not exceed Urea 50 kg/ha.

harvest.method: Cut vines to 10 cm. Dig ridges carefully with fork. Extract tubers without cutting.
harvest.frequency: Single harvest or early + main harvest
harvest.post_harvest: Cure in shade 5–7 days to harden skin. Store in cool dry ventilated area. Shelf life 1–2 months.

Kiri Ala.growth_stages_text: Sweet potato cultivation follows several important growth stages including vine establishment, vegetative growth, tuber initiation, tuber bulking, and harvesting. Unlike seed-based crops, sweet potato is usually propagated using vine cuttings, which establish roots within 7–14 days after planting. Vegetative vine growth continues for about 30–60 days, followed by tuber initiation beneath the soil surface. Tuber bulking occurs from around 60 days onwards and continues until harvest, which typically takes place between 90–150 days depending on the variety. Farmers commonly use vine cuttings of 20–30 cm length and maintain spacing of approximately 25–30 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure proper ground coverage and tuber expansion.
Kiri Ala.management_text: Successful sweet potato cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, vine planting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is generally moderate, with emphasis on potassium to support tuber development, while excessive nitrogen is avoided as it promotes vine growth instead of tuber formation. Organic manure is commonly used to improve soil structure and yield. Irrigation is most important during vine establishment and early tuber formation, while mature crops can tolerate dry conditions. Harvesting indicators include yellowing of leaves, drying vines, and well-developed underground tubers with proper size and skin formation.
Kiri Ala.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are important in sweet potato cultivation. Sweet potato weevils are among the most damaging pests, boring into tubers and causing internal decay and yield loss. Aphids feed on plant sap and may also transmit viral diseases, while fungal leaf spots reduce leaf function and plant vigor. Root rot occurs in poorly drained soils and leads to tuber decay and reduced yield. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, use of healthy vine cuttings, field sanitation, proper soil drainage, balanced fertilizer use, and biological or chemical control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, weed control, and integrated pest management techniques help improve yield stability and crop quality.

Okra.overview: Okra, commonly known as “Bandakka” in Sri Lanka, is a warm-season vegetable crop belonging to the Malvaceae family and scientifically known as Abelmoschus esculentus. It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones as well as home gardens, due to its high adaptability to tropical climates. Okra grows best in temperatures between 24°C and 35°C and requires well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.5. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy plant growth and continuous pod production. Okra is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine for curries and stir-fry dishes and is valued for its high fiber, vitamin, and mineral content. The crop is fast-growing and can be harvested within 45 to 60 days after planting, with regular picking encouraging continuous yield. Important factors affecting okra cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, fruit borers, whiteflies, jassids, powdery mildew, and yellow vein mosaic virus, which can significantly reduce yield and pod quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough 20 cm and fine till
activities.description: Remove weeds thoroughly. Form rows.
activities.why: Good seedbed ensures uniform germination.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 8 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Organic base for fast-growing continuous-harvesting crop.

activities.title: Sow 2 seeds per hole at 2–3 cm depth
activities.description: Spacing 30×60 cm. Water.
activities.why: Higher germination success with 2 seeds per hole.

activities.title: Thin to 1 plant
activities.description: Remove weaker seedling at 7–10 days.
activities.why: Single strong plant per position is more productive.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Hand weed or hoe carefully.
activities.why: Critical weed-free period. Okra is a poor early weed competitor.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha ring.
activities.why: Nitrogen drives the rapid growth needed for early fruiting.

activities.title: Scout for whitefly and jassids
activities.description: Check undersides of leaves. Look for yellowing — early YVMV sign.
activities.why: Yellow Vein Mosaic Virus is the biggest threat to okra.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Sustains continuous pod production.

activities.title: Daily YVMV check
activities.description: Look for yellow vein patterns on leaves. Remove infected plants immediately.
activities.why: Every infected plant is a virus source. Remove before whitefly spread it further.

activities.title: Begin first harvest
activities.description: Pick pods at 5–8 cm — tender and finger-thick.
activities.why: Harvest at correct size: too young = waste; too old = fibrous and tough.

activities.title: Harvest every 2 days
activities.description: Pick all pods at correct size. Never leave mature pods.
activities.why: Leaving mature pods signals plant to stop producing new flowers.

activities.title: Late top dressing
activities.description: Urea 25 kg/ha + MOP 25 kg/ha.
activities.why: Small late feeding extends productive life by 2–3 weeks.

irrigation.notes: Okra tolerates moderate drought but regular irrigation improves pod size and quality significantly.

diseases.name: Yellow Vein Mosaic Virus
diseases.symptoms: Yellowing of leaf veins in network pattern; stunted growth; fruit deformation; severe yield loss.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High whitefly population; hot dry weather; overlapping cropping.
diseases.prevention: Control whitefly vector. Use resistant varieties. Remove infected plants immediately.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Manage whitefly: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam fortnightly.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and fruit production.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Warm dry days, cool nights, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Tebuconazole (1 ml/L).

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap; most critical pest as vector of Yellow Vein Mosaic Virus.
pests.identification: Clouds of tiny white insects when plants disturbed; yellowing leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective mulch. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam. Rotate insecticide classes.

pests.name: Jassid (Leafhopper)
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes leaf curling, yellowing, and stunted growth especially in young plants.
pests.identification: Tiny wedge-shaped insects that jump when disturbed; pale stippling on leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Avoid dense canopy.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) or Imidacloprid.

pests.name: Fruit Borer
pests.damage: Larvae bore into pods reducing marketable yield.
pests.identification: Entry holes and frass on pod surface.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Remove and destroy infested pods.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) at pod formation stage.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes leaf curling; secondary honeydew promotes sooty mould.
pests.identification: Colonies on young growth and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Encourage predatory insects.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L).

risks.name: Yellow Vein Mosaic Virus
risks.description: Single biggest threat to okra in Sri Lanka. Once 20% plants infected, epidemic begins.
risks.signs: Yellow vein patterns spreading through field.
risks.mitigation: Zero tolerance — remove every infected plant immediately. Control whitefly from day 1.

risks.name: Delayed Harvest
risks.description: Old fibrous pods have zero market value and stop new production.
risks.signs: Hard fibrous pods. Reduced new flowers.
risks.mitigation: Harvest every 2 days without exception.

indicators: Tip of pod still soft and pliable when bent
harvest.method: Cut with sharp knife or scissors leaving 1 cm stalk. Wear gloves — plant is spiny.
harvest.post_harvest: Grade by size. Store in cool shaded area. Market within 1–2 days — okra wilts rapidly.

Okra.growth_stages_text: Okra cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, flowering, pod development, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 4–7 days after sowing, while vegetative growth continues for about 20–30 days depending on environmental conditions and soil fertility. Flowering begins around 35–45 days after planting, followed quickly by pod formation and continuous harvesting. Farmers typically start harvesting okra within 45–60 days after planting when pods are tender, green, and still soft. Frequent picking every 2–3 days is essential to maintain continuous production and prevent pods from becoming fibrous. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 30–45 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure good air circulation, sunlight exposure, and efficient nutrient uptake.
Okra.management_text: Successful okra cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to support vigorous plant growth and sustained pod production. Irrigation frequency is highest during germination and flowering stages due to high water demand, while moderate irrigation is maintained during later stages to avoid water stress and flower drop. Harvesting indicators include tender pods that snap easily, smooth texture, and bright green color before seed hardening begins.
Okra.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are critical in okra cultivation. Aphids and whiteflies suck plant sap, leading to curling, yellowing, and reduced plant vigor, while fruit borers damage developing pods internally, reducing market quality. Jassids cause leaf curling and stunted growth, especially in young plants. Powdery mildew appears as white fungal growth on leaves and reduces photosynthesis, while yellow vein mosaic virus causes yellowing of leaf veins, stunted growth, and severe yield loss. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plants, use of resistant varieties, and biological or chemical pest control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, maintaining soil fertility, and integrated pest management techniques help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Sunflower.overview: Sunflower is an oilseed crop belonging to the Asteraceae family and scientifically known as Helianthus annuus. It is cultivated in several parts of Sri Lanka, particularly in dry and intermediate zones, due to its ability to grow under warm climatic conditions and relatively low water requirements. Sunflower grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and requires well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.5. The crop requires moderate rainfall, adequate sunlight, and moderate irrigation for healthy stem growth, flowering, and seed development. Sunflower is mainly cultivated for its seeds, which are used for edible oil production, animal feed, and snacks, while the plant also has ornamental value due to its large bright yellow flowers. The crop generally matures within 80 to 120 days after planting depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting sunflower cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, caterpillars, cutworms, downy mildew, rust, and fungal stem rot diseases, which can reduce seed yield and crop quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough 25 cm and fine till
activities.description: Remove weeds. Level field.
activities.why: Sunflower has a taproot — good tilth improves anchorage against wind.

activities.title: Sow 1–2 seeds per hole at 3–4 cm
activities.description: Spacing 30×60 cm.
activities.why: Correct spacing gives optimal head size.

activities.title: Thin to 1 plant
activities.description: Remove weaker seedling.
activities.why: Single plant per position gives full-sized heads.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Weed thoroughly — sunflower is poor weed competitor until stem elongates.
activities.why: Weeds in first 4 weeks can reduce yield by 30–50%.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha + MOP 75 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Nitrogen for stem growth; potassium for head development.

activities.title: Second weeding and hill up
activities.description: Hill up soil 10 cm around stem base.
activities.why: Hilling improves anchorage — prevents lodging in wind.

activities.title: Ensure moisture at bud stage
activities.description: Water every 5–7 days.
activities.why: Water stress at budding and flowering reduces seed set significantly.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: MOP 75 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Potassium for seed oil content.

activities.title: Begin bird protection
activities.description: Post scarecrows. Stretch reflective tape across field. Patrol at dawn and dusk.
activities.why: Birds can destroy 30–50% of seeds at this stage — the most critical risk.

activities.title: Check for seed maturity
activities.description: Press seeds — hard and firm. Back of head turns yellow-brown.
activities.why: Soft seeds = too early; fully dry = seeds shattering.

activities.title: Cut heads
activities.description: Cut head with 30 cm stalk when back is yellow-brown.
activities.why: Leaving too long causes shattering and bird damage.

fertilization.why: Base P and K at planting.

irrigation.notes: Sunflower is relatively drought tolerant. Most critical moisture period is bud to petal drop.

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.symptoms: Yellowing of young leaves; stunted plants; white downy growth on leaf undersides.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High soil moisture, cool temperatures after sowing, poor drainage.
diseases.prevention: Use treated seeds. Well-drained soil. Crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl seed treatment. Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) spray if outbreak.

diseases.name: Rust
diseases.symptoms: Orange-brown powdery pustules on leaves; defoliation in severe cases.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, moderate temperatures, dense canopy.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Crop rotation. Remove infected lower leaves.
diseases.treatment: Tebuconazole (1 ml/L) or Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) every 10 days.

diseases.name: Stem Rot / Sclerotinia
diseases.symptoms: White cottony growth on stem near soil; stem becomes soft and plant collapses.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High soil moisture, cool conditions, dense crop.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained soil. Crop rotation. Good plant spacing.
diseases.treatment: Carbendazim (1 ml/L) drench at soil level or Thiophanate-methyl.

pests.name: Cutworm
pests.damage: Cuts seedling stems at soil level overnight; young plants found collapsed.
pests.identification: Fat grey or brown caterpillar found in soil near damaged plant.
pests.prevention: Soil tillage before planting. Remove crop debris. Hand-pick larvae at night.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules (5 g/m²) in soil before planting.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Colonises growing head and stem; causes plant weakening and reduces seed fill.
pests.identification: Dense colonies on stem, leaves, and developing flower head.
pests.prevention: Encourage natural enemies (ladybirds). Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) if population exceeds threshold.

pests.name: Caterpillar (Head Borer)
pests.damage: Bores into developing seed heads; reduces seed quality and oil yield.
pests.identification: Entry holes in flower head; frass visible; larvae inside when head opened.
pests.prevention: Monitor from flowering stage. Remove and destroy infested heads.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) at early head stage.

risks.name: Bird Damage
risks.description: Most serious risk at grain fill. Can destroy 50% of head.
risks.signs: Seeds missing from heads, bird flocks.
risks.mitigation: Post reflective tape and scarecrows before heads open. Patrol daily.

risks.name: Lodging in Wind
risks.description: Tall plants fall over, making harvest difficult.
risks.signs: Plants leaning or fallen after storms.
risks.mitigation: Hill up soil at 5 WAP. Space correctly — do not plant too densely.

indicators: Seeds hard and firm when pressed
harvest.method: Cut head with 20–30 cm stalk using sickle or sharp knife.
harvest.frequency: Single harvest over 1–2 weeks as heads mature
harvest.post_harvest: Dry heads in sun 5–7 days. Thresh by beating head. Winnow seeds. Sun dry grain to 8% moisture for oil extraction. Store in sealed bags.

Sunflower.growth_stages_text: Sunflower cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, bud formation, flowering, and seed development. Germination usually occurs within 5–10 days after planting, followed by rapid seedling establishment and vegetative growth for about 30–45 days. Bud formation begins around 40–55 days after planting, leading to flowering between 55–70 days depending on the variety. After pollination, seed development continues for about 30–40 days until maturity, with harvesting typically carried out within 80–120 days when flower heads turn yellow-brown and seeds become firm and fully developed. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 45–60 cm between plants and 60–75 cm between rows to ensure proper sunlight exposure, air circulation, and strong stem development.
Sunflower.management_text: Successful sunflower cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer management is important during early vegetative and flowering stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium fertilizers along with organic compost to support strong plant growth and improve seed yield and oil content. Potassium plays a key role in enhancing seed filling and oil quality, while excessive nitrogen is avoided as it may promote excessive vegetative growth. Irrigation is most important during germination, flowering, and seed filling stages, while the crop can tolerate moderate drought during later stages.
Sunflower.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in sunflower cultivation. Aphids suck plant sap and can also transmit viral diseases, while caterpillars and cutworms damage leaves and young seedlings, reducing plant vigor. Downy mildew causes yellowing and stunted growth, especially in humid conditions, while rust appears as orange-brown pustules on leaves, reducing photosynthesis. Fungal stem rot weakens stems and may cause lodging and plant collapse. These issues can be managed through crop rotation, field sanitation, removal of infected plants, balanced fertilizer use, proper spacing, resistant varieties, and biological or chemical control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, timely irrigation management, and integrated pest management help improve yield stability and seed quality.

Beans.overview: Beans, commonly known as “Haricot beans” or “French beans” in Sri Lanka, are a widely cultivated vegetable crop belonging to the Fabaceae family and scientifically known as Phaseolus vulgaris. They are mainly grown in cooler and intermediate regions such as Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, and Kandy, where climatic conditions are suitable for healthy pod development. Beans grow best in temperatures between 15°C and 25°C and require well-drained fertile loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and good sunlight exposure for optimal flowering and pod formation. Beans are a major source of protein, fiber, and essential nutrients and are widely used in Sri Lankan curries and vegetable dishes. The crop is relatively short-duration and can be harvested within 50 to 70 days after planting for green beans, depending on the variety. Important factors affecting bean cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, bean flies, thrips, pod borers, and fungal diseases such as powdery mildew and rust, which can reduce yield and pod quality if not properly controlled.

activities.title: Plough to 20 cm and fine till
activities.description: Fine seedbed. Remove weeds. Form raised beds if wet zone.
activities.why: Beans have shallow roots — fine tilth ensures even germination.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 8 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Good organic base for this fast-growing crop.

activities.title: Sow 60–80 kg/ha at 3–4 cm depth
activities.description: Rows 45 cm apart, 10 cm within row.
activities.why: Higher seed rate gives good canopy for weed suppression.

activities.title: Irrigate if soil is dry
activities.description: Bring soil to field capacity.
activities.why: Beans need moisture for uniform germination.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Hand weed or hoe carefully. Beans are shallow-rooted.
activities.why: Weed competition in first 4 weeks reduces yield by 40%.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Moderate nitrogen — beans fix some nitrogen. Potassium for pod quality.

activities.title: Scout for bean fly and aphids
activities.description: Check stem base for bean fly damage (swelling). Check leaves for aphid colonies.
activities.why: Bean fly is the most damaging early pest of beans.

activities.title: Maintain even moisture
activities.description: Water every 3–4 days. Avoid wet-dry cycles.
activities.why: Irregular moisture causes pod drop and pod deformities.

activities.title: Watch for rust disease
activities.description: Check undersides of leaves for orange-brown pustules.
activities.why: Rust spreads rapidly in cool humid up-country conditions.

activities.title: First harvest
activities.description: Pick when pods snap cleanly and seeds not yet visible through skin.
activities.why: Young tender pods have best market value.

activities.title: Harvest every 3–5 days
activities.description: Pick all mature pods regularly.
activities.why: Leaving mature pods stops new pod development.

fertilization.why: Foundation with P for root nodules.

irrigation.notes: Beans are sensitive to both drought and waterlogging. Consistent moisture is key.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery growth on leaves; reduces photosynthesis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Low humidity, high temperatures, poor airflow.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Triadimefon (1 g/L).

diseases.name: Rust Disease
diseases.symptoms: Reddish-brown raised pustules on undersides of leaves; weakens plant vigour.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Cool humid weather, overcrowding, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Crop rotation. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Tebuconazole (1 ml/L).

pests.name: Bean Fly
pests.damage: Larvae attack stems causing wilting and death of young plants.
pests.identification: Wilting seedlings at 7–14 days; maggots in stem.
pests.prevention: Early planting. Seed dressing with insecticide.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos soil drench (2 ml/L) or Carbosulfan seed dressing.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes leaf curling and stunted growth.
pests.identification: Colonies on new shoots and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Yellow sticky traps.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L).

pests.name: Thrips
pests.damage: Damages flowers reducing pod formation.
pests.identification: Tiny insects inside flowers; silvery petal damage.
pests.prevention: Blue sticky traps. Spray at flower bud stage.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L).

risks.name: Frost
risks.description: Kills beans instantly. Common in up-country during cold nights.
risks.mitigation: Cover with frost cloth at night. Harvest mature pods before frost season.

risks.name: High Temperature (>28°C)
risks.description: Poor flower set at high temperatures.
risks.mitigation: Plant in cooler months. Partial shade in mid-country.

indicators: Pod is full but seeds not bulging through skin
harvest.method: Hand pick individual pods. Do not pull vines.
harvest.post_harvest: Store cool. Market within 2–3 days. Beans lose quality rapidly after picking.

Beans.growth_stages_text: Bean cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, vegetative growth, flowering, pod formation, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 5–8 days after sowing, while vegetative growth continues for about 15–25 days depending on environmental conditions and soil fertility. Flowering begins around 25–35 days after planting, followed by pod development and rapid maturation. Harvesting of green beans typically begins within 50–70 days after planting when pods are tender, green, and suitable for consumption. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 30–45 cm between plants and 45–60 cm between rows to ensure proper airflow, sunlight penetration, and healthy pod development.
Beans.management_text: Successful bean cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is important during early vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to support plant growth and improve pod quality. Irrigation frequency is highest during germination and flowering stages, while moderate irrigation is maintained during pod development to avoid water stress. Harvesting indicators include firm, tender, green pods that break easily without fiber formation.
Beans.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in bean cultivation. Aphids feed on plant sap, causing curling, yellowing, and stunted growth, while bean flies attack seedlings and can cause early plant death. Thrips damage flowers, reducing pod formation, and pod borers feed inside developing pods, lowering yield and quality. Powdery mildew appears as white fungal growth on leaves, reducing photosynthesis, while rust disease causes reddish-brown spots that weaken plant vigor. These problems can be controlled through crop rotation, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plant parts, resistant varieties, and biological or chemical pest control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, maintaining soil fertility, and integrated pest management strategies help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Black Gram.overview: Black gram, commonly known as “Undu” in Sri Lanka, is an important leguminous pulse crop belonging to the Fabaceae family and scientifically known as Vigna mungo. It is mainly cultivated in the dry and intermediate zones of Sri Lanka due to its ability to tolerate warm climatic conditions and relatively low water availability. Black gram grows best in temperatures between 25°C and 35°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.5. The crop requires low to moderate rainfall, good sunlight exposure, and moderate soil moisture for healthy growth and pod development. Black gram is highly valued for its high protein content and is widely used in Sri Lankan cuisine for preparing curries, flour-based foods, and traditional dishes. The crop also improves soil fertility through nitrogen fixation, making it beneficial in crop rotation systems. Black gram generally matures within 70 to 100 days after planting depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, pod borers, whiteflies, leaf curl disease, powdery mildew, and root rot, which can reduce yield and seed quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Plough to 15–20 cm
activities.description: Clear weeds. Fine seedbed for small seeds.
activities.why: Small seeds need good soil contact for germination.

activities.title: Sow 20–25 kg/ha at 3 cm depth
activities.description: Rows 30 cm, 10 cm within row.
activities.why: Correct population density optimises yield.

activities.title: Pre-sowing irrigation if needed
activities.description: Bring soil to field capacity.
activities.why: Dry soil delays germination significantly.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Critical weed-free period.
activities.why: Weeds reduce yield by up to 50% if not controlled.

activities.title: Apply top dressing
activities.description: MOP 50 kg/ha + Urea 25 kg/ha.
activities.why: Minimal N — black gram fixes its own. Potassium for pod strength.

activities.title: Scout for pod borer
activities.description: Check pods for entry holes.
activities.why: Pod borer is the key yield threat.

activities.title: Irrigate if dry at pod fill
activities.description: One irrigation if no rain for 10+ days.
activities.why: Moisture at pod fill prevents seed shrivelling.

activities.title: First picking
activities.description: Pick dark-coloured mature pods.
activities.why: 2–3 picks needed as pods mature unevenly.

activities.title: Final harvest
activities.description: Cut entire plant when 80% pods mature.

fertilization.why: P for nodules. No N basal.

irrigation.notes: Black gram is drought tolerant. Excess water causes root rot and lodging.

diseases.name: Leaf Curl Disease
diseases.symptoms: Severe leaf curling, distortion, and yellowing; stunted plant growth; drastically reduced pod yield.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High whitefly populations; hot dry weather.
diseases.prevention: Control whitefly vector. Use virus-free certified seeds. Remove infected plants.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Manage whitefly: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam fortnightly.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery layer on upper leaf surfaces; reduces photosynthesis.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Hot dry weather alternating with humidity.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid excessive nitrogen.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Triadimefon (1 g/L).

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Yellowing, wilting, and death of plants; brown decay of roots at soil level.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged soil, poor drainage, heavy rainfall.
diseases.prevention: Well-drained soil. Crop rotation. Avoid overwatering.
diseases.treatment: Captan drench (2 g/L) at early stage.

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.damage: Sucks sap; primary vector of Leaf Curl Disease — most serious threat to black gram.
pests.identification: White insects on leaf undersides; yellowing and curling leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective silver mulch. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam. Spray fortnightly.

pests.name: Pod Borer
pests.damage: Larvae feed inside pods reducing seed quality and yield.
pests.identification: Entry holes on pod surface; frass visible.
pests.prevention: Pheromone traps. Monitor at pod formation.
pests.treatment: Spinosad 45SC (0.3 ml/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L).

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; causes leaf curling and may transmit viral diseases.
pests.identification: Colonies on young shoots and leaf undersides.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Yellow sticky traps.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L).

risks.name: Drought at Pod Fill
risks.description: Shrivelled seeds, low yield.
risks.mitigation: One irrigation at pod fill if no rain.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Root rot — zero tolerance.
risks.mitigation: Never plant in poorly drained areas.

harvest.method: 2–3 hand pickings of dark pods OR cut plant and sun-dry, then thresh.
harvest.post_harvest: Sun dry to 12% moisture. Store in sealed bags with neem leaves.

Black Gram.growth_stages_text: Black gram cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, flowering, pod formation, and harvesting. Germination typically occurs within 4–7 days after sowing, followed by rapid vegetative growth for about 20–35 days. Flowering usually begins around 35–50 days after planting, followed by pod development and seed filling until maturity. Harvesting is generally carried out within 70–100 days when most pods turn black or brown and become dry and ready for threshing. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 30–45 cm between plants and 45–60 cm between rows to ensure proper branching, airflow, and efficient pod formation.
Black Gram.management_text: Successful black gram cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer requirements are relatively low due to biological nitrogen fixation, but phosphorus and potassium are important for root development, flowering, and pod filling. Organic manure is often applied to improve soil structure and water retention. Irrigation is most important during germination and flowering stages, while mature plants are relatively drought tolerant and require minimal watering.
Black Gram.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in black gram cultivation. Aphids and whiteflies suck plant sap and may transmit viral diseases, leading to leaf curling and stunted growth. Pod borers directly damage developing pods and reduce seed yield and quality. Leaf curl disease causes deformation and reduced plant vigor, while powdery mildew appears as a white powdery layer on leaves, reducing photosynthesis. Root rot occurs in poorly drained soils and can lead to plant death and yield loss. These issues can be managed through crop rotation, use of resistant varieties, timely sowing, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, and integrated pest management practices. Regular field monitoring and early intervention help ensure stable and productive harvests.

Cucumber.overview: Cucumber, commonly known as “Pipinna” in Sri Lanka, is a fast-growing tropical vegetable crop belonging to the Cucurbitaceae family and scientifically known as Cucumis sativus. It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, especially in dry and intermediate zones as well as home gardens, due to its adaptability to warm climatic conditions and short growing period.
