Cucumber grows best in temperatures between 24°C and 32°C and requires well-drained fertile sandy or loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.8. The crop needs moderate rainfall, regular irrigation, and full sunlight exposure for healthy vine growth and proper fruit development. It is commonly used in salads, curries, and fresh consumption due to its high water content and cooling properties. Cucumber is a short-duration crop and can be harvested within 40 to 60 days after planting under suitable conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include fruit flies, aphids, cucumber beetles, powdery mildew, downy mildew, and viral infections, which can significantly reduce yield and fruit quality if not properly managed.

activities.title: Dig 45×45×45 cm pits and install trellis
activities.description: Fill pits with compost + topsoil mix. Erect 2 m trellis.
activities.why: Rich pits + trellis are the foundation of a good cucumber crop.

activities.title: Sow 2 seeds per pit at 2 cm depth
activities.description: Water gently after sowing.
activities.why: Two seeds ensures at least one germinates.

activities.title: Thin to 1 plant per pit
activities.description: Keep the stronger seedling.
activities.why: Competition reduces both plants' productivity.

activities.title: Guide vines onto trellis
activities.description: Direct main stem upward from day 10.
activities.why: Early training prevents tangled vines.

activities.title: Top dressing 1
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha ring + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Nitrogen drives rapid vine growth for early fruiting.

activities.title: Scout for whitefly and cucumber mosaic
activities.description: Check leaf undersides. Look for mosaic discolouration.
activities.why: Cucumber Mosaic Virus spread by aphids is the main disease risk.

activities.title: Top dressing 2
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Sustained nutrition for continuous fruiting.

activities.title: Set fruit fly traps
activities.description: Place protein bait traps from day 35.
activities.why: Fruit fly causes 40–60% loss if not controlled.

activities.title: First harvest
activities.description: Pick when 15–25 cm, firm and dark green.
activities.why: Harvest at correct size — over-ripe fruits turn yellow.

activities.title: Harvest every 2 days
activities.description: Pick all mature fruits without delay.
activities.why: Leaving mature fruits stops new production.

irrigation.notes: Cucumber is very sensitive to water stress — bitter fruits result from drought at fruit fill.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves; leads to yellowing and leaf death.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Warm days, cool nights, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Trellis use. Avoid overcrowding.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Myclobutanil (1 ml/L).

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.symptoms: Yellow angular patches on upper leaf surface; grey-purple growth below.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, overhead irrigation, wet weather.
diseases.prevention: Avoid overhead watering. Trellis for improved airflow.
diseases.treatment: Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) every 7 days in wet conditions.

diseases.name: Viral Mosaic
diseases.symptoms: Mosaic patterns, leaf distortion, stunted growth, and bitter fruits.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Aphid infestation; hot dry weather.
diseases.prevention: Control aphid vectors. Use certified virus-free seeds.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants. Manage aphids with Imidacloprid.

pests.name: Fruit Fly
pests.damage: Punctures fruits to lay eggs; internal maggot damage causing rotting.
pests.identification: Soft sunken spots; maggots inside young fruits.
pests.prevention: Protein bait traps. Bag fruits at young stage. Collect fallen fruits.
pests.treatment: Malathion bait spray. Fipronil bait stations.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; major vector of mosaic viruses.
pests.identification: Colonies on growing tips and undersides of young leaves.
pests.prevention: Yellow sticky traps. Reflective silver mulch. Neem oil spray.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid 200SL (0.3 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam.

pests.name: Cucumber Beetle
pests.damage: Adults damage leaves, flowers, and fruits; larvae damage roots.
pests.identification: Yellowish-green striped or spotted beetles on plants.
pests.prevention: Row covers at seedling stage. Hand-pick adults.
pests.treatment: Carbaryl (2 g/L) or Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L).

risks.name: Fruit Fly
risks.description: Primary threat. Can destroy 40–60% yield.
risks.mitigation: Weekly bait traps from day 30. Remove fallen fruits daily.

risks.name: High Temperature (>35°C)
risks.description: Bitter fruits, poor flowering.
risks.mitigation: Harvest before overheating. Shade nets in hottest months.

harvest.method: Cut with knife leaving 2 cm stalk.
harvest.post_harvest: Store cool. Market within 3–5 days.

Cucumber.growth_stages_text: Cucumber cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, rapid vegetative vine growth, flowering, fruit development, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 4–7 days after sowing, while vegetative growth is very rapid and continues for about 15–25 days depending on environmental conditions and nutrient availability. Flowering begins around 25–35 days after planting, followed by continuous fruit development and frequent harvesting. Farmers can start harvesting cucumbers within 40–60 days after planting when fruits are still tender, green, and have reached optimal size. Harvesting is done repeatedly over several weeks as the plant continues to produce fruits. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 60–90 cm between plants and rows to ensure proper vine expansion, sunlight exposure, and air circulation. Trellis systems are often used to improve fruit quality, reduce soil contact, and minimize disease occurrence.
Cucumber.management_text: Successful cucumber cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, vine training, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer application is crucial during early vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium fertilizers, and organic compost to promote vine development and fruit production. Irrigation frequency is high during germination and flowering stages due to rapid growth and high water demand, while moderate irrigation is maintained during later stages to avoid water stress. Harvesting indicators include firm, green, and tender fruits with smooth skin and optimal length before seeds fully mature.
Cucumber.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management play a major role in successful cucumber cultivation. Fruit flies damage developing fruits by laying eggs inside, causing internal decay and reducing market value. Aphids suck plant sap and cause curling, yellowing, and stunted growth, while cucumber beetles feed on leaves, flowers, and fruits, causing direct damage and spreading diseases. Powdery mildew appears as white powder-like fungal growth on leaves, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor, while downy mildew causes yellow patches and leaf collapse under humid conditions. Viral infections may lead to mosaic patterns, leaf distortion, and reduced yield. These problems can be controlled through proper field sanitation, crop rotation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plant parts, use of trellis systems, biological pest control methods, and recommended pesticide or fungicide applications when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, maintaining soil fertility, use of disease-free seeds, and climate-aware cultivation methods help reduce crop losses and improve overall productivity.

Kohila.overview: Kohila, scientifically known as Lasia spinosa, is a traditional vegetable crop widely cultivated and consumed in Sri Lanka. It is a semi-aquatic perennial plant belonging to the Araceae family and is commonly grown in lowland wet zones, marshy areas, and home gardens across the country. Kohila thrives in warm and humid climatic conditions with temperatures ranging between 22°C and 30°C. The crop grows well in waterlogged, muddy, or highly moist soils rich in organic matter and requires abundant water availability for healthy growth. Both the stems and leaves are used in Sri Lankan cuisine and are valued for their nutritional and medicinal properties, including high fiber content and digestive health benefits. Kohila can be cultivated throughout the year under suitable moisture conditions and generally requires partial to full sunlight exposure. Important factors affecting kohila cultivation include soil moisture, temperature, humidity, rainfall, and soil fertility. Farmers must also manage issues such as root rot, leaf diseases, and pest attacks caused by excessive stagnant water, fungal infections, or poor field management practices.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Prepare wet or moist planting area.

activities.title: Prepare moist or waterlogged planting bed
activities.description: Kohila thrives in waterlogged conditions. No need to drain.
activities.why: Kohila is adapted to wetlands — waterlogging is beneficial, not harmful.

activities.title: Apply organic matter
activities.description: Incorporate 10 t/ha compost or water hyacinth compost.
activities.why: Rich organic matter in moist soil is ideal for Kohila.

activities.title: Plant rhizome pieces with at least 2 nodes
activities.description: Plant 5–8 cm deep. Keep well watered.
activities.why: Each division produces a new plant.

activities.title: Maintain saturated soil
activities.description: Kohila does not need irrigation management — keep wet.
activities.why: Saturated soil is the natural habitat of this plant.

activities.title: Apply compost mulch
activities.description: Apply 3 kg compost per plant as surface mulch.
activities.why: Slow release organic nutrition.

activities.title: Remove competing weeds
activities.description: Pull out competing grasses and sedges.
activities.why: Competition from other wetland plants slows establishment.

activities.title: Check new shoot development
activities.description: Count new shoots — indicates healthy establishment.
activities.why: Multiple shoots = good root development.

activities.title: Annual organic topdress
activities.description: Apply 5 kg compost per plant annually.
activities.why: Perennial plants benefit from annual nutrition replenishment.

activities.title: Check stem quality
activities.description: Inspect stems — firm, white inner stem = best quality.
activities.why: Stem colour and firmness determine culinary quality.

activities.title: First stem harvest
activities.description: Cut young stems at base when 30–50 cm tall.
activities.why: Young stems are most tender and flavourful.

activities.title: Harvest every 2–3 weeks
activities.description: Cut stems regularly to encourage new growth.
activities.why: Regular harvesting keeps plant productive for years.

irrigation.frequency: Keep soil waterlogged or very moist at all times
irrigation.method: Flood irrigation, wetland, or near water source
over_watering_signs: Not applicable — Kohila thrives in standing water
irrigation.notes: Kohila is a wetland plant — the more water the better. Plant near natural water sources, drainage channels, or in paddyfield borders.

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Blackened, soft, decaying roots; plant wilts and yellows despite moist soil.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Excessive stagnant water, poor water quality, high temperatures.
diseases.prevention: Maintain water flow. Avoid completely stagnant conditions. Good drainage.
diseases.treatment: Remove infected plants. Improve drainage. Copper fungicide drench if severe.

diseases.name: Leaf Disease / Blight
diseases.symptoms: Yellowing, browning, or spotted leaves; reduced plant vigour.
diseases.favorable_conditions: High humidity, stagnant water, poor sanitation.
diseases.prevention: Remove infected leaves. Field sanitation. Avoid overcrowding.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP spray (2.5 g/L) on foliage if necessary.

pests.name: Stem Borer
pests.damage: Tunnels inside stems reducing plant vigour and causing wilting.
pests.identification: Entry holes in stems; frass visible near damage point.
pests.prevention: Remove and destroy affected stems. Regular monitoring.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos 20EC (2 ml/L) applied to stem area if severe.

pests.name: Leaf Hopper / Plant Hopper
pests.damage: Yellowing leaves, curled leaf edges, weak plant growth from sap-sucking under leaves and around moist, weedy areas.
pests.identification: Small jumping insects on undersides of leaves; yellowing starts at leaf margins and spreads inward.
pests.signs: Leaf yellowing, curled edges, honeydew deposits attracting ants.
pests.prevention: Remove weeds, avoid overcrowding, maintain field sanitation, avoid excess nitrogen fertilizer.
pests.mitigation: Apply neem extract or soap spray for early-stage attack. For severe infestation use a DOA-recommended systemic insecticide according to label instructions.

pests.name: Snails / Slugs
pests.damage: Irregular holes on young leaves and damaged shoots, especially during wet periods.
pests.identification: Irregular feeding holes on leaves; silvery slime trails visible near plants in the morning.
pests.signs: Slime trails, chewed leaf margins, damaged new shoots.
pests.prevention: Remove weeds, improve field drainage, reduce excessive shade, collect and destroy snails manually during early morning or evening.
pests.mitigation: Hand-pick during morning and evening inspections. Use DOA-approved snail bait only if infestation is severe.

risks.name: Drought
risks.description: Kohila cannot survive without abundant water.
risks.mitigation: Plant only in permanently wet areas or provide constant irrigation.

risks.name: Overharvesting
risks.description: Cutting too many stems weakens plant permanently.
risks.mitigation: Leave at least 50% of stems at each harvest.

indicators: Inner stem is firm and white
Leaf is still tightly folded (not fully open)
harvest.method: Cut stems at base with knife. Leave roots and some stems.
harvest.post_harvest: Keep cool and moist. Wilts quickly in heat. Market same day if possible.

Kohila.growth_stages_text: Kohila cultivation includes several important growth stages such as planting establishment, vegetative growth, stem development, and harvesting. Initial establishment occurs within 1–2 weeks after planting, while vegetative growth continues for several weeks depending on water availability and environmental conditions. Stem thickening and mature leaf development occur gradually, and harvesting can usually begin within 4–6 months after planting under suitable conditions. Kohila is commonly propagated using stem cuttings or rhizome divisions rather than seeds. Farmers usually maintain adequate spacing between plants to support healthy growth, airflow, and proper water circulation in wet cultivation areas.
Kohila.management_text: Successful kohila cultivation requires a week-by-week management schedule including land preparation, planting, water management, fertilizer application, weed removal, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Organic fertilizers and compost are commonly applied during early growth stages to improve soil fertility and support healthy stem development. Additional fertilizer applications may be provided during active vegetative growth stages depending on soil conditions. Irrigation management is highly important because the crop requires continuously moist or waterlogged soil conditions throughout its growth cycle. During dry periods, frequent watering is necessary to maintain adequate moisture levels, while proper drainage management is important to avoid excessive stagnant water that can increase disease risk.
Kohila.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential for maintaining healthy kohila production. Root rot commonly appears as blackened or decaying roots caused by excessive fungal growth in poorly managed wet soils, while leaf diseases may appear as yellowing, browning, or spotted leaves that reduce plant vigor. Pest attacks can also damage stems and leaves, reducing crop quality and growth performance. These problems can be minimized through proper field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, controlled water management, removal of infected plant parts, and appropriate fungicide or pest control measures when necessary. Farmers identify harvesting readiness when stems become thick, tender, and reach suitable market size. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper drainage control, maintaining water quality, use of healthy planting materials, and timely disease management help reduce crop damage and improve overall productivity.

Menari (Proso Millet).overview: Proso millet, commonly known in Sri Lanka as “Menari”, is a small-seeded cereal crop belonging to the Poaceae family and scientifically known as Panicum miliaceum. It is a traditional hardy grain cultivated in small-scale farming systems, especially in dry and intermediate zones of Sri Lanka, due to its strong tolerance to drought and poor soil conditions. Proso millet grows best in warm temperatures between 20°C and 32°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.5. The crop requires low rainfall, full sunlight exposure, and very limited irrigation, making it suitable for rainfed agriculture in arid regions. Menari is valued as a nutritious cereal rich in carbohydrates, fiber, and minerals, and it is used in traditional Sri Lankan food preparations as a rice substitute or porridge. The crop generally matures within 60 to 90 days after planting, making it a short-duration and climate-resilient crop. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include shoot flies, stem borers, aphids, leaf blight, and fungal infections, which can reduce grain yield and quality if not properly managed.

stages.name: Land Preparation
stages.description: Minimal preparation — grows in poor soils.

activities.title: Light plough and weed removal
activities.description: Plough once 15 cm. Clear old crop debris.
activities.why: Menari tolerates poor soil but clean seedbed improves yield.

activities.title: Rake to fine tilth
activities.description: Break surface clods. Very small seeds need fine tilth.
activities.why: Small seeds need good soil contact.

activities.title: Sow 8–10 kg/ha
activities.description: Broadcast and rake in 1–2 cm deep OR sow in rows 25 cm apart.
activities.why: Small seeds sown too deep fail to germinate.

activities.title: One irrigation if soil is dry
activities.description: Bring surface to field capacity.
activities.why: Tiny seeds need surface moisture to germinate.

activities.title: First and only weeding
activities.description: Weed once at 2 WAP. Canopy closes after this.
activities.why: One clean weeding is the most critical management step.

activities.title: Apply top dressing
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Modest nitrogen — menari is adapted to low fertility soils.

stages.name: Heading & Grain Fill
stages.description: Panicles emerge and fill with grain.

activities.title: Watch for blast disease
activities.description: Check leaves and panicles for grey lesions.
activities.why: Blast is the key disease risk.

activities.title: Bird protection
activities.description: Post scarecrows. Patrol morning and evening.
activities.why: Birds are a serious threat at grain fill in dry zone.

activities.title: Harvest when grain hard
activities.description: Cut panicles with sickle when grain fully formed.
activities.why: Unlike sorghum, menari has no shattering problem.

activities.title: Thresh and dry
activities.description: Beat heads on clean mats. Sun dry 3–5 days.
activities.why: Rapid drying prevents mold.

irrigation.notes: Menari is one of the most drought-tolerant crops in Sri Lanka. Requires less water than any other cereal. Overwatering promotes blast disease.

diseases.name: Leaf Blight
diseases.symptoms: Brown elongated lesions on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and grain quality.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, dense sowing, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Timely sowing. Resistant varieties.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Carbendazim (1 g/L) spray.

diseases.name: Rust
diseases.cause: Fungal spores spread by wind, favoured by humid weather and moderate temperatures.
diseases.symptoms: Orange-brown powdery spots on leaf surfaces; leaves dry and die early in severe cases.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid weather, moderate temperatures, dense planting.
diseases.prevention: Use healthy certified seeds, avoid dense planting, remove crop residues after harvest, practice crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: Remove and destroy heavily infected plants. Apply a recommended fungicide if the disease spreads widely; follow DOA label instructions.

diseases.name: Grain Smut / Head Smut
diseases.cause: Seed-borne or soil-borne fungal infection (Tilletia and Sphacelotheca species).
diseases.symptoms: Grain heads turn black or powdery; grain filling is absent or very poor in infected heads.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Infected seed, poor seed selection, continuous cropping on the same land.
diseases.prevention: Use clean certified seeds, treat seeds before planting with a recommended seed treatment fungicide, practice crop rotation.
diseases.treatment: No effective chemical cure after infection. Remove and destroy infected heads immediately. Improve seed selection and avoid saving seed from infected fields.

pests.name: Shoot Fly
pests.damage: Attacks young seedlings causing 'dead heart' — central shoot dies and dries.
pests.identification: Withered central shoot in young plants; maggot visible inside base of dead shoot.
pests.prevention: Early sowing to avoid pest peak. Use of resistant varieties.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules at sowing or Carbosulfan seed dressing.

pests.name: Stem Borer
pests.damage: Tunnels into stems interfering with nutrient transport; 'dead heart' or 'white ear' symptoms.
pests.identification: Entry holes in stems with frass; wilted or dead shoot/panicle.
pests.prevention: Field sanitation. Remove and destroy infested plants.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) spray at early infestation.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from panicles and leaves; reduces grain yield.
pests.identification: Colonies on panicles and leaves; sticky honeydew.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Natural predators.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) only if infestation is severe.

risks.name: Birds
risks.description: Most serious risk at grain fill in dry zone.
risks.mitigation: Post scarecrows before heading. Patrol morning and evening.

risks.name: Waterlogging
risks.description: Menari has zero waterlogging tolerance.
risks.signs: Yellow wilting plants in wet soil.
risks.mitigation: Never plant in flood-prone areas.

harvest.method: Cut panicles with sickle. Beat on mats to thresh. Winnow.
harvest.post_harvest: Sun dry to 12% moisture. Store in sealed containers. Menari stores well for 1–2 years.

Menari (Proso Millet).growth_stages_text: Proso millet cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, tillering, panicle initiation, flowering, grain filling, and harvesting. Germination usually occurs within 3–7 days after sowing, followed by seedling establishment and tillering, during which the plant produces multiple stems that contribute to grain production. Panicle initiation generally begins around 25–40 days after planting, followed by flowering and pollination. Grain filling occurs over the next few weeks as nutrients accumulate in the developing seeds. Harvesting is typically carried out within 60–90 days when the panicles turn golden-brown and the grains become hard and mature. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 20–30 cm between plants and 30–45 cm between rows to ensure proper plant growth, sunlight penetration, and airflow.
Menari (Proso Millet).management_text: Successful proso millet cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer requirements are relatively low compared to many cereal crops due to the crop’s ability to perform well in marginal soils. However, balanced application of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium can improve grain yield and crop vigor. Organic manure is often incorporated during land preparation to improve soil fertility and water retention. Irrigation is most important during germination, flowering, and grain filling stages, although the crop can tolerate periods of drought better than many other cereals.
Menari (Proso Millet).disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are important for maintaining grain quality and productivity. Shoot flies attack young seedlings, causing dead hearts and reduced plant populations, while stem borers damage stems and interfere with nutrient transport. Aphids suck plant sap and weaken plants, reducing growth and grain production. Leaf blight causes brown lesions on leaves that reduce photosynthesis, while fungal infections can affect leaves, stems, and grain quality under humid conditions. These problems can be managed through crop rotation, timely planting, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer application, use of healthy seeds, and integrated pest management practices. Risk mitigation strategies such as maintaining proper field drainage, monitoring pest populations regularly, conserving soil moisture, and using disease-resistant varieties help ensure stable grain production and improve resilience against adverse environmental conditions.

Mustard.overview: Mustard is a cool-season oilseed and leafy vegetable crop belonging to the Brassicaceae family and commonly represented by species such as Brassica juncea and Brassica nigra. In Sri Lanka, mustard is cultivated on a small scale in cooler and intermediate regions as well as home gardens for both its edible leaves and seeds. The crop grows best in temperatures between 15°C and 25°C and prefers fertile, well-drained loamy soil with a soil pH ranging from 6.0 to 7.5. Mustard requires moderate rainfall, regular soil moisture, and good sunlight exposure for healthy leaf growth and seed production. Mustard leaves are commonly consumed as a leafy vegetable, while mustard seeds are used as a spice and for oil extraction due to their strong flavor and nutritional value. The crop is generally ready for leaf harvesting within 30 to 45 days, while seed harvesting usually takes about 80 to 110 days depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting mustard cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, humidity, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include aphids, flea beetles, cutworms, powdery mildew, white rust, and fungal leaf spot diseases, which can reduce crop yield and quality if not properly managed.

duration.note: days after sowing for seed harvest; 30–45 days for leafy vegetable

activities.title: Plough and form fine seedbed
activities.description: Fine tilth for small seeds. Raise beds in wet areas.
activities.why: Small seeds need good soil contact.

activities.title: Apply compost
activities.description: Incorporate 8 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Rich base for fast-growing leafy crop.

stages.name: Sowing
stages.description: Broadcast or row sow small seeds.

activities.title: Sow 5–8 kg/ha in rows or broadcast
activities.description: Rows 30 cm, cover seeds 1 cm deep.
activities.why: Shallow sowing critical — seeds die if too deep.

activities.title: Gentle irrigation after sowing
activities.description: Use fine spray. Avoid washing seeds.
activities.why: Small seeds need surface moisture.

activities.title: First weeding
activities.description: Weed carefully between rows.
activities.why: Mustard is a poor early weed competitor.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 75 kg/ha between rows.
activities.why: Nitrogen for rapid leaf growth.

activities.title: Watch for aphids
activities.description: Check stems and leaves for aphid colonies.
activities.why: Aphids are the primary pest of mustard.

stages.name: Flowering & Pod Fill
stages.description: Yellow flowers then seed pods develop.

activities.title: Top dressing 2 (seed crop only)
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 50 kg/ha.
activities.why: Needed only if growing for seed harvest.

activities.title: Watch for white rust
activities.description: Look for white pustules on leaves.
activities.why: White rust spreads rapidly in cool wet conditions.

activities.title: Leaf harvest (optional)
activities.description: Cut entire plants at 5 cm above soil for leafy vegetable use.
activities.why: Young leaves are most tender — harvest before flowering.

activities.title: Seed harvest
activities.description: Cut plants when 75% of pods turn yellow-brown. Thresh.
activities.why: Delayed harvest causes pod shattering and seed loss.

fertilization.why: Seed fill — only for seed harvest crop.

irrigation.notes: Mustard is moderately drought tolerant. For leaf crop, water regularly. For seed crop, reduce watering as pods mature.

diseases.name: White Rust
diseases.symptoms: White blister-like pustules on leaf undersides; yellow patches on upper surface; deformation of flowers and pods.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Cool humid weather, dew, poor air circulation.
diseases.prevention: Good plant spacing. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) or Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) every 10 days.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.symptoms: White powdery fungal coating on leaves; reduces photosynthesis and seed production.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Warm days with cool nights, low humidity, dense canopy.
diseases.prevention: Good spacing. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) or Tebuconazole (1 ml/L).

diseases.name: Fungal Leaf Spot
diseases.symptoms: Brown circular spots with yellow halos on leaves; reduces leaf quality.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Humid conditions, wet foliage, cool weather.
diseases.prevention: Crop rotation. Good spacing. Remove infected leaves.
diseases.treatment: Mancozeb 80WP (2.5 g/L) every 10 days.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap from tender shoots and flowers; causes yellowing and curling; major pest of mustard.
pests.identification: Dense colonies on growing tips and undersides of young leaves.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray (5 ml/L). Yellow sticky traps. Avoid excessive nitrogen.
pests.treatment: Dimethoate 40EC (1.5 ml/L) or Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L).

pests.name: Flea Beetle
pests.damage: Creates small holes in leaves; severe in seedling stage reducing establishment.
pests.identification: Tiny jumping black beetles on seedlings; circular shothole damage.
pests.prevention: Row covers on seedlings. Keep area weed-free.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos 20EC (2 ml/L) or Carbaryl dust.

pests.name: Cutworm
pests.damage: Cuts seedling stems at ground level overnight.
pests.identification: Collapsed seedlings; fat greyish caterpillar in soil near base.
pests.prevention: Soil tillage before planting. Remove surface debris.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos granules in soil at planting.

risks.name: High Temperature (>28°C)
risks.description: Bolting (premature flowering) without good leaf development.
risks.signs: Plants flower without developing good leaf mass.
risks.mitigation: Plant in cool season only. Avoid low country in hot months.

risks.name: Delayed Seed Harvest
risks.description: Pod shattering causes seed loss.
risks.mitigation: Harvest when 75% pods turn yellow. Do not wait for all pods.

indicators.leaf: Plants 20–25 cm tall, leaves tender and full sized
indicators.seed: 75% of pods turned yellow-brown; pods rattle when shaken

harvest.method: Leaf: cut at 5 cm from soil. Seed: cut plants, bundle, sun-dry, thresh.
harvest.post_harvest: Leaves: market same day. Seeds: sun dry to 10% moisture, store in sealed bags.

Mustard.growth_stages_text: Mustard cultivation follows several important growth stages including germination, seedling establishment, vegetative growth, flowering, pod formation, and harvesting. Germination typically occurs within 3–7 days after sowing, followed by rapid seedling establishment and vegetative leaf growth for about 15–25 days when leaves are harvested in leafy vegetable production systems. For seed production, vegetative growth continues for about 25–40 days before flowering begins around 35–50 days after planting. After successful pollination, pod formation and seed filling occur, and harvesting for seeds is usually completed within 80–110 days when pods turn yellow-brown and seeds become hard and mature. Farmers commonly use direct seeding methods and maintain spacing of approximately 20–30 cm between plants and 30–45 cm between rows to ensure proper leaf development, air circulation, and efficient seed formation.
Mustard.management_text: Successful mustard cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, seed sowing, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer management is important during early vegetative growth and flowering stages, commonly using nitrogen for leafy growth in vegetable production and balanced nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium for seed production. Organic manure is often used to improve soil fertility and structure. Irrigation is most important during germination and early vegetative stages, while moderate moisture is maintained during flowering and pod development to support seed filling.
Mustard.disease_management_text: Disease and pest management are essential in mustard cultivation. Aphids are a major pest, feeding on plant sap and causing curling, yellowing, and reduced plant vigor, while flea beetles create small holes in leaves, reducing photosynthetic efficiency. Cutworms damage young seedlings by cutting stems at soil level. Powdery mildew appears as white fungal growth on leaves, reducing plant health, while white rust causes white pustules on leaf surfaces and affects overall yield. Fungal leaf spot diseases also reduce leaf quality and seed production. These issues can be managed through crop rotation, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, removal of infected plants, resistant varieties, and biological or chemical control methods when necessary. Risk mitigation practices such as regular field monitoring, proper irrigation management, and integrated pest management techniques help improve productivity and ensure stable crop yield.

Sweet Potato (Bathala).overview: Sweet potato, commonly known as “Bathala” in Sri Lanka, is a tropical root crop belonging to the Convolvulaceae family and scientifically known as Ipomoea batatas. It is widely cultivated across Sri Lanka, particularly in dry and intermediate zones as well as home gardens, because of its adaptability to warm climatic conditions and low maintenance requirements. Sweet potato grows best in temperatures between 20°C and 30°C and prefers well-drained sandy or loamy soils with a soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 6.5. The crop requires moderate rainfall, adequate sunlight, and moderate irrigation for proper vine growth and tuber development. Sweet potato is an important food crop in Sri Lanka and is commonly consumed boiled, steamed, fried, or used in traditional dishes due to its high carbohydrate content and nutritional value, including vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants. The crop is generally harvested within 90 to 150 days after planting depending on the variety and environmental conditions. Important factors affecting cultivation include temperature, soil moisture, rainfall, sunlight, and soil fertility. Common pests and diseases include sweet potato weevils, aphids, stem borers, root rot, and fungal leaf diseases, which can significantly reduce tuber yield and quality if not properly managed.
Sweet Potato (Bathala).propagation: Vine cuttings (30–40 cm from healthy vines)

activities.title: Form ridges 25–30 cm high
activities.description: Row spacing 90 cm.
activities.why: High ridges prevent waterlogging and allow tubers to develop freely.

activities.title: Apply compost to ridges
activities.description: Incorporate 8 t/ha compost.
activities.why: Organic matter improves drainage and nutrition in ridges.

activities.title: Plant cuttings at 45° angle on ridge
activities.description: 2–3 nodes underground. Water immediately.
activities.why: Angled planting develops roots and tubers from underground nodes.

activities.title: Water every 3 days for 2 weeks
activities.description: Consistent moisture for vine rooting.
activities.why: Critical establishment period.

activities.title: One thorough weeding at 3 WAP
activities.description: Weed before vines spread and cover ridges.
activities.why: One early weeding is usually sufficient.

activities.title: Top dressing
activities.description: Urea 50 kg/ha + MOP 100 kg/ha along ridges.
activities.why: Low N, high K — drives tuber development not vine growth.

activities.title: Turn stray vines back onto ridge
activities.description: Redirect vines rooting away from ridge.
activities.why: Off-ridge rooting reduces main plant tuber yield.

stages.name: Tuber Development
stages.description: Tubers form and bulk up under ridges.

activities.title: Check tuber size
activities.description: Scrape ridge to inspect tuber development.
activities.why: Early check reveals if conditions are right.

activities.title: Reduce irrigation frequency
activities.description: Water every 7–10 days.
activities.why: Excessive moisture late causes excess vine growth and fewer tubers.

activities.title: Early harvest option
activities.description: Harvest some plants at 90 days for young sweet potatoes.
activities.why: Early harvest gets premium price for smaller tubers.

activities.title: Main harvest
activities.description: Cut vines. Dig ridge carefully. Handle tubers gently.
activities.why: Main harvest at 120–150 days for full-sized tubers.

fertilization.why: Foundation nutrition. No nitrogen or potassium basal.
Low N, high K for tuber development.

irrigation.notes: Bathala tolerates moderate drought. Excess N or water causes vine-heavy, low-tuber yield. High potassium is the key input.

diseases.name: Sweet Potato Virus Disease
diseases.symptoms: Severe leaf distortion, yellowing, stunting. 50–80% yield loss.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Infected planting material, whitefly vectors.
diseases.prevention: Use certified virus-free cuttings. Control whitefly.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Plant clean material only.

diseases.name: Root Rot
diseases.symptoms: Soft rot of tubers in waterlogged conditions.
diseases.favorable_conditions: Waterlogged ridges.
diseases.prevention: High ridges for drainage.
diseases.treatment: Prevention only. Good drainage.

pests.name: Sweet Potato Weevil
pests.damage: Adults and larvae bore into tubers causing internal decay; most destructive sweet potato pest.
pests.identification: Orange-red and blue metallic ant-like beetles; tunnels inside tubers.
pests.prevention: Use certified vine cuttings. Hill up soil around plants. Harvest on time. Crop rotation.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos soil drench. Pheromone traps for adult monitoring.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.damage: Sucks sap; may transmit viral diseases causing leaf distortion.
pests.identification: Colonies on young leaves and stem tips.
pests.prevention: Neem oil spray. Encourage natural predators.
pests.treatment: Imidacloprid (0.3 ml/L) if population is high.

pests.name: Stem Borer
pests.damage: Bores into vines reducing vigour and overall plant health.
pests.identification: Entry holes in stems; frass near bore points; wilting shoot tips.
pests.prevention: Remove and destroy affected vines.
pests.treatment: Chlorpyrifos (2 ml/L) applied to vine stems.

risks.name: Sweet Potato Weevil
risks.description: Delayed harvest dramatically increases damage to tubers.
risks.mitigation: Harvest at maturity. Never leave tubers in ground beyond maturity.

risks.name: Excessive Nitrogen
risks.description: Causes lush vine growth with few or small tubers.
risks.signs: Dense vine canopy, poor tuber yield.
risks.mitigation: Keep nitrogen low. Do not exceed 50 kg/ha Urea.

harvest.method: Cut vines to 10 cm. Dig ridge carefully. Extract without cutting tubers.
harvest.post_harvest: Cure in shade 5–7 days. Store in cool dry ventilated area. Shelf life 1–2 months.

Sweet Potato (Bathala).growth_stages_text: Sweet potato cultivation follows several important growth stages including vine establishment, vegetative growth, tuber initiation, tuber bulking, and harvesting. The crop is typically propagated using vine cuttings rather than seeds, and these cuttings establish roots within 7–14 days after planting. Vegetative vine growth continues for about 30–60 days, during which the plant develops a strong canopy that supports photosynthesis and later tuber formation. Tuber initiation begins around 40–60 days after planting, followed by tuber bulking, where underground storage roots expand and accumulate starch until harvest. Depending on the variety, harvesting is carried out between 90–150 days when vines begin to yellow and tubers reach full size with firm skin.
Sweet Potato (Bathala).management_text: Successful sweet potato cultivation requires a structured week-by-week crop management plan including land preparation, vine planting, fertilizer application, irrigation, weed control, pest monitoring, and harvesting activities. Fertilizer management is particularly important for balancing vine and tuber growth, with emphasis on potassium to enhance tuber size and quality, while excessive nitrogen is avoided as it promotes excessive foliage growth. Organic manure is commonly used to improve soil fertility and structure. Irrigation is most critical during vine establishment and early tuber formation stages, while mature plants can tolerate relatively dry conditions once tubers are well developed.
Sweet Potato (Bathala).disease_management_text: Disease and pest management play a key role in sweet potato production. Sweet potato weevils are among the most destructive pests, damaging tubers by creating tunnels that lead to internal decay and reduced market value. Aphids feed on plant sap and may also transmit viral diseases that weaken plant growth. Stem borers damage vines, reducing plant vigor, while root rot occurs in poorly drained soils and leads to tuber decay. Fungal leaf diseases cause spotting and premature leaf drop, reducing photosynthesis and yield potential. These issues can be managed through crop rotation, use of healthy planting materials, proper soil drainage, field sanitation, balanced fertilizer use, and integrated pest management practices. Regular field monitoring and timely intervention help minimize losses and ensure stable production.
